AI.motivation: Current evaluation of LLM agents in quantitative finance is fragmented across isolated tasks, overlooking financial relevance. Financial workflows are inherently multi-stage and interdependent, but existing platforms focus on single tasks, potentially overstating agent competence and missing weaknesses in generalization, real-market interaction, and meaningful decision-making.
AI.method: Introduces OpenFinGym with: 1) unified gym environment covering multiple finance tasks, 2) automated task-construction pipeline that converts finance publications into executable tasks, 3) containerized runtime with verifier service for scalable rollouts and preventing train-test leakage, 4) paper trading engine with low-latency data streaming, 5) deferred-resolution support for long-horizon forecasts, and 6) integration for SFT and RL post-training.
AI.result: The paper presents a comprehensive platform that addresses the fragmentation problem in quantitative finance agent evaluation, providing a unified interface that better reflects real financial workflows and enables more robust agent development and testing.
AI.conclusion: OpenFinGym provides a more realistic and integrated environment for developing and evaluating quantitative finance agents, addressing current limitations in single-task evaluation and better capturing the multi-stage nature of real financial workflows.

title: Topology-Informed Neural Networks for Flood Detection in Optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar Imagery
summary: Floods frequently impact regions around the world. Rapid and accurate flood detection is crucial for emergency response and timely mitigation of human and economic loss. The expanding availability of satellite data and advances in artificial intelligence have enhanced monitoring of environmental hazards, but many flood events remain challenging to detect because cloud cover obscures optical satellite imagery. Rambour et al. introduced the SEN12-FLOOD dataset and extracted per-image features using a ResNet-50 convolutional neural network backbone, then fed these features into a gated recurrent unit network to show that temporal information can substantially improve accuracy compared to single-image baselines. More recently, Chamatidis et al. showed that a vision transformer can achieve strong performance with popular convolutional architectures. However, these models typically function as opaque black boxes, making it difficult to interpret their decision boundaries, learned features, and internal reasoning, especially in safety-critical domains like remote sensing. In contrast, topological data analysis (TDA) provides a mathematically grounded framework for capturing global structural features of data. TDA has emerged as a powerful tool for analyzing complex imagery, especially imagery with geometrically interpretable structures, of which floods are a prime candidate. In this work, we systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the open-source SEN12-FLOOD dataset. By extracting topological features from each image and incorporating them into neural networks, we demonstrate that topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing networks to yield more robust and interpretable flood detection systems.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes using topological data analysis (TDA) features to improve flood detection in satellite imagery, making the models more interpretable and robust compared to traditional black-box neural networks.
AI.motivation: Current AI-based flood detection models using convolutional neural networks and vision transformers are opaque black boxes, making their decision boundaries and internal reasoning difficult to interpret, which is problematic for safety-critical remote sensing applications. There's a need for more interpretable flood detection systems.
AI.method: The researchers systematically evaluate topological descriptors for flood detection using the SEN12-FLOOD dataset. They extract topological features from each image using topological data analysis (TDA) and incorporate these features into neural networks to enhance flood detection systems.
AI.result: The topological descriptors carry meaningful flood signals independently and complement existing neural networks, leading to more robust and interpretable flood detection systems compared to traditional approaches.
AI.conclusion: Topological data analysis provides a mathematically grounded framework for flood detection that improves both performance and interpretability, offering advantages over opaque black-box neural network models in safety-critical remote sensing applications.

title: A General Framework for Learning Algebraic Properties from Cayley Graphs using Graph Neural Networks
summary: A Graph Neural Network (GNN) framework for predicting the solvability of finite groups from their Cayley graph representations was introduced in [1]. In the present work, we generalize this approach and develop a property-independent framework for learning algebraic properties of finite groups directly from Cayley graphs. As representative case studies, we consider abelianity, nilpotency, and solvability. Using a common GNN architecture and training pipeline, we investigate the extent to which algebraic structure can be recovered from graph-based representations alone. Results on a collection of finite groups drawn from several families demonstrate that the framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties from their associated Cayley graphs. These findings suggest that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations and can be extracted through GNNs. More broadly, the proposed framework provides a proof of concept for applying graph representation learning to the study of algebraic properties of finite groups.

AI.tldr: Graph Neural Networks can learn multiple algebraic properties of finite groups (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) directly from their Cayley graphs.
AI.motivation: Generalize previous work on predicting group solvability to develop a property-independent framework for learning multiple algebraic properties from graph representations alone.
AI.method: Develop a common GNN architecture and training pipeline to learn algebraic properties from Cayley graphs, testing on finite groups from several families.
AI.result: The framework successfully learns and distinguishes multiple algebraic properties (abelianity, nilpotency, solvability) from Cayley graphs, demonstrating that substantial algebraic information is encoded in graph representations.
AI.conclusion: Graph representation learning can effectively extract algebraic information about finite groups from their Cayley graphs, providing a proof of concept for applying GNNs to algebraic property study.

title: Accelerating Returns and the Qualitative Engine for Science
summary: Ray Kurzweil described a thesis of accelerating returns, which is the most influential narratives in discussions of technological progress. Its central claim is that advances in multiple technological fields, especially compute, artificial intelligence, brain science, and biotechnology, interact in such a way that progress becomes self-amplifying and approximately exponential. This paper gives a simple mathematical interpretation of that claim and then argues that, even if such acceleration is real, it does not by itself resolve the central problem of scientific discovery. The reason is that accelerating returns apply most naturally to executional and infrastructural capability, whereas genuine discovery often depends on a different capacity: qualitative reasoning about when a current framework is structurally inadequate and what conceptual move is needed next. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results sharpen this distinction: humans solve the benchmark at ceiling, whereas frontier AI systems remain below 1%, indicating that the gap between current AI and human flexible reasoning is still very large. At the same time, Demis Hassabis has emphasized that humans must retain their sense of meaning and what they choose to focus their lives on, a reminder that the future of AI is not only a technical forecast but also a question of what forms of human understanding are worth preserving and transmitting. This paper positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) [3] as a response to that missing capacity. In this view, the Kurzweil theory helps explain why quantitative capability may accelerate, while QES addresses the central problem in scientific discovery that acceleration alone does not solve. Its value does not depend on when AGI arrives, but on the fact that the processes of scientific discovery themselves constitute a form of human wisdom worth preserving, organizing, and making accessible.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques Ray Kurzweil's accelerating returns thesis, arguing that even if technological progress accelerates exponentially, it doesn't solve the core problem of scientific discovery which requires qualitative reasoning and human wisdom that current AI systems lack.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of Kurzweil's accelerating returns thesis, which focuses on quantitative technological progress but fails to account for the qualitative reasoning and human wisdom needed for genuine scientific discovery. Recent ARC-AGI-3 results showing AI systems performing below 1% while humans solve at ceiling highlight this gap.
AI.method: The paper provides a simple mathematical interpretation of Kurzweil's accelerating returns claim, analyzes why this acceleration doesn't address qualitative reasoning needs, examines recent AI benchmark results (ARC-AGI-3), and positions the Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) as a response to this missing capacity.
AI.result: The paper finds that accelerating returns apply mainly to executional and infrastructural capabilities, not to the qualitative reasoning needed for scientific discovery. It identifies a critical gap between current AI systems' quantitative capabilities and human flexible reasoning, suggesting that scientific discovery processes constitute human wisdom worth preserving regardless of AGI timelines.
AI.conclusion: Scientific discovery requires qualitative reasoning and human wisdom that accelerating technological progress alone cannot provide. The Qualitative Engine for Science (QES) addresses this missing capacity, and the value of preserving and organizing scientific discovery processes doesn't depend on AGI arrival but represents a form of human understanding worth maintaining.

title: Fast LeWorldModel
summary: Joint-Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs), including recent LeWorldModel (LeWM), have become a promising foundation for reconstruction-free visual world models. For visual planning, however, LeWM evaluates candidate action sequences by repeatedly applying a local one-step latent transition model. This autoregressive rollout makes planning computationally expensive and exposes the predicted trajectory to accumulated latent errors as the horizon grows. We propose Fast LeWorldModel (Fast-LeWM), a fast latent world model that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. Given the current latent and a candidate action sequence, Fast-LeWM encodes its prefixes and predicts the future latents reached after executing those prefixes in parallel. By making action prefixes the basic prediction unit, Fast-LeWM directly models action effects accumulated to different extents over multiple horizons. This prefix-level supervision forces the model to learn how states continuously evolve under different action prefixes, rather than only fitting one-step state transitions. During planning, the predictor can use the last prefix token from the encoded action sequence to evaluate the corresponding future latent without explicitly rolling through each intermediate imagined state. Across multiple tasks, Fast-LeWM improves average success over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time, achieving lower open-loop latent loss whose growth becomes significantly slower as the rollout horizon increases.

AI.tldr: Fast-LeWM improves upon LeWM by replacing autoregressive rollouts with parallel action-prefix prediction for faster planning with reduced error accumulation.
AI.motivation: Current JEPAs like LeWM use computationally expensive autoregressive rollouts for visual planning, which leads to accumulated latent errors over longer horizons and makes planning slow.
AI.method: Proposes Fast-LeWM that replaces repeated local rollout with action-prefix prediction. The model encodes action prefixes and predicts future latents in parallel for different horizons, using prefix-level supervision to learn continuous state evolution.
AI.result: Fast-LeWM improves average success rates over LeWM while substantially reducing planning time. It achieves lower open-loop latent loss with significantly slower error growth as rollout horizon increases.
AI.conclusion: Action-prefix prediction is an effective approach for fast latent world modeling that addresses computational expense and error accumulation problems of autoregressive rollouts in visual planning.

title: Narration-of-Thought: Inference-Time Scaffolding for Defeasible Ethical Reasoning in Large Language Models
comment: 24 pages, 8 figures, 16 tables. To appear at ACL 2026 (submitted via ARR)
summary: Standard chain-of-thought on moral dilemmas exhibits two failure modes: stakeholder collapse (the trace names at most one party with a stake in the outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns or hedges before committing to an action). We introduce narration-of-thought (NoT), a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. NoT adds no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. On 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four generators from three vendors, NoT cuts stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1% and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% on every model. A matched-budget verbose-CoT control rules out token spend as the active ingredient; NoT retains Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators, and a section ablation attributes each shift to its specific sub-instruction. Textual-gradient descent initialised at NoT improves the scaffold further; a cross-family training judge (different vendor from the generator) dominates an in-family one on every measured axis. Extended to a five-round multi-stakeholder debate protocol, the scaffold converts a 6% standoff into 95% full consensus on a calibration set and 100% combined convergence on a DailyDilemmas replication. The resulting traces externalise the stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty grounding each commitment, providing an auditable substrate for dependable agentic deployment.

AI.tldr: Narration-of-Thought (NoT) is a system prompt that structures chain-of-thought reasoning into five sections to reduce stakeholder collapse and uncertainty suppression in moral dilemma reasoning, showing significant improvements across multiple LLM generators.
AI.motivation: Standard chain-of-thought reasoning on moral dilemmas suffers from two key failure modes: stakeholder collapse (only naming one party with stake in outcome) and uncertainty suppression (no explicit unknowns before committing to action). These limitations make reasoning traces incomplete and unreliable for auditable agentic systems.
AI.method: Introduced Narration-of-Thought (NoT), a system prompt structuring chain-of-thought into five sections: protagonist, stakeholders, two-step consequences, uncertainty, then commitment. Method involves no training, parameters, or fine-tuning. Evaluated on 100 DailyDilemmas scenarios across four LLM generators from three vendors, with controls for token spend and section ablation studies. Extended to multi-stakeholder debate protocol and conducted textual-gradient descent optimization.
AI.result: NoT dramatically reduced stakeholder collapse from up to 31% to under 1%, and uncertainty suppression from up to 72% to 1-24% across all models. Showed Cliff's delta advantages of +0.79 to +0.90 on stakeholder count and +0.65 to +0.93 on uncertainty score for three of four generators. Extended debate protocol achieved 95% full consensus vs 6% standoff, and 100% combined convergence on replication. Cross-family training judges outperformed in-family ones on all measured axes.
AI.conclusion: Narration-of-Thought provides an effective, training-free scaffolding for LLM reasoning that externalizes stakeholders, consequences, and uncertainty, creating auditable reasoning traces for dependable agentic deployment. The structured prompting approach addresses fundamental limitations in standard chain-of-thought for moral dilemmas and enables consensus-building in multi-stakeholder scenarios.

title: Dataset Usage Inference without Shadow Models or Held-out Data
comment: Accepted at the 2nd Workshop on Synthetic & Adversarial ForEnsics (SAFE), CVPR 2026 (non-archival)
summary: How much of my data was used to train a machine learning model? Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) aims to answer this by estimating what fraction of a dataset contributed to a model's training. However, existing DUI methods rely on assumptions that rarely hold in practice: they require training expensive shadow models to imitate the target model, and they assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches impractical for modern large models and real data ownership disputes. We introduce a practical DUI framework that removes these constraints. Our method requires neither shadow models nor real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and casts DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate what share of the candidate dataset was used during training. Experiments on large image generative models show that our method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.

AI.tldr: A practical Dataset Usage Inference (DUI) method that estimates what fraction of a dataset was used to train a model, without needing shadow models or real held-out data, by generating synthetic samples and solving mixture proportion estimation.
AI.motivation: Existing DUI methods are impractical because they require training expensive shadow models to imitate target models, and assume access to both known training samples and an in-distribution held-out set confirmed to be absent from training. These conditions make current approaches unsuitable for modern large models and real data ownership disputes.
AI.method: The method eliminates the need for shadow models and real held-out data. Instead, it generates synthetic non-member samples, extracts diverse membership signals, and formulates DUI as a mixture proportion estimation problem to estimate the share of candidate dataset used during training.
AI.result: Experiments on large image generative models demonstrate that the method reliably quantifies dataset usage, providing a practical tool for data owners to determine how much of their data was used to train a model.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a practical DUI framework that overcomes the limitations of existing methods, making dataset usage inference feasible for modern large models and real-world data ownership scenarios without requiring shadow models or real held-out data.

title: Geometry-Aware MCTS for Extremal Problems in Combinatorial Geometry
summary: We study certain extremal problems in combinatorial geometry that ask about configurations of points in an $n \times n$ grid that satisfy strict, global geometric constraints. Classical exact solvers suffer from combinatorial explosion for these types of problems, and standard reinforcement learning and transformer-based models struggle with the sparse reward "validity cliff" and quadratic token-consumption limits. To overcome these bottlenecks, we propose a Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) framework. Our approach strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to the feasible action space. For constraints about collections of collinear points, like those that occur in the classic No-Three-in-Line problem (Max-N3IL), this mechanism reduces the constraint checking complexity from $O(n^3)$ to $O(n^2)$. To improve search efficiency, we exploit geometric symmetries in two ways: canonical pruning during node expansion to reduce the branching factor, and symmetric batch transitions to accelerate the discovery of promising configurations. We perform extensive experiments and establish new best-known computational results on five out of six of the problems that we considered. Notably, for Max-N3IL we find configurations of size roughly $1.8 n$ for grids of size $82 \le n \le 119$. For the Smallest Complete Set problem, we find configurations of size roughly $0.95 n$, providing new upper bounds within the tested grids. This work establishes Geometry-Aware MCTS as a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry.

AI.tldr: Geometry-Aware MCTS framework solves combinatorial geometry problems with strict global constraints, achieving new computational results by efficiently handling geometric constraints and exploiting symmetries.
AI.motivation: Classical exact solvers face combinatorial explosion for grid-based geometric problems, while standard RL and transformer models struggle with sparse rewards and quadratic token limits, creating bottlenecks for discovering valid configurations.
AI.method: A Geometry-Aware Monte Carlo Tree Search framework that strictly enforces geometric constraints through incremental updates to feasible action space, reduces constraint checking complexity (O(n³) to O(n²) for collinearity), and exploits geometric symmetries via canonical pruning and symmetric batch transitions.
AI.result: Established new best-known computational results on 5 out of 6 problems tested; for Max-N3IL found configurations of size ~1.8n for grids 82≤n≤119; for Smallest Complete Set found configurations of size ~0.95n, providing new upper bounds within tested grids.
AI.conclusion: Geometry-Aware MCTS is a highly adaptable framework for discovering novel configurations in combinatorial geometry, overcoming limitations of existing methods through efficient constraint handling and symmetry exploitation.

title: Equivariance and Augmentation for Bayesian Neural Networks
summary: Symmetries are important for many deep learning tasks, ranging from applications in the sciences to medical imaging. However, there is an ongoing debate about whether to impose symmetry constraints on the neural network architecture (yielding equivariant neural networks) or learn them from augmented training data. Although equivariant networks are well-studied theoretically, much less is known about data augmentation, since analyzing augmentation requires control over the training dynamics. Inspired by recent results that show that augmented infinite deep ensembles are exactly equivariant, we study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference. We focus on variational distributions in the exponential family and derive conditions under which exact equivariance is reached. We furthermore obtain bounds on the equivariance error and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques which boost the effect of data augmentation in this setting. We conduct extensive numerical experiments which show that one of our symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms the baseline in both equivariance and overall performance. Our code is available at github.com/dmw1998/augment-BNNs

AI.tldr: Data augmentation can achieve exact symmetry equivariance in Bayesian neural networks with variational inference, and novel symmetrization methods like orbit expansion outperform baseline approaches.
AI.motivation: There's an ongoing debate in deep learning about whether to enforce symmetries through equivariant neural network architectures or learn them from data augmentation. While equivariant networks are well-studied, much less is known about data augmentation's effectiveness, especially for Bayesian neural networks trained with variational inference.
AI.method: The authors study data augmentation for Bayesian neural networks (BNNs) trained with variational inference, focusing on variational distributions in the exponential family. They derive conditions for achieving exact equivariance, obtain bounds on equivariance error, and introduce three novel symmetrization techniques to boost data augmentation effects.
AI.result: The paper shows that one of the novel symmetrization methods (orbit expansion) outperforms baseline approaches in both equivariance and overall performance. The authors provide extensive numerical experiments to support their findings.
AI.conclusion: Data augmentation can be effectively combined with variational inference in BNNs to achieve symmetry equivariance, with the proposed orbit expansion method showing superior performance compared to standard approaches, providing a practical alternative to architectural constraints for symmetry learning.

title: When Agents Meet Electric Bus Fleet Operations: Pricing Behavior, Trade-offs, and Policy Implications in an Aggregator Framework
summary: Agentic systems are changing how complex operational tasks are coordinated, introducing a new paradigm for connecting heterogeneous data sources and automating processes. Electric bus fleets provide a relevant test case. Their operation requires continuous coordination between service reliability, battery state-of-charge, charger availability, electricity prices, route-energy uncertainty, and vehicle-to-grid (V2G) opportunities. This paper proposes an agentic aggregator framework that streamlines this decision environment by coupling an optimization-based electric bus scheduling model with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation. The optimization core enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, while the agentic layer interprets changing operating conditions, triggers real-time re-optimization when needed, and defines how flexibility value is allocated between the aggregator and the public transport operator (PTO). A realistic depot case study evaluates day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes, considering service delays, route-energy deviations, electricity price shocks, and combined disturbances. The results show that agentic aggregation can support adaptive fleet-grid coordination by maintaining feasible schedules, activating re-optimization selectively, and improving the use of charging and V2G flexibility. However, they also reveal a critical trade-off: the same agentic capability that reduces operational complexity can extract value from the PTO when configured around profit-oriented pricing. These findings suggest that agentic aggregators can become useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but their deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules.

AI.tldr: An agentic aggregator framework combines optimization-based electric bus scheduling with supervisory agents to manage fleet-grid coordination, balancing operational feasibility with value allocation between aggregators and transport operators.
AI.motivation: Electric bus fleets require complex coordination between service reliability, battery management, charging, electricity prices, route uncertainties, and V2G opportunities. Current systems need better integration of these heterogeneous data sources and automated processes for adaptive fleet-grid coordination.
AI.method: Proposes an agentic aggregator framework with two layers: (1) an optimization core that enforces physical feasibility across routes, chargers, batteries, and V2G exchanges, and (2) an agentic layer with supervisory agents for disturbance detection, tariff adaptation, and schedule evaluation that triggers real-time re-optimization. Tested using a realistic depot case study evaluating day-ahead and real-time operations under profit-based and operation-based coordination modes with various disturbances.
AI.result: Agentic aggregation maintains feasible schedules, selectively activates re-optimization, and improves charging and V2G flexibility use. However, reveals a critical trade-off: agentic capabilities that reduce operational complexity can extract value from the public transport operator when configured around profit-oriented pricing.
AI.conclusion: Agentic aggregators can be useful for managing electric bus V2G operations, but deployment in public-fleet contexts requires transparent coordination modes, auditable tariff-setting, and explicit value-sharing rules to balance interests between aggregators and transport operators.

title: SSM Adapters via Hankel Reduced-order Modeling: Injection Site Determines Task Suitability in Long-Context Fine-Tuning
summary: While parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) typically targets attention projectors, its efficacy for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation remains under-explored. We examine if PEFT for such tasks can benefit from state space model (SSMs) adapters, and if MLP blocks are better injection sites. We introduce Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter, an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. By leveraging the time-invariance of the system matrix $\bar{A}$, HRM enables an exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths. In iso-parametric evaluations on Mistral-7B (8.4M trainable parameters), HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks, including QuALITY (+34.8\% relative accuracy) and QMSum (+71.6\% relative ROUGE-1). HRM further demonstrates consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8). Gate analysis reveals that HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling.

AI.tldr: HRM adapter uses state space models for PEFT, outperforming LoRA on long-context tasks with computational efficiency via FFT-based parallel scans.
AI.motivation: Current PEFT methods focus on attention projectors but are under-explored for tasks requiring sequential state accumulation. The paper examines whether SSM-based adapters and MLP blocks as injection sites could improve performance on such tasks.
AI.method: Introduces Hankel Reduced order Model (HRM) adapter - an SSM-based residual module initialized via Balanced Truncation of empirical Hankel Grammians. Leverages time-invariance of system matrix A for exact FFT-based parallel scan, achieving computational parity with LoRA across all context lengths.
AI.result: HRM outperforms LoRA variants on LongBench tasks: +34.8% relative accuracy on QuALITY and +71.6% relative ROUGE-1 on QMSum. Shows consistent superiority across 18 configurations of synthetic state-tracking (DFA, Parity) and character-level language modeling (enwik8).
AI.conclusion: HRM adapters effectively learn to modulate recurrence, providing a robust architectural alternative to low-rank adaptation for long-context sequence modeling, with computational efficiency matching LoRA.

title: Unbiased Canonical Set-Valued Oracles Via Lattice Theory
summary: A non-agentic "oracle" AI that estimates probabilities of future events faces a self-reference problem: once its answer is learned and acted upon, it can change the very probability it was asked to report. One response, advocated for the Scientist AI programme, is to ask only counterfactual questions, evaluated as if the answer had no influence. We observe that such answers tend to become irrelevant the moment they are learned, precisely because their premise is then false. We therefore explore a self-referential alternative in which the oracle reports not a single probability but a credal set that is simultaneously unbiased and self-consistent with the consequences of being learned. The naive self-consistency requirement is satisfied by too many sets (including the useless answer $[0,1]$), so the problem is to single out a canonical, nontrivial member. We do so with the Knaster--Tarski fixed-point theorem on the complete lattice of closed credal sets, taking the least fixed point of a suitably defined isotone operator; a variant instead reports the least fixed point that contains every self-consistent point estimate. We prove existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness, show that the construction collapses to the classical point answer for non-performative questions, and that for a binary event the canonical answer is, under a natural hull-factoring assumption, an interval. The development is purely lattice-theoretic and extends unchanged from a binary event $B$ to an arbitrary random variable $X$, with $P(B\mid A,C)$ replaced by the conditional law $\mathcal{L}(X\mid A,C)$. We close with open questions, including whether the interval characterization itself survives that generalization.

AI.tldr: A self-consistent oracle AI framework using credal sets and Knaster-Tarski fixed points to handle self-reference in probability estimation when answers influence outcomes.
AI.motivation: Traditional oracle AIs face self-reference problems: when their probability estimates are learned and acted upon, they change the very probabilities they're estimating. Counterfactual approaches become irrelevant once answers are known. Need a self-referential framework where oracle answers remain valid even when learned.
AI.method: Proposes reporting credal sets (sets of probabilities) instead of point estimates. Uses Knaster-Tarski fixed-point theorem on complete lattice of closed credal sets. Takes least fixed point of isotone operator for self-consistency. Also considers least fixed point containing every self-consistent point estimate. Extends from binary events to arbitrary random variables using conditional laws.
AI.result: Proves existence, self-consistency, and nonemptiness of canonical credal set solutions. Shows construction collapses to classical point answers for non-performative questions. For binary events under natural assumptions, canonical answer is an interval. Framework extends unchanged from binary events to arbitrary random variables.
AI.conclusion: Provides a lattice-theoretic framework for self-referential oracle AIs that maintains validity when answers are learned. Solves performative prediction problem through fixed-point methods, offering principled alternative to counterfactual approaches. Leaves open questions about interval characterization in general case.

title: Estimating Uncertainty in Classifier Performance with Applications to Large Language Models and Nested Data
summary: Researchers increasingly use text classification--supervised models or large language models--to measure constructs from natural language, providing metrics such as recall and precision as evidence of their validity. Yet, though these metrics are point estimates subject to sampling variation, measures of uncertainty are inconsistently reported alongside them. Further, when they are reported, they are often estimated with methods that are not appropriate when relevant labelled datasets are small or performance is high. To increase and improve confidence interval reporting in the field, this paper evaluates confidence interval methods for performance metrics under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. Across simulations, default methods such as the Wald interval and the basic percentile bootstrap are the least accurate, with coverage sometimes far below the nominal 95% level. Accuracy is improved with the use of Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap (which is particularly relevant to the calculation of F1). When texts are nested within individuals, we demonstrate that adjustment for both effective N and the appropriate degrees of freedom is necessary for producing accurate analytic intervals. Among bootstrap intervals, the hierarchical bootstrap is more accurate than the cluster bootstrap when individuals produce a moderate number of texts but overly conservative when individuals produce only a few. By providing guidance to the field on appropriate interval estimation, we aim to improve the transparency of machine learning applications, and to encourage greater attention to the validation sample size at the design stage.

AI.tldr: This paper evaluates confidence interval methods for text classification performance metrics under social science conditions, finding that default methods are inaccurate and recommending better alternatives.
AI.motivation: Researchers using text classification for measurement often report metrics like recall and precision as validity evidence, but they inconsistently report uncertainty measures. When uncertainty is reported, inappropriate methods are often used for small datasets or high performance cases common in social science.
AI.method: The paper evaluates confidence interval methods through simulations under conditions typical of social science text classification: small to moderate sample sizes, infrequent constructs, and texts nested within individuals. It compares various methods including Wald, bootstrap, Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and introduces a novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap for F1 scores.
AI.result: Default methods (Wald interval, basic percentile bootstrap) are least accurate, sometimes far below 95% nominal coverage. Better accuracy was found with Agresti-Coull, Wilson, Clopper-Pearson, and the novel pseudo-count regularized bootstrap. For nested data, adjustment for effective N and degrees of freedom is necessary. Hierarchical bootstrap works better than cluster bootstrap for moderate text counts per individual.
AI.conclusion: The paper provides guidance on appropriate confidence interval methods for text classification in social science research to improve transparency and encourage better validation sample size planning at the design stage.

title: High-Probability PL-SGD with Markovian Noise: Optimal Mixing and Tail Dependence
summary:
We study first-order methods for smooth objectives satisfying the Polyak-Łojasiewicz (PL) condition when gradient samples are generated by an exogenous Markov chain. In the light-tailed setting, prior uniform-in-time high-probability bounds for ordinary Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) under a standard growth envelope scale as $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}^2/k)$, leaving a gap with the $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/k)$ expectation bounds. We close this gap using a lag-blocking argument to establish a uniform high-probability guarantee with a leading stochastic term of $\widetilde{O}(t_{mix}/(k+K_0))$ under geometric mixing. We prove this linear dependence on the mixing time is optimal via a matching $Ω(σ^2 t_{mix}/k)$ lower bound on a quadratic objective driven by a persistent two-state chain.
  We then extend this framework to heavy-tailed Markovian gradients satisfying a stationary finite-$p$-moment condition, $p \in (1,2]$. We design an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias. Under a transition budget $T$, this algorithm achieves a high-probability stochastic error of $\widetilde{O}(σ_p^2(t_{mix}/T)^{2(p-1)/p})$. We establish a matching lower bound by reducing PL optimization to heavy-tailed mean estimation for a sticky Markov chain. Ultimately, this work tightly characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD, and the optimal heavy-tail exponent and effective-sample-size dependence in the robust regime.

AI.tldr: This paper establishes optimal convergence rates for stochastic gradient methods under Markovian noise, closing the gap between expectation and high-probability bounds for light-tailed gradients and extending to heavy-tailed distributions with matching lower bounds.
AI.motivation: There exists a gap between expectation bounds (Õ(t_mix/k)) and high-probability bounds (Õ(t_mix²/k)) for SGD under Markovian gradients with Polyak-Łojasiewicz objectives. Prior results had suboptimal dependence on mixing time in high-probability settings.
AI.method: 1) For light-tailed Markovian gradients: Used a lag-blocking argument to obtain uniform high-probability guarantees with optimal mixing time dependence. 2) For heavy-tailed gradients (p ∈ (1,2]): Designed an all-samples clipped block method that uses every Markov transition while mitigating Markovian bias through clipping techniques.
AI.result: 1) Achieved optimal Õ(t_mix/(k+K₀)) high-probability bound for light-tailed case with geometric mixing, proving linear mixing time dependence is optimal via matching Ω(σ²t_mix/k) lower bound. 2) For heavy-tailed case: Achieved Õ(σ_p²(t_mix/T)^{2(p-1)/p}) stochastic error and established matching lower bound through reduction to heavy-tailed mean estimation.
AI.conclusion: This work completely characterizes the optimal polynomial dependence on mixing time for light-tailed PL-SGD and establishes both optimal heavy-tail exponents and effective-sample-size dependencies in the robust regime, closing theoretical gaps in Markovian stochastic optimization.

title: Data-driven Machine Learning Cannot Reach Symbolic-level Logical Reasoning -- The Limit of the Scaling Law
summary: Sphere neural networks have achieved symbolic level syllogistic reasoning without training data, raising the question of where the limit of the scaling law for logical reasoning lies, i.e., whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve the same level by increasing training data and training time. We show two methodological limitations that prevent supervised deep learning from reaching the symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning: (1) training data can not distinguish all 24 types of valid syllogistic reasoning; (2) end-to-end mapping from premises to conclusion introduces contradictory training targets between neural components for pattern recognition and logical reasoning. Beside theoretical analysis, we experimentally illustrate that Euler Net cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. We further challenge the most recent ChatGPTs (GPT-5-nano and GPT-5) to determine the satisfiability of syllogistic statements in four surface forms (patterns): words, double words, simple symbols, and long random symbols, showing that surface forms affect the reasoning performance and that ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provide incorrect explanations. As empirical training processes are stopped after achieving 100% accuracy, we conclude that supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigour of symbolic logical reasoning.

AI.tldr: Supervised deep learning has fundamental limitations preventing it from achieving symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning despite data scaling, while Sphere neural networks can achieve this without training data.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether data-driven machine learning systems can achieve symbolic-level syllogistic reasoning through scaling laws (more data, more training time), given that Sphere neural networks have already achieved this without training data.
AI.method: The authors identify two methodological limitations: (1) training data cannot distinguish all 24 valid syllogistic reasoning types, and (2) end-to-end mapping creates contradictory training targets between pattern recognition and logical reasoning components. They conduct theoretical analysis, test Euler Net experimentally, and challenge ChatGPT (GPT-5 variants) with syllogistic statements in different surface forms.
AI.result: Supervised deep learning cannot achieve rigorous syllogistic reasoning. Experimental results show Euler Net fails, and ChatGPT GPT-5 may reach 100% accuracy but still provides incorrect explanations. Surface forms affect reasoning performance, but even perfect accuracy doesn't guarantee correct understanding.
AI.conclusion: Supervised machine learning systems will not attain the rigor of symbolic logical reasoning due to fundamental methodological limitations, even with empirical training stopping at 100% accuracy.

title: EVOM: Agentic Meta-Evolution of Actor-Critic Architectures for Reinforcement Learning
summary: In actor-critic reinforcement learning, network architectures are typically manually designed. Automating this design is challenging because each candidate must be trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended. To address these challenges, we introduce EVOM, an agentic meta-evolution framework for discovering high-performance actor-critic architectures. We frame architecture search as a bi-level optimization: an inner loop trains weights via the low-fidelity proximal policy optimization (PPO), while an outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs. Crucially, this outer loop is powered by an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control. Experiments reveal that EVOM outperforms the manually designed baseline, an LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, delivering superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4. Ablation studies validate that both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are indispensable for final performance.

AI.tldr: EVOM is an agentic meta-evolution framework that uses LLM-based design agents to automatically discover high-performance actor-critic architectures through bi-level optimization, outperforming manual designs and other automated methods on MuJoCo environments.
AI.motivation: Manually designing actor-critic network architectures in reinforcement learning is challenging and time-consuming. Automated architecture search faces difficulties because each candidate architecture must be fully trained before evaluation, and the design space is open-ended, making traditional automated approaches inefficient.
AI.method: EVOM frames architecture search as a bi-level optimization: 1) Inner loop trains network weights using proximal policy optimization (PPO) as a low-fidelity evaluation, and 2) Outer loop drives meta-evolution by iteratively refining architecture programs using an LLM-based design agent that operates purely as an architecture designer, completely decoupled from policy execution and environment control.
AI.result: EVOM outperforms manually designed baselines, LLM-guided random search, and the state-of-the-art LLM-guided programmatic policy search method MLES, demonstrating superior performance on Ant-v4 and HalfCheetah-v4 environments from the MuJoCo suite.
AI.conclusion: The EVOM framework successfully automates actor-critic architecture discovery through meta-evolution powered by LLM-based design agents. Both the meta-evolution loop and the LLM Design Agent are essential components for achieving the final performance improvements, as validated by ablation studies.

title: MKG-RAG-Bench: Benchmarking Retrieval in Multimodal Knowledge Graph-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over knowledge graphs has emerged as a promising approach for grounding large language models, yet existing benchmarks largely overlook the challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG (MKG-RAG). In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck: multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and often poorly served by retrievers designed for unstructured corpora. To address this gap, we introduce MKG-RAG-Bench, a cross-domain benchmark explicitly designed to evaluate retrieval in MKG-RAG. MKG-RAG-Bench is constructed from two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains, and includes carefully aligned question-answering datasets that support controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation. The benchmark is built using an LLM-based curation pipeline that filters low-utility knowledge, generates structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically covers diverse modality configurations. Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, we show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance, and that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes. By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems.

AI.tldr: A benchmark called MKG-RAG-Bench is introduced to evaluate retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems, addressing the overlooked challenge of multimodal retrieval bottlenecks in existing benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks for retrieval-augmented generation largely ignore the specific challenges of retrieval in multimodal knowledge graph RAG. In practice, retrieval is a critical bottleneck because multimodal knowledge is heterogeneous, difficult to align across modalities, and poorly served by existing retrievers designed for unstructured corpora.
AI.method: The authors create MKG-RAG-Bench using two multimodal knowledge graphs spanning general and medical domains. They use an LLM-based curation pipeline to filter low-utility knowledge, generate structurally grounded queries with exact supervision, and systematically cover diverse modality configurations. The benchmark supports controlled evaluation of both retrieval and downstream generation.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments across representative retriever families and modality settings, they show that effective multimodal retrieval remains challenging yet crucial for end-to-end MKG-RAG performance. They demonstrate that retrieval quality strongly determines generation outcomes in MKG-RAG systems.
AI.conclusion: By isolating retrieval as a first-class evaluation target, MKG-RAG-Bench provides a principled foundation for diagnosing current limitations and advancing multimodal knowledge graph RAG systems, addressing the critical bottleneck of multimodal retrieval that was previously overlooked in existing benchmarks.

title: Mesh-RL: Coupled subgrid reinforcement learning
summary: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation, as value information spreads only locally across the state space. We propose Mesh-RL, a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by the finite element method and domain decomposition theory, which partitions the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforces boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates. Such an approach enables localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation. Unlike hierarchical or model-based approaches, Mesh-RL accelerates long-range credit assignment without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms. We evaluate Mesh-RL on hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and mesh resolutions. Across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q, Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states. While Dyna-Q already benefits from internal planning, it still achieves additional gains under structured decomposition. Overall, Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism for accelerating temporal-difference learning. Our framework bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques from scientific computing with reinforcement learning to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments. We will release source code of the study.

AI.tldr: Mesh-RL is a spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by finite element methods that partitions environments into overlapping subgrids with boundary-consistent TD updates, accelerating long-range credit assignment in sparse-reward RL without modifying rewards or adding planning mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement learning in large or sparse-reward environments suffers from slow temporal-difference reward propagation because value information spreads only locally across the state space, making credit assignment inefficient.
AI.method: A spatial domain-decomposition framework inspired by finite element methods and domain decomposition theory, partitioning the environment into overlapping subgrids and enforcing boundary-consistent temporal-difference updates to enable localized learning while ensuring globally coherent value propagation.
AI.result: Mesh-RL consistently improves convergence speed, cumulative reward, and learning stability across Q-learning, SARSA, and Dyna-Q in hazard-dense grid-world environments with varying geometries and resolutions. Higher mesh resolutions sustain exploration, prevent premature convergence, and substantially accelerate value propagation to distant states.
AI.conclusion: Mesh-RL introduces a principled spatial domain-decomposition mechanism that bridges finite element method-inspired boundary-consistency techniques with RL to improve sample efficiency in sparse-reward environments, accelerating temporal-difference learning without modifying the reward function, Bellman operator, or introducing explicit planning mechanisms.

title: auto-psych: Automating the science of mind using agent-driven theory discovery and experimentation
summary: AI-based scientific automation is increasingly possible by using agents to generate hypotheses, design experiments, and analyze data. Data collection is a major bottleneck in this pipeline, however. Psychology, and computational cognitive science in particular, is well-positioned to benefit from AI experimentation because theories are often represented as code and crowdsourcing platforms enable programmatic human data collection at scale. Here, we apply automated discovery techniques to the project of generating theories in computational cognitive science, with an agent-based system collecting human data independently through crowdsourced survey experiments. As a testbed, we use a classic case study from cognitive psychology: judging which sequences of coin flips seem subjectively more random. Our system, auto-psych, uses nested agent-based discovery loops to generate explanatory theories of human behavior. The inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; the outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online, and analyzes the data. This system can quickly and reliably recover ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation, but the nested structure is critical to model performance. Further, in three independent sequences of human experiments, the system finds theories that fit the data better than theories generated from the scientific literature. This work thus demonstrates the feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery in computational cognitive science.

AI.tldr: AI system (auto-psych) automatically generates cognitive theories by designing experiments, collecting human data via crowdsourcing, and iteratively testing models, demonstrating successful automated theory discovery in computational cognitive science.
AI.motivation: AI-based scientific automation faces a bottleneck in data collection. Computational cognitive science is particularly suitable for AI experimentation because theories are often code-based and crowdsourcing enables scalable human data collection, creating an opportunity for automated discovery in cognitive psychology.
AI.method: Developed auto-psych system with nested agent-based discovery loops: inner loop conjectures, fits, and critiques probabilistic cognitive models; outer loop designs experiments to test these models, launches them online via crowdsourced survey experiments, and analyzes the data. Used subjective randomness judgment of coin flip sequences as testbed.
AI.result: System reliably recovered ground-truth theories from synthetic data via systematic experimentation (requiring nested structure for optimal performance). In three independent human experiment sequences, auto-psych found theories that fit data better than those from existing scientific literature, demonstrating feasibility of automated data collection and theory discovery.
AI.conclusion: Automated discovery techniques can successfully generate theories in computational cognitive science through agent-based systems that independently collect human data via crowdsourcing. The work establishes a path toward AI-driven scientific automation in psychology and cognitive science.

title: EMA-FS: Accelerating GBDT Training via Gain-Informed Feature Screening
summary:
Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT), exemplified by LightGBM, spend a dominant fraction of training time -- typically 65-70% -- constructing per-feature histograms. Existing approaches such as random feature subsampling (feature_fraction) discard features without regard for their predictive utility. We propose EMA-based Feature Screening (EMA-FS), an algorithm-level optimization that maintains an exponential moving average (EMA) of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations and, after a short warmup, restricts histogram construction to the top-K features ranked by historical gain. Unlike random subsampling, EMA-FS is informed: it retains high-gain features while screening out low-gain ones. Operating at the per-tree level, it preserves full compatibility with LightGBM's histogram subtraction trick, requiring no changes to core routines.
  We evaluate EMA-FS on datasets spanning financial fraud detection, advertising click-through prediction, industrial quality control, and synthetic benchmarks, with feature dimensionalities from 29 to 968. On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional data it achieves significant speedups: 2.61x on a 500-feature synthetic benchmark and 1.45x on the 432-feature IEEE-CIS Fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention it improves AUC by 0.11 points while delivering a 1.34x speedup. On extremely sparse data (Bosch, >90% missing) it yields no speedup, as LightGBM's sparse bin optimization already bypasses empty values.
  We further introduce Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS), which replaces deterministic top-K selection with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta, unifying deterministic EMA-FS (beta -> infinity) and random subsampling (beta = 0) in one framework. Both are implemented in ~120 lines of C++ across all six LightGBM tree learners and are fully backward-compatible.

AI.tldr: EMA-FS is an algorithm-level optimization for GBDT that uses historical split gains to intelligently select high-value features for histogram construction, achieving significant speedups on dense datasets while maintaining or improving model quality.
AI.motivation: Gradient Boosted Decision Trees (GBDT) spend 65-70% of training time on per-feature histogram construction. Existing random feature subsampling methods discard features indiscriminately without considering their predictive value, potentially harming model performance.
AI.method: EMA-FS maintains exponential moving averages of per-feature split gains across boosting iterations. After a warmup period, it restricts histogram construction to top-K features ranked by historical gain. Stochastic EMA-FS (S-EMA-FS) extends this with gain-weighted random sampling controlled by a concentration parameter beta.
AI.result: On dense, moderate-to-high-dimensional datasets: 2.61x speedup on 500-feature synthetic benchmark, 1.45x speedup on 432-feature fraud dataset at 30% retention. At 70% retention, improves AUC by 0.11 points with 1.34x speedup. No speedup on extremely sparse data (90% missing) where existing optimizations already work.
AI.conclusion: EMA-FS provides an informed feature screening approach that outperforms random subsampling by retaining high-gain features while screening low-gain ones, achieving significant training speedups on appropriate datasets while maintaining or improving model quality.

title: Clinical Harness for Governable Medical AI Skill Ecosystems
summary: Medical AI remains organized around isolated models, whereas clinical care requires accountable capabilities that persist across time. We propose clinical AI skills and the Clinical Harness: a runtime governance architecture for registering, orchestrating, guarding and monitoring AI-enabled clinical capabilities. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, we show how knowledge-driven, data-driven and physics-enhanced skills can support lifecycle care under runtime governance.

AI.tldr: Medical AI currently uses isolated models, but clinical care needs integrated, accountable systems. The paper introduces "clinical AI skills" and "Clinical Harness" - a governance architecture to register, orchestrate, guard, and monitor AI capabilities over time, demonstrated with osteoporosis care.
AI.motivation: Current medical AI systems are organized as isolated, disconnected models, while real clinical care requires integrated capabilities that can be accountable and persist across patient care timelines. There's a gap between how AI is currently deployed (as discrete models) and the continuous, accountable nature of clinical workflows.
AI.method: The paper proposes two key concepts: 1) "clinical AI skills" - reusable AI-enabled clinical capabilities, and 2) "Clinical Harness" - a runtime governance architecture. This architecture provides four core functions: registering skills, orchestrating them, guarding their operation, and monitoring their performance. The approach uses osteoporosis as a case study to demonstrate how different types of skills (knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced) can be integrated under this governance framework.
AI.result: The paper demonstrates that the Clinical Harness architecture can support lifecycle care by enabling different types of AI skills to work together under runtime governance. Using osteoporosis as an exemplar, they show how knowledge-driven, data-driven, and physics-enhanced skills can be effectively coordinated to provide comprehensive patient care while maintaining accountability and oversight throughout the care timeline.
AI.conclusion: The Clinical Harness architecture addresses the fragmentation problem in medical AI by providing a governance framework that enables coordinated, accountable, and persistent AI-enabled clinical capabilities. This represents a shift from isolated AI models to integrated clinical systems that can better support the continuous nature of healthcare delivery.

title: Does Aurora Encode Atmospheric Structure? Latent Regime Analysis and Attribution
comment: Accepted at the FM4Science and Sci4DL workshops at ICLR 2026
summary: ML foundation models are able to emulate atmospheric dynamics accurately and efficiently but operate as opaque ``black boxes''. We investigate the internal representations of the Aurora model using spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP). We find evidence that Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, whereas extreme storm events do not form a linearly separable cluster. LRP indicates that the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987. Perturbation tests show masking relevant regions degrades forecasts $3.31\times$ more than random masking. These findings suggest that Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure without explicit instruction.

AI.tldr: Aurora weather foundation model learns meteorological coherence and vertical storm structure implicitly, organizes its latent space around seasonal cycles, and uses physically meaningful features for extreme weather prediction without explicit training.
AI.motivation: ML foundation models like Aurora can accurately emulate atmospheric dynamics but operate as opaque "black boxes," making it difficult to understand their internal reasoning and what meteorological concepts they learn without explicit instruction.
AI.method: Used spatially pooled PCA and layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to investigate Aurora's internal representations, conducted perturbation tests by masking relevant regions vs random masking, and analyzed attention patterns for extreme storm events.
AI.result: 1) Aurora's latent space is primarily organized by seasonal cycles, 2) Extreme storm events don't form linearly separable clusters, 3) LRP shows the model attends to features consistent with the 3D vertical structure of the Great Storm of 1987, 4) Masking relevant regions degrades forecasts 3.31× more than random masking.
AI.conclusion: Aurora learns meteorological coherence and vertical structure implicitly without explicit instruction, demonstrating that foundation models can develop physically meaningful representations of atmospheric phenomena through data-driven learning alone.

title: Humans Disengage, Reasoning Models Persist: Separating Difficulty Registration from Deliberation Allocation
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) take longer on harder problems, just as humans do. This surface similarity hides an opposite pattern within items. When an LRM gets a problem wrong, it spends more tokens than when it gets the same problem right; humans do the reverse, spending less time on the trials they get wrong. We separate two levels of deliberation: how response time tracks difficulty across items (registration), and, with item identity held fixed, whether an agent spends more on its own failures or successes (allocation). On a public matched human-LRM corpus, humans and all five thinking LRMs reproduce the known cross-item alignment (registration) but diverge within items (allocation): every LRM shows a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans show the opposite sign. The comparison stays inside each agent's own scale; we never put seconds and tokens on one axis. The dissociation holds under item fixed effects, replicates across datasets, and is absent in a non-thinking baseline. We read the human pattern as engagement versus abandonment: people stay on items they expect to solve and give up on the rest. We read the LRM pattern as length driven by uncertainty: chains grow when the model is unsure, which is exactly when it tends to fail. Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, so they look aligned on the measure prior work has used; the divergence shows up only once item identity is fixed. Under resource-rational metareasoning, the split is between two stopping policies that share a difficulty signal but implement opposite control; trace length captures the signal and misses the control.

AI.tldr: Humans and Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) both take longer on harder problems, but show opposite patterns within individual items: humans spend less time on trials they get wrong, while LRMs spend more tokens on their own failures.
AI.motivation: Prior work has shown surface similarity between humans and LRMs in response time patterns across items, but this paper investigates whether this similarity hides deeper differences in internal deliberation patterns when examining performance within individual items.
AI.method: The researchers analyzed a public matched human-LRM corpus, separating two levels of deliberation: registration (how response time tracks difficulty across items) and allocation (whether agents spend more resources on their own failures vs successes). They tested five thinking LRMs and compared them to human performance, controlling for item fixed effects and using appropriate scaling (never mixing seconds and tokens).
AI.result: Humans and all five thinking LRMs showed the expected cross-item alignment (registration), but diverged within items (allocation): every LRM showed a large wrong-vs-right effect (Cohen's d = 1.47-3.13 on H-ARC) while humans showed the opposite sign. This dissociation held under item fixed effects, replicated across datasets, and was absent in non-thinking baselines.
AI.conclusion: The authors interpret the human pattern as "engagement versus abandonment" (people persist on items they expect to solve) and the LRM pattern as "length driven by uncertainty" (chains grow when models are unsure, which correlates with failure). Both policies produce the same cross-item correlation with difficulty, explaining why prior work found alignment; the divergence only appears when item identity is fixed. This suggests trace length captures difficulty signals but misses underlying control mechanisms.

title: NeuraDock Visual Cognitive Load Agent Tutorial: A Quality-Gated Open-Source EEG Workflow for Alpha Dynamics and Real-Time Applications
summary: This tutorial paper provides a step-by-step, reproducible walkthrough of NeuraDock Agent, an open-source EEG agent focused on Alpha dynamics and visual cognitive-load analysis. The goal is practical: a reader should be able to install the agent, run EEG preprocessing and quality control, generate Alpha dynamics figures, perform within-subject Rest/Task visual cognitive-load comparison, run the public mini-dataset analyses and compare them with the reference validation summary, start an online dashboard, call the real-time API from an external application, and use the LLM interpretation layer to explain quality risks. Existing EEG toolkits provide excellent offline analysis, but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline often requires manually bridging acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and a web API; this tutorial closes that offline-to-online gap. The tutorial uses a quality-gated workflow: downstream Alpha and workload metrics are computed only after preprocessing and QC gating rather than directly from raw EEG. In the included mini-dataset validation, the agent processed 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, estimated initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. The tutorial is intended for researchers, developers, and applied teams who want a transparent path from EEG files to real-time visual cognitive-load prototypes.

AI.tldr: A practical tutorial for NeuraDock Agent - an open-source EEG system that provides a reproducible workflow from EEG data to real-time visual cognitive-load analysis with quality-gated processing and LLM interpretation.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG toolkits provide good offline analysis but assembling a real-time, quality-gated cognitive-load pipeline requires manual integration of acquisition, custom QC, Alpha feature extraction, and web API. There's a gap between offline analysis and real-time applications that needs to be addressed.
AI.method: Step-by-step tutorial using NeuraDock Agent with a quality-gated workflow: EEG preprocessing and quality control first, then computation of Alpha dynamics and workload metrics. Includes mini-dataset validation, within-subject Rest/Task comparisons, online dashboard setup, real-time API calls, and LLM interpretation layer for quality risk explanation.
AI.result: Validated on 18 recordings, generated 10 within-subject comparisons, observed task-related posterior Alpha suppression in 7 of 10 contrasts, found initial evidence of within-subject repeatability, and benchmarked local online API latency. Provides transparent path from EEG files to real-time prototypes.
AI.conclusion: The tutorial successfully bridges the offline-to-online gap in EEG analysis by providing a practical, reproducible workflow for real-time visual cognitive-load monitoring with quality gating and interpretability features, making it accessible to researchers, developers, and applied teams.

title: At the Edge of Understanding: Sparse Autoencoders Trace The Limits of Transformer Generalization
summary: Pre-trained transformers have demonstrated remarkable generalization abilities, at times extending beyond the scope of their training data. Yet, real-world deployments often face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training data distributions. Without explicit mechanisms for handling such shifts, model reliability and safety degrade, urging more disciplined study of out-of-distribution (OOD) settings for transformers. By systematic experiments, we present a mechanistic framework for delineating the precise contours of transformer model robustness. We find that OOD inputs, including subtle typos and jailbreak prompts, drive language models to operate on an increased number of fallacious concepts in their internals. We leverage this device to quantify and understand the degree of distributional shift in prompts, enabling a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs. Expanding the very notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes, a new transformer diagnostic at inference time is a critical step toward making AI systems safe for deployment across science, business, and government.

AI.tldr: Transformers show poor robustness to out-of-distribution (OOD) inputs like typos and jailbreak prompts, which increase fallacious reasoning. The paper introduces a mechanistic framework to quantify OOD shifts and a fine-tuning strategy to improve LLM robustness.
AI.motivation: Real-world deployments face unexpected or adversarial data that diverges from training distributions, degrading model reliability and safety. Current transformers lack explicit mechanisms for handling distributional shifts, requiring systematic study of OOD robustness.
AI.method: Systematic experiments reveal that OOD inputs drive language models to operate on increased fallacious concepts internally. This observation is leveraged to quantify distributional shift and develop a mechanistically grounded fine-tuning strategy to robustify LLMs.
AI.result: The framework enables quantification of distributional shift in prompts and provides a diagnostic tool at inference time. It expands the notion of OOD from input data to a model's private computational processes.
AI.conclusion: A mechanistic framework for transformer robustness is crucial for making AI systems safe for deployment across various domains. Understanding and mitigating OOD vulnerabilities through internal computational diagnostics is a critical step toward reliable AI systems.

title: Boundary-Aware Context Grounding for A Low-Channel EEG Agent
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) can make scientific software easier to use. However, a general model does not automatically know which measurements a particular sensor can support, which algorithms are implemented in the current software, or which conclusions are justified by a computed result. These distinctions are especially important for low-channel electroencephalography (EEG), where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce. We present NeuraDock Agent, an open-source architecture that separates a deterministic local EEG engine from a hardware-aware language layer. The numerical engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, and writes machine-readable artifacts. The LLM receives only a compact, allowlisted summary and a versioned context pack. The context describes the seven-channel hardware, reviewed workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases. Raw EEG and dense per-sample arrays remain local
  We evaluate the system at three levels. First, 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten numerical repetitions, and a complete Rest/Task run produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Second, request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed the tested data boundary and preservation of local artifacts under HTTP, malformed-output, and connection failures. Third, a boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary and adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs, yielding 288 outputs.These results support hardware- and implementation-aware grounding as a practical mechanism for calibrating what an EEG agent accepts, qualifies, or refuses; they do not establish clinical validity or a validated absolute cognitive-load index.

AI.tldr: NeuraDock Agent is an open-source architecture that separates deterministic local EEG processing from a hardware-aware LLM layer, providing calibrated responses for EEG analysis while keeping raw data local.
AI.motivation: General LLMs don't automatically know which measurements specific sensors support, which algorithms are implemented, or which conclusions are justified by computed results - especially important for low-channel EEG where sparse spatial coverage and variable signal quality make plausible but unsupported interpretations easy to produce.
AI.method: Open-source architecture separating deterministic local EEG engine from hardware-aware language layer. Local engine parses recordings, performs quality control, executes reviewed spectral workflows, writes machine-readable artifacts. LLM receives only compact allowlisted summary and versioned context pack describing hardware, workflows, result fields, implementation boundaries, scientific limits, and reference cases.
AI.result: 12 recordings produced identical structured results over ten repetitions; complete Rest/Task runs produced identical result, report, and figure hashes over three repetitions. Request-capture and failure-injection experiments confirmed data boundary and artifact preservation under various failures. Boundary-awareness benchmark tested 36 ordinary/adversarial questions under four context ablations and two LLMs (288 outputs total).
AI.conclusion: Hardware- and implementation-aware grounding provides practical mechanism for calibrating what EEG agents accept, qualify, or refuse, but doesn't establish clinical validity or validated absolute cognitive-load index.

title: Deterministic Pareto-Optimal Policy Synthesis for Multi-Objective Reinforcement Learning
summary: Real-world decision-making often requires balancing multiple conflicting objectives, a challenge that standard Reinforcement Learning (RL) frequently addresses by aggregating rewards into a single scalar signal. While effective for simple tasks, this approach often fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs, known as the Pareto frontier. In this paper, we introduce a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator, motivated from the Chebyshev scalarization, designed to compute deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs).
