We prove that this operator satisfies an enveloping property, where the estimated value functions upper-bound the true Pareto frontier, and demonstrate that it monotonically converges to a coverage set of this frontier. Furthermore, we also show how to extract deterministic policies from these converged Q-estimates. This ensures the agent can recover a policy for any given preference, capturing the entire Pareto-optimal frontier while guaranteeing each synthesized policy remains approximately Pareto-optimal. Experimental results validate that our algorithm successfully recovers complex trade-offs, providing a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis.

AI.tldr: A novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator based on Chebyshev scalarization computes deterministic Pareto-optimal policies for multi-objective RL, recovering the full Pareto frontier with guaranteed optimality.
AI.motivation: Standard RL aggregates multiple objectives into a single scalar reward, which fails to capture the full spectrum of optimal trade-offs (Pareto frontier) in multi-objective decision-making scenarios.
AI.method: Introduces a novel preference-conditioned Bellman operator motivated by Chebyshev scalarization for Multi-Objective Markov Decision Processes (MOMDPs), with theoretical proofs of convergence and policy extraction methods.
AI.result: Proves the operator satisfies an enveloping property (estimated value functions upper-bound true Pareto frontier) and monotonically converges to a coverage set of the frontier, enabling extraction of deterministic policies for any preference.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully recovers complex trade-offs and provides a solution for deterministic Pareto-optimal policy synthesis, ensuring policies remain approximately Pareto-optimal for any given preference.

title: Radical AI Interpretability
comment: Draft of manuscript to appear as Cambridge Element in the Philosophy of Artificial Intelligence
summary: We develop a framework for interpreting AI systems as agents, drawing on the philosophical tradition of radical interpretation and the tools of mechanistic interpretability. The core question is: given the computational facts about a system, how do we solve for its beliefs, desires, and meanings? This matters increasingly for safety. We want to be able to trust the systems we deploy, whether by understanding their goals or, more modestly, by reliably detecting deception. Interpretability researchers are building tools to read beliefs and desires off a model's internals, but there is no settled account of when such a tool has succeeded. This book supplies one. We propose criteria on both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, and tie each to tests current interpretability methods can carry out. A central lesson is that these attributions cannot be made piecemeal. Beliefs, desires, and the propositional structure they presuppose are jointly constrained, and a method that fixes one while measuring the others inherits whatever distortions that introduces. This holism becomes pressing for AI systems, which may not share the interpreter's concepts. However, it also provides leverage: a system's attitudes constrain its propositional structure, that structure constrains which attitudes can be attributed, and mechanistic interpretability can help us measure both.

AI.tldr: A framework for interpreting AI systems as agents using philosophical radical interpretation and mechanistic interpretability to determine when interpretability tools successfully read beliefs and desires from model internals.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more capable and potentially deceptive, we need reliable ways to interpret them as agents to ensure safety. Current interpretability research lacks clear criteria for when tools successfully read beliefs and desires from model internals.
AI.method: Combines philosophical radical interpretation tradition with mechanistic interpretability tools. Proposes criteria for both representationalist and interpretationist approaches, with tests current interpretability methods can implement. Emphasizes holistic approach where beliefs, desires, and propositional structure are jointly constrained.
AI.result: Develops a framework showing that attitude attributions cannot be made piecemeal - beliefs, desires, and propositional structure must be analyzed together. The holistic nature provides analytical leverage where attitudes constrain propositional structure and vice versa, with mechanistic interpretability helping measure both.
AI.conclusion: Provides a principled account for when interpretability tools succeed in reading beliefs and desires from AI systems, addressing safety concerns through holistic analysis that accounts for the interrelationships between attitudes and propositional structure.

title: Beyond Feedforward Networks: Reentry Neural Systems as the Fundamental Basis of Subjecthood and Intrinsic Safety of Next-Generation AGI
summary: We propose a complete architectural blueprint for safe artificial general intelligence based on a closed reentry loop (D <-> I cycle). In contrast to feedforward networks, which are directed acyclic graphs (C=0, S=0) incapable of self-reference, the proposed architecture contains a structural cycle (C >= 1) with self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1), mathematically guaranteeing the emergence of a self-model, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behaviour. The agent's goals are encoded as a non-textual D-vector in the architecture itself, making them immune to reinterpretation and prompt injection. We present the S-measure -- a polynomial-time [O(N^3)] computable alternative to Tononi's NP-hard Phi -- with machine-verified Lean 4 proof that S>0 implies positive integrated information. The work provides full Python/NumPy implementations (Tarjan-based cycle complexity, Delta-S barrier), industrial horizontal scaling via Apache Kafka and Docker Compose, a taxonomy of six epochs of AI evolution, a zoo of future reentry architectures (RAS, diffusion attractors, fractal loops), gauge-invariant networks for safe swarms, fault-tolerance and recovery protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions. All formal proofs are machine-verified in Lean 4. This architecture is deployable today and represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI.

AI.tldr: A complete architectural blueprint for safe AGI using closed reentry loops that guarantees emergence of self-models and goal-directed behavior, with machine-verified formal proofs and practical implementations.
AI.motivation: To create a safe-by-design AGI architecture that addresses limitations of feedforward networks (which lack self-reference) and provides mathematical guarantees against misinterpretation, prompt injection, and unsafe behaviors.
AI.method: Uses closed reentry loops (D <-> I cycle) with structural cycles (C >= 1) and self-sustaining amplification (rho > 1). Introduces S-measure as polynomial-time computable alternative to Tononi's Phi, with D-vector goal encoding. Includes practical implementations using Python/NumPy, Apache Kafka, Docker Compose, and formal verification in Lean 4.
AI.result: The architecture mathematically guarantees emergence of self-models, instrumental self-preservation, and unprogrammed goal-directed behavior. Provides full implementations, industrial scaling capabilities, taxonomy of AI evolution, various architectural variants, fault-tolerance protocols, and eight falsifiable predictions.
AI.conclusion: This represents a topologically protected, safe-by-design approach to AGI that is deployable today, with machine-verified formal guarantees and practical industrial scalability.

title: PMDformer: Patch-Mean Decoupling Information Transformer for Long-term Forecasting
summary: Long-term time series forecasting (LTSF) plays a crucial role in fields such as energy management, finance, and traffic prediction. Transformer-based models have adopted patch-based strategies to capture long-range dependencies, but accurately modeling shape similarities across patches and variables remains challenging due to scale differences. To address this, we introduce patch-mean decoupling (PMD), which separates the trend and residual shape information by subtracting the mean of each patch, preserving the original structure and ensuring that the attention mechanism captures true shape similarities. Futhermore, to more effectively model long-range dependencies and capture cross-variable relationships, we propose Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) and Proximal Variable Attention (PVA). The former module reintegrates the decoupled trend from PMD while calculating attention output. And the latter focuses cross-variable attention on the most relevant, recent time segments to avoid overfitting on outdated correlations. Combining these components, we propose PMDformer, a model designed to effectively capture shape similarity in long-term forecasting scenarios. Extensive experiments indicate that PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in stability and accuracy across multiple LTSF benchmarks. The code is available at https://github.com/aohu1105/PMDformer.

AI.tldr: PMDformer improves long-term time series forecasting by separating patch trends from shape information to better capture similarities across patches and variables.
AI.motivation: Transformer-based models using patch strategies struggle to accurately model shape similarities across patches and variables due to scale differences between trend and residual components, limiting their effectiveness in capturing true shape patterns in long-term forecasting.
AI.method: Proposes PMDformer with three key components: 1) Patch-Mean Decoupling (PMD) separates trend and shape information by subtracting patch means, 2) Trend Restoration Attention (TRA) reintegrates decoupled trends during attention calculation, and 3) Proximal Variable Attention (PVA) focuses cross-variable attention on recent time segments to avoid outdated correlations.
AI.result: PMDformer outperforms existing state-of-the-art methods in both stability and accuracy across multiple long-term time series forecasting benchmarks, demonstrating superior performance in capturing shape similarities.
AI.conclusion: The proposed PMDformer effectively addresses scale differences in patch-based Transformers by decoupling trends from shape information, enabling better capture of shape similarities and cross-variable relationships for improved long-term forecasting performance.

title: Otter Weather: Skillful and Computationally Efficient Medium-Range Weather Forecasting
summary: State-of-the-art medium-range AI weather models can outperform traditional Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) but require massive training budgets. This restricts usage for under-resourced groups and severely limits fast model iteration. Here we develop Otter Weather, a highly efficient spatiotemporal forecasting model designed to democratise high-performance weather prediction with AI. Evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using standard WeatherBench protocols, the Otter family significantly advances the skill-compute Pareto frontier. The deterministic version outperforms the best NWP baseline by 9.6% at a 24-hour lead time while requiring fewer than 3.5 A100-days for training. It provides a 2x efficiency gain over lightweight AI models and a 100-fold reduction in compute compared to resource-intensive frontier architectures. We extend these efficiency gains into probabilistic forecasting by training via the Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS). Scaling to a larger architecture, Otter-XL achieves a 9.7% CRPS improvement over the IFS ENS baseline. This yields an almost two-fold increase in predictive skill over comparable lightweight models at similar compute budgets. Otter-XL also outperforms frontier architectures like GenCast by over 2%, while using an order of magnitude less compute. Finally, Otter is applied out-of-the-box to a complex acoustic scattering PDE task where it outperforms a state-of-the-art foundation modelling approach, suggesting that the advances made here might apply across a range of scientific domains.

AI.tldr: Otter Weather is a highly efficient AI weather forecasting model that achieves state-of-the-art performance with dramatically reduced computational costs compared to both traditional NWP systems and other AI models, enabling wider accessibility and faster iteration.
AI.motivation: Current state-of-the-art AI weather models require massive training budgets, limiting accessibility for under-resourced groups and restricting fast model iteration. There's a need to democratize high-performance weather prediction by developing more efficient AI models.
AI.method: Developed Otter Weather, a highly efficient spatiotemporal forecasting model evaluated on ERA5 reanalysis data at 1.5° resolution using standard WeatherBench protocols. The approach includes deterministic and probabilistic versions (trained via Continuous Ranked Probability Score), with different model sizes including Otter-XL for scaling. The model was also tested on a complex acoustic scattering PDE task.
AI.result: Otter significantly advances the skill-compute Pareto frontier: deterministic version outperforms best NWP baseline by 9.6% at 24-hour lead time with <3.5 A100-days training. Provides 2x efficiency gain over lightweight AI models and 100x compute reduction vs frontier architectures. Otter-XL achieves 9.7% CRPS improvement over IFS ENS baseline, outperforming GenCast by >2% with 10x less compute. Successfully applied to acoustic scattering PDE task, outperforming state-of-the-art foundation modeling.
AI.conclusion: Otter Weather democratizes high-performance weather prediction by achieving superior forecasting skill with dramatically reduced computational requirements, making advanced AI weather models accessible to more researchers and enabling faster innovation cycles. The approach's success on different scientific tasks suggests broad applicability across domains beyond weather forecasting.

title: Explainable Ensemble-Based Machine Learning Models for Detecting the Presence of Cirrhosis in Hepatitis C Patients
summary: Hepatitis C is a liver infection caused by a virus, which results in mild to severe inflammation of the liver. Over many years, hepatitis C gradually damages the liver, often leading to permanent scarring, known as cirrhosis. Patients sometimes have moderate or no symptoms of liver illness for decades before developing cirrhosis. Cirrhosis typically worsens to the point of liver failure. Patients with cirrhosis may also experience brain and nerve system damage, as well as gastrointestinal hemorrhage. Treatment for cirrhosis focuses on preventing further progression of the disease. Detecting cirrhosis earlier is therefore crucial for avoiding complications. Machine learning (ML) has been shown to be effective at providing precise and accurate information for use in diagnosing several diseases. Despite this, no studies have so far used ML to detect cirrhosis in patients with hepatitis C. This study obtained a dataset consisting of 28 attributes of 2038 Egyptian patients from the ML Repository of the University of California at Irvine. Four ML algorithms were trained on the dataset to diagnose cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients: a Random Forest, a Gradient Boosting Machine, an Extreme Gradient Boosting, and an Extra Trees model. The Extra Trees model outperformed the other models achieving an accuracy of 96.92%, a recall of 94.00%, a precision of 99.81%, and an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 96% using only 16 of the 28 features.

AI.tldr: The study developed a machine learning system using Extra Trees algorithm to detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients, achieving 96.92% accuracy with only 16 out of 28 features.
AI.motivation: Hepatitis C causes gradual liver damage leading to cirrhosis, which often has no early symptoms but can progress to liver failure. Early detection is crucial for preventing complications, but no previous studies have used ML for cirrhosis detection in hepatitis C patients despite ML's proven effectiveness in medical diagnosis.
AI.method: Used a dataset of 28 attributes from 2038 Egyptian patients from UCI ML Repository. Trained and compared four ML algorithms: Random Forest, Gradient Boosting Machine, Extreme Gradient Boosting, and Extra Trees model to diagnose cirrhosis.
AI.result: The Extra Trees model outperformed all other models, achieving 96.92% accuracy, 94.00% recall, 99.81% precision, and 96% AUC, using only 16 features (reduced from original 28 features).
AI.conclusion: ML algorithms, particularly Extra Trees, can effectively detect cirrhosis in hepatitis C patients with high accuracy using a reduced feature set, demonstrating the potential for early cirrhosis detection to prevent severe complications.

title: Rethinking Training & Inference for Forecasting: Linking Winner-Take-All back to GMMs
summary: Trajectory forecasting for autonomous driving has advanced rapidly, yet representative models often produce uninformative posteriors over forecast modes, causing problems for mode pruning. We trace this to a modeling-training mismatch: forecasters are typically modeled as conditional Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) but trained with a winner-take-all (WTA) loss that assigns each sample to its nearest mode. We argue that this K-means-like hard assignment (one-hot), while preventing mode collapse, is the source of uninformative mode probabilities: it over-segments the trajectory space, ignores relatedness among nearby modes, and yields assignment instability under small perturbations. Guided by this lens, we introduce two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging that aggregates nearby candidate trajectories; and (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes. Across several WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps produce more informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors and strengthen final forecasts on popular displacement metrics -- without retraining. Our analysis unifies recent design choices through a GMM-vs-K-means perspective and offers principled, practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference.

AI.tldr: Lightweight post-hoc fixes (trajectory merging & soft EM update) for WTA-trained trajectory forecasters to improve uninformative mode posteriors without retraining.
AI.motivation: Trajectory forecasting models for autonomous driving produce uninformative posteriors over forecast modes, causing problems for mode pruning. This stems from a modeling-training mismatch: models are modeled as Gaussian mixture models (GMMs) but trained with winner-take-all (WTA) loss using hard assignments.
AI.method: Two post-hoc treatments: (1) test-time posterior-weighted merging that aggregates nearby candidate trajectories, and (2) a one-step expectation-maximization (EM) update that replaces hard labels with soft responsibilities, sharing probability mass across neighboring modes.
AI.result: Across several WTA-trained architectures, these lightweight steps produce more informative, faithfully ranked mode posteriors and strengthen final forecasts on popular displacement metrics without retraining.
AI.conclusion: The analysis unifies recent design choices through a GMM-vs-K-means perspective and offers principled, practical corrections that better align training objectives with inference.

title: EvoOptiGraph: Weakness-Driven Coevolution via Graph-Based Structural Generation for Optimization Modeling
summary: Automating optimization modeling from natural language with large language models (LLMs) faces two key challenges. First, training corpora lack structural diversity. Second, data generation pipelines remain static and decoupled from model learning. To address these challenges, we propose EvoOptiGraph, a novel framework where data and model co-evolve, driven by model weaknesses. EvoOptiGraph represents each mixed-integer linear program (MILP) as an attributed bipartite graph and applies validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate structurally diverse instances. The evolved graphs are converted into solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation and verified back-translation. Training proceeds in two stages: supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on an initial dataset, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), where graph-derived weakness signals guide the generation of new instances targeting the model's failures. This forms a closed loop that continuously updates the training distribution. Empirical results on six public datasets show that EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines in accuracy, executability, and generalization. These results demonstrate that targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: EvoOptiGraph is a framework where data and LLMs co-evolve for optimization modeling from natural language, using evolutionary graph operations and weakness-guided training to outperform existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM approaches for optimization modeling from natural language face two main challenges: 1) training corpora lack structural diversity, and 2) data generation pipelines are static and decoupled from model learning.
AI.method: Proposes EvoOptiGraph framework with MILP represented as attributed bipartite graphs. Uses validity-preserving evolutionary operators to generate diverse instances, converts graphs to solver code and natural language via deterministic compilation/back-translation. Two-stage training: SFT on initial data, then RLVR with graph-derived weakness signals to target model failures.
AI.result: EvoOptiGraph significantly outperforms larger generalist models, agentic methods, and specialized baselines on six public datasets in accuracy, executability, and generalization.
AI.conclusion: Targeted data-model coevolution is an effective strategy for improving LLMs on optimization modeling tasks, demonstrating the value of continuous, weakness-driven training distribution updates.

title: DualEval: Joint Model-Item Calibration for Unified LLM Evaluation
summary: Current LLM evaluation relies on two complementary but often disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective correctness labels and arena-style preference data that better reflect open-ended user interactions. We introduce DualEval, a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability together with item difficulty and sharpness. We apply DualEval across four domains: coding, math, miscellaneous domain-knowledge tasks, and generic everyday user queries. Our evaluation uses 18 frontier LLMs, static benchmark labels, and reward-model scores validated against held-out human preferences for open-ended model responses. Empirically, our framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support downstream applications such as benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination or outlier analysis. Overall, DualEval unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, producing model rankings and item-level diagnostics that support more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines.

AI.tldr: DualEval is a framework that unifies static benchmarks and preference-based evaluation by jointly modeling models and items in a shared space to produce reliable rankings and item-level diagnostics.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation relies on two disconnected signals: static benchmarks with objective labels and arena-style preference data for open-ended interactions, creating a need for unified evaluation that combines both approaches.
AI.method: Introduces DualEval, a latent model-item calibration framework that represents models and evaluation items in a shared space, jointly estimating model ability with item difficulty and sharpness across four domains using 18 frontier LLMs.
AI.result: The framework produces reliable and balanced model rankings, and its learned item-level profiles support applications like benchmark compression for sample-efficient evaluation and anomaly detection for contamination analysis.
AI.conclusion: DualEval successfully unifies static and arena-style evaluation through joint model-item calibration, enabling more sample-efficient, interpretable, and auditable evaluation pipelines with both rankings and diagnostics.

title: A Multi-Level Validation and Traceability Framework for AI-Generated Telescope Scheduling Decisions
summary: With the gradual introduction of AI into telescope scheduling, AI-based decision-making has shown advantages in handling complex multi-constraint problems. However, its outputs often suffer from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks. In this work, we propose a multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that performs systematic reliability verification of AI-generated decisions prior to execution, and enables explicit representation of the reasoning process to support traceable decision-making. The framework integrates data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational and instrumental constraint verification to filter and correct invalid decisions. It also introduces atomic reasoning units and their dependency relationships, representing scheduling decisions as a sequence of interconnected reasoning steps that support error localization and post hoc analysis. Experiments show that the framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling and reduces loss of transient opportunities. In particular, feedback correction and structured validation of reasoning steps enhance the ability to repair and block erroneous decisions, especially in complex scenarios. Compared with pure AI methods, the framework-enhanced approach maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability and executability. These results demonstrate a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling.

AI.tldr: A multi-level validation and traceable reasoning framework that verifies AI-generated telescope scheduling decisions before execution, improving reliability and executability while maintaining AI's flexibility.
AI.motivation: AI-based telescope scheduling suffers from inconsistent data references, reasoning errors, and non-executable decisions, limiting applicability in high-reliability observational tasks where mistakes can lead to loss of valuable transient opportunities.
AI.method: Proposes a multi-level validation framework with systematic reliability verification, including data reference validation, logical consistency checks, and observational/instrumental constraint verification. Introduces atomic reasoning units with dependency relationships to represent scheduling decisions as interconnected reasoning steps for traceability.
AI.result: The framework improves executability and reliability of AI scheduling, reduces loss of transient opportunities, enhances ability to repair and block erroneous decisions (especially in complex scenarios), and maintains flexibility while substantially improving reliability compared to pure AI methods.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates a feasible and verifiable pathway for applying AI to high-reliability astronomical observation scheduling by providing systematic validation, traceable reasoning, and error correction capabilities.

title: Embedding Foundation Model Predictions in Discrete-Choice Models with Structural Guarantees
summary: Tabular foundation models achieve strong accuracy on choice prediction tasks, but their predictions often violate the economic logic those tasks require: raising a price can increase predicted demand, implied willingness-to-pay estimates are frequently negative or implausible, and unavailable alternatives receive nonzero probability. We propose a two-stage adapter that takes a foundation model's predicted choice probabilities as a precomputed feature and embeds them inside a multinomial logit's utility. In Stage 1, we fit the multinomial logit's structural coefficients by maximum likelihood with sign constraints; in Stage 2, we freeze those coefficients and fit a small neural correction operating on the foundation model's predictions. We prove that this composition exactly preserves the multinomial logit's marginal rate of substitution, so analytically computable value-of-time becomes a mathematical guarantee rather than an empirical accident. Across three datasets and two foundation models, the adapter gains 6.4 percentage points (pp) of test accuracy on average over the multinomial logit and up to 12.8 pp, maintains 100% cost monotonicity, and produces values of time within the published transportation-economics range on the transportation datasets. Performance degrades gracefully under foundation-model context restriction, retaining at least 6 pp of accuracy gain even at 10% of the original foundation-model context.

AI.tldr: A two-stage adapter combines foundation model predictions with multinomial logit to enforce economic rationality while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Tabular foundation models achieve good accuracy but violate economic rationality (price-demand relationships, negative willingness-to-pay, nonzero probability for unavailable options).
AI.method: Two-stage adapter: Stage 1 fits multinomial logit coefficients with sign constraints; Stage 2 freezes coefficients and adds small neural correction on foundation model predictions.
AI.result: Adapter gains 6.4 pp average accuracy over multinomial logit (up to 12.8 pp), maintains 100% cost monotonicity, produces realistic values of time, and degrades gracefully with context restriction.
AI.conclusion: The adapter successfully combines foundation models' predictive power with multinomial logit's economic rationality guarantees, creating reliable choice prediction models.

title: Content-Based Smart E-Mail Dispatcher Using Large Language Models
summary: Email communication has become an integral part of personal and professional life, but handling its vast volume is still a significant issue for large organisations. Manual perusal of emails and forwarding their contents and attachments to intended recipients using other instant messaging platforms has proved to be error-prone and time-consuming leading to losses in terms of productivity and creating undue stress. The main objective of this paper is to explore an alternative mechanism that is to automate the task of dispatching emails based on their contents to the respective WhatsApp groups of students of various semesters of programs in an engineering college, facilitating a smooth flow of information from one end to another end in an organisation. The dispatcher system is built using agents querying large language models (LLMs) to enable it to analyze the contents of emails and route them to the relevant groups of students for their information and consumption. The system harnesses the capabilities of LLMs in analysing the textual contents for decision-making. With a well-structured agent framework prompt that includes email content as input with instructions and context, the system figures out the relevant groups to which the email message is dispatched, thus providing the required information on time. The proposed system does not rely on labelled datasets and provides several benefits, including enhanced productivity and a reduction in the cognitive load associated with reading emails.

AI.tldr: An automated email-to-WhatsApp dispatcher system that uses LLM agents to analyze email content and route messages to appropriate student WhatsApp groups, eliminating manual forwarding in educational organizations.
AI.motivation: Email volume in large organizations creates productivity losses and stress when manually forwarding emails and attachments to intended recipients via instant messaging platforms like WhatsApp. This manual process is error-prone and time-consuming, disrupting information flow in educational institutions.
AI.method: The system uses agents that query large language models (LLMs) to analyze email content. A structured agent framework prompt includes email content, instructions, and context to determine relevant student groups. The approach doesn't rely on labeled datasets but leverages LLM capabilities for textual analysis and decision-making.
AI.result: The system successfully automates email dispatch to appropriate WhatsApp groups based on content analysis, enabling timely information delivery to relevant student groups in engineering college programs across different semesters.
AI.conclusion: The LLM-based email dispatcher system effectively addresses manual email forwarding challenges, enhancing productivity and reducing cognitive load associated with email management in educational organizations while facilitating smooth information flow.

title: Optimizing CUDA like a Human: Micro-Profiling Tools as Expert Surrogates for LLM-Based GPU Kernel Optimization
summary: We present KernelPro, a closed-loop multi-agent system that automatically generates, profiles, and iteratively optimizes GPU kernel code by integrating large language model (LLM) code generation with hardware profiler feedback and pluggable bottleneck detection tools. KernelPro introduces four contributions: (1) a semantic feedback operator that encodes expert heuristics as pluggable micro-profiling tools, transforming raw hardware metrics into actionable natural language guidance; (2) a two-stage tool invocation architecture where roofline-based bottleneck classification filters which specialized analysis tools execute, combining kernel-level (ncu), instruction-level (SASS), and system-level (nsys) profiling; (3) a domain-adapted MCTS with progressive widening, asymmetric branching, log-reward calibration, dead-end pruning, and search memory for cross-iteration learning; and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation via autonomous code search over the CUTLASS/CuTe codebase. On KernelBench, KernelPro achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on Levels 1/2/3, establishing state-of-the-art performance across all difficulty levels. On VeOmni's expert-optimized MoE training kernels, KernelPro achieves 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton by generating a from-scratch raw-CUDA+CuTe Hopper WGMMA kernel. Ablation studies demonstrate that each design component independently and significantly improves optimization quality: micro-profiling tools (p < 0.0001 vs raw metrics), MCTS search (26% higher geometric mean vs greedy, p = 0.004), and proactive tool orchestration (23% improvement, p = 0.035). Finally, KernelPro is the first CUDA kernel coding agent to optimize energy efficiency beyond the speed-only focus of prior systems, demonstrating an 11.6% measured energy reduction at matched speed.

AI.tldr: KernelPro is an AI-powered system that automatically optimizes GPU kernel code using LLMs with hardware profiler feedback, achieving significant speedups and energy efficiency improvements.
AI.motivation: Current GPU kernel optimization requires extensive manual expertise. The paper aims to automate this process by integrating LLM code generation with hardware profiling feedback to create an intelligent, closed-loop optimization system.
AI.method: KernelPro uses a multi-agent system with four key components: (1) semantic feedback operators that convert hardware metrics to natural language guidance, (2) two-stage tool invocation with bottleneck classification, (3) domain-adapted MCTS search with advanced features, and (4) direct CuTe source-level code generation with autonomous code search.
AI.result: Achieves geometric mean speedups of 2.42x/4.69x/5.30x on three difficulty levels, 1.23x over hand-tuned Triton kernels, and shows statistically significant improvements from all design components. Also demonstrates 11.6% energy reduction at matched speed.
AI.conclusion: KernelPro represents a significant advancement in automated GPU kernel optimization, successfully integrating LLMs with hardware profiling to achieve state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks while also addressing energy efficiency - a dimension overlooked by prior systems.

title: LLM-based Models for Detecting Emerging Topics in Service Feedback
summary:
Enhancing the analysis of service feedback is essential for public sector organizations, particularly tax administrations, where trust and compliance depend on fair and effective service delivery. As feedback volumes grow, identifying emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations becomes increasingly challenging. Traditional approaches often rely on manual review or static expert-defined indicators, limiting scalability and the ability to capture complex patterns in textual feedback.
  This paper presents a novel methodology that integrates large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration to improve multilingual customer feedback analysis. The primary objective is to detect emerging service quality topics that may also reveal potential inequities in service delivery. Our framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight to produce accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses.
  The proposed approach was evaluated using similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers, demonstrating stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models. By incorporating a human-in-the-loop framework, the methodology reduces LLM fabrication while improving the reliability and relevance of generated insights.
  The results demonstrate the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise to support scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. This work contributes to the development of responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through more effective analysis of multilingual customer feedback.

AI.tldr: A novel human-AI collaboration framework using LLMs and statistical methods to analyze multilingual customer feedback for detecting service quality issues and potential inequities in public sector tax administrations.
AI.motivation: Public sector organizations, especially tax administrations, need to analyze growing volumes of customer feedback to maintain trust and compliance. Traditional manual review or static expert-defined approaches lack scalability and struggle to capture complex patterns in textual feedback, making it difficult to identify emerging service quality issues and potential disparities across diverse populations.
AI.method: A novel methodology integrating large language models (LLMs), statistical techniques, and human-AI collaboration. The framework combines fine-tuned, quantized LLMs with expert oversight for accurate, computationally efficient, and context-aware analyses. It uses a human-in-the-loop approach to reduce LLM fabrication and improve reliability.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated stronger alignment with expert judgments than baseline models through similarity analysis and assessments from experienced tax officers. It reduces LLM fabrication while improving reliability and relevance of generated insights.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates the practicality of combining LLMs with human expertise for scalable, evidence-based decision-making in public sector organizations. It contributes to responsible AI systems that enhance service quality, responsiveness, fairness, and public trust through effective multilingual customer feedback analysis.

title: Finding the Time to Think: Learning Planning Budgets in Real-Time RL
summary: Deliberating takes time. In real-time settings, that time is not free. Standard reinforcement learning (RL) sidesteps this as the environment waits indefinitely for the agent's decision. Instead, we study real-time RL environments where the environment progresses while waiting for the agent's action. Building on prior real-time formalizations, we introduce variable-delay real-time RL, where the agent chooses how long to deliberate at each decision point since the environment progresses. For the planning agents we use, the right delay is state-dependent, and naively planning how long to plan can paralyze the agent. We instead approach this setting by training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets. Across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go, our gating policy outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines, and transfers to a real-time setup where the environment and agent run on two different GPUs.

AI.tldr: Training a lightweight gating policy on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets outperforms fixed-budget approaches in real-time RL environments where the environment progresses during deliberation.
AI.motivation: Standard RL assumes the environment waits indefinitely for agent decisions, but in real-time settings, deliberation time is costly as the environment continues to progress. The paper aims to address this gap by studying real-time RL where the environment advances while waiting for agent actions.
AI.method: Introduces variable-delay real-time RL where agents choose deliberation time at each decision point. Uses a lightweight gating policy trained on top of a planner to select state-dependent planning budgets, avoiding the paralysis that comes from naively planning how long to plan.
AI.result: The gating policy approach outperforms fixed-budget and heuristic baselines across real-time Pac-Man, Tetris, Snake, Speed Hex, and Speed Go. It also successfully transfers to a real-time setup where environment and agent run on different GPUs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively manages deliberation time in real-time RL environments by using a learned gating policy to adapt planning budgets based on state, demonstrating practical value across multiple domains and real-world computational setups.

title: Autoformalization of Agent Instructions into Policy-as-Code
comment: Accepted at the Second Workshop on Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond (AIWILD), ICML 2026
summary: Agent safety in high-stakes domains requires formal policy enforcement, but most existing approaches either rely on probabilistic guardrails (fine-tuned classifiers, prompt-based steering) that offer no formal guarantees, or on hand-coded symbolic enforcement that does not scale to the breadth of real policy specifications. We present an autoformalization pipeline that translates agent prompts, MCP tool descriptions, and natural language policy documents into formally verified policies using an LLM-based generator-critic loop. The resulting policies are written in the Cedar Policy Language. On the MedAgentBench benchmark, our autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than the hand-coded symbolic enforcement in prior work.

AI.tldr: A system that automatically translates natural language agent policies into formally verified policies using LLMs, achieving better coverage than manual approaches.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for agent safety either lack formal guarantees (probabilistic methods) or don't scale well (hand-coded symbolic enforcement). There's a need for scalable formal policy enforcement for high-stakes domains.
AI.method: An autoformalization pipeline that uses LLM-based generator-critic loops to translate agent prompts, tool descriptions, and natural language policies into formally verified policies in Cedar Policy Language.
AI.result: On the MedAgentBench benchmark, the autoformalized policies cover substantially more of the source natural-language specification than hand-coded symbolic enforcement from prior work.
AI.conclusion: The approach successfully bridges the gap between scalable natural language policy specification and formally verified enforcement, offering both coverage and formal guarantees for agent safety in high-stakes domains.

title: A Causal Foundation Model for Structure and Outcome Prediction
comment: 20 pages, 7 figures, 17 tables, 43rd ICML Workshop on Foundation Models for Structured Data
summary: We introduce TabPFN-CFM, a causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal problems. TabPFN-CFM predicts both causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports queries on all three levels of Pearl's Causal Hierarchy and uses known graph structure when available to improve predictions. TabPFN-CFM is trained on synthetic datasets, and generalises to real datasets, demonstrating improved performance over both structural and outcome prediction baselines.

AI.tldr: TabPFN-CFM is a causal foundation model that handles multiple causal problems, predicts causal structure and outcomes from observational data, supports Pearl's Causal Hierarchy queries, and leverages known graph structure when available.
AI.motivation: There's a need for models that can handle multiple causal inference tasks simultaneously, work with observational data, and leverage prior structural knowledge when available to improve predictions.
AI.method: The model is trained on synthetic datasets and generalizes to real-world data. It predicts both causal structure and outcomes, supports queries across Pearl's Causal Hierarchy (association, intervention, counterfactual levels), and can incorporate known graph structure to enhance predictions.
AI.result: TabPFN-CFM demonstrates improved performance over both structural prediction baselines and outcome prediction baselines, showing it effectively generalizes from synthetic training data to real datasets.
AI.conclusion: TabPFN-CFM represents an effective causal foundation model that can handle multiple causal inference problems, bridge different levels of causal reasoning, and leverage structural information to improve predictive performance across real-world applications.

title: SKILL-DISCO: Distilling and Compiling Agent Traces into Reusable Procedural Skills
summary: Agents often repeatedly solve similar task instances from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning cost and long execution traces. Prior work has explored workflow reuse and executable skill induction, but it remains unclear which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how the shared procedural structure should be represented across successful traces. We study this problem in FSM-defined scenarios, where successful traces can be viewed as paths in an unknown transition graph, and formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. Based on this view, we introduce SkillDisCo, a distillation-and-compilation framework that distills reusable PFSM subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills. Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales, demonstrating the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.

AI.tldr: SkillDisCo is a framework that extracts reusable procedural skills from successful agent execution traces to improve efficiency in repeated tasks.
AI.motivation: Agents repeatedly solve similar tasks from scratch, leading to unnecessary reasoning costs and long execution traces. Current approaches to workflow reuse and skill induction lack clear understanding of which task scenarios admit procedural skills and how to represent shared procedural structures across traces.
AI.method: The authors study FSM-defined scenarios where successful traces are paths in an unknown transition graph. They formulate procedural skills as reusable parameterized control-flow subgraphs. SkillDisCo distills reusable Probabilistic Finite State Machine (PFSM) subgraphs from successful traces and compiles them into callable, executable, and verifiable procedural skills.
AI.result: Experiments on ALFWorld and WebArena show that SkillDisCo improves success rates and reduces agent turns across benchmarks and model scales. This demonstrates the benefits of representing shared experience as reusable execution structures.
AI.conclusion: Representing shared agent experience as reusable procedural skills through distillation and compilation improves task performance and efficiency, reducing redundant reasoning and execution costs in repeated task scenarios.

title: Epiphany-Aware KV Cache Eviction Without the Attention Matrix
summary: As reasoning models emit chains of thought tens of thousands of tokens long, KV cache increasingly becomes a deployment bottleneck. Existing cache eviction methods rank tokens by attention weight, which is a noisy importance proxy in long reasoning traces, and prohibits the use of fused kernels in production inference by forcing the model to materialize the attention matrix. In this work, we instead score tokens with a metric we term the epiphany score: the change in the model's internal representation, read directly from the forward pass with no attention matrix and negligible extra state. Our resulting cache eviction method, EpiKV, requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel, and can be used directly in FlashAttention inference stacks unchanged -- scaling to a 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. upper-mid layers negatively) and remove a positional trend with a causal rolling z-score. At a 4096-token cache EpiKV reaches 72% on MATH-500, matching the strongest attention-based baseline (ThinKV 71%, H2O 67%); a lag-normalized KV variant reaches 37% on AIME-2024 at 8192 tokens against the best of them (33%), at up to 2.8x the speed.

AI.tldr: EpiKV is a KV cache eviction method that scores tokens using epiphany score (change in model's internal representation) instead of attention weights, enabling longer reasoning contexts without custom kernels or training.
AI.motivation: KV cache becomes a bottleneck as reasoning models emit long chains of thought (tens of thousands of tokens). Existing attention-based eviction methods are noisy for long reasoning traces and force models to materialize attention matrices, preventing use of fused kernels in production inference.
AI.method: EpiKV scores tokens using epiphany score - the change in model's internal representation, measured directly from forward pass without attention matrix and with negligible extra state. It adjusts scores by layer (upper-mid layers negatively) and removes positional trend with causal rolling z-score. Requires no training, classifier, or custom kernel and works with FlashAttention inference stacks.
AI.result: Scales to 16x longer feasible context than attention-based scoring. At 4096-token cache: 72% on MATH-500 (matches ThinKV 71%, beats H2O 67%). At 8192 tokens: 37% on AIME-2024 (beats best baseline 33%) with up to 2.8x speed improvement.
AI.conclusion: EpiKV provides an effective, lightweight KV cache eviction method that outperforms attention-based baselines on reasoning tasks, enables significantly longer contexts, works with existing inference stacks, and improves inference speed without requiring training or custom implementations.

title: NebulaExp-8B: An Empirical Post-Training Pipeline via Full-Scale Ablation Research
summary: Post-training alignment determines the reasoning and human preference following capabilities of large language models, yet most existing works withhold detailed data construction, filtering rules and training recipes, which hinders community reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. This work presents NebulaExp, a fully transparent, ablation-driven post-training pipeline built on Qwen3-8B-base, covering two orthogonal model branches: general instruct model and complex reasoning-specialized model. We curate a raw corpus of 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and a 200K verifiable RL candidate pool, and design an end-to-end data processing stack including response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification and diversity-aware sampling. For the Instruct branch, our three-stage optimized supervised fine-tuning approach NebulaExp-Ins-SFT improves the average benchmark score from the 55.01 baseline of Qwen3-8B-nothink to 60.99. GRPO reinforcement learning then further elevates the average score to 61.85. For the Reasoning branch, medium-difficulty GRPO RL improves average reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. To address RL's dependency on task verifiers, we systematically investigate single-teacher and multi-teacher OPD (MOPD): utilizing merely 4K instruction-following samples and outperforms RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval with +4.43 average overall gain; MOPD fuses four domain-specialist teachers with merely 10K samples, lifting average performance by 4.18 over the base model. This report provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs, and comprehensively dissects the capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation and general knowledge.

AI.tldr: NebulaExp is a fully transparent post-training pipeline for 8B-scale LLMs with two branches: general instruction following and complex reasoning specialization, featuring comprehensive data curation, multi-stage SFT, RL training, and novel OPD methods that outperform baselines across multiple benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing post-training alignment works lack transparency in data construction, filtering rules, and training recipes, hindering reproducibility and lightweight model optimization. There's a need for a fully transparent, ablation-driven approach that enables community reproducibility and systematic capability trade-off analysis.
AI.method: Built on Qwen3-8B-base with two branches: general instruct and complex reasoning. Created 3.84M multi-source SFT samples and 200K verifiable RL candidates. Used response distillation, multi-dimensional cross-verification filtering, fine-grained difficulty grading, task classification, and diversity-aware sampling. Applied three-stage optimized SFT (NebulaExp-Ins-SFT), GRPO reinforcement learning, and introduced single/multi-teacher OPD methods requiring minimal samples.
AI.result: Instruct branch: SFT improved average benchmark score from 55.01 to 60.99; GRPO RL further increased to 61.85. Reasoning branch: GRPO RL improved reasoning score from 73.88 to 75.17. OPD methods: single-teacher with 4K samples outperformed RL baseline by 3.26 points on IFEval (+4.43 overall); multi-teacher with 10K samples lifted average performance by 4.18 points over base model.
AI.conclusion: The work provides a fully reproducible empirical post-training recipe for 8B-scale LLMs and comprehensively analyzes capability trade-offs among instruction adherence, mathematical reasoning, code generation, and general knowledge. Demonstrates that transparent, ablation-driven approaches with innovative training methods (like OPD) can achieve significant improvements with minimal data.

title: When Does Quality-Aware Multimodal Fusion Matter? A Leakage-Safe Diagnostic for Decision-Level Dependence
comment: Accepted to INTERSPEECH 2026. 5 pages, 1 figure, 5 tables
summary: Many multimodal systems estimate the reliability of each modality and weight their contributions to the final prediction. However, it remains unclear whether these scores influence model decisions or merely correlate with performance. We propose a simple diagnostic to test whether reliability information is used during inference. After training, the model and inputs are fixed while reliability scores are permuted across test examples. If predictions depend on these scores, performance should degrade. Experiments on StressID for stress recognition and CMU-MOSEI for sentiment analysis show that permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive controls where reliability signals identify the correct modality, the same frozen fusion rules yield significant improvements, indicating that reliability signals influence fused decisions only when they reliably predict unimodal correctness.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a diagnostic test to determine if multimodal models actually use reliability scores during inference, finding that most models ignore them despite potential performance gains.
AI.motivation: While many multimodal systems estimate modality reliability scores and weight modalities accordingly, it's unclear whether these scores actually influence model decisions or are just correlated with performance without causal impact.
AI.method: A diagnostic test where reliability scores are permuted across test examples while keeping the model and inputs fixed. If predictions truly depend on these scores, performance should degrade when scores are randomly assigned. Tested on StressID (stress recognition) and CMU-MOSEI (sentiment analysis) datasets.
AI.result: Permuting reliability scores leaves performance unchanged despite substantial potential gains from selecting the best modality per example. In positive control experiments where reliability signals reliably predict unimodal correctness, the same fusion rules yield significant improvements, showing reliability scores influence decisions only when they reliably predict modality correctness.
AI.conclusion: Current multimodal fusion approaches with reliability estimation often ignore the reliability signals they compute during inference. Reliability scores only influence fused decisions when they reliably predict which modality is correct for each example.

title: Do Safety Guardrails Need to Reason? LeanGuard: A Fast and Light Approach for Robust Moderation
comment: 9 pages, 6 figures, 3 tables. Project page: https://ndb796.github.io/LeanGuard ; code and models: https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard
summary: In order to screen a prompt or a response, the recent guardrail methods generate a chain-of-thought (CoT) before they issue a verdict. This design follows a common belief that step-by-step reasoning improves a decision. However, CoT also makes the guard heavy and slow, because the model must generate many tokens before it decides. This may not match how guardrails are actually deployed. A guardrail sometimes should not be heavy and slow, and it often runs on-device, for example on an embodied robot. In this paper, we pose a question whether a safety guardrail really needs to reason. To answer this question, we train a lightweight bidirectional encoder and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, and we then remove only the reasoning while we keep everything else fixed. With this controlled same-base comparison, we show that the chain does not improve moderation accuracy. We name the resulting guard LeanGuard. A 395M label-only encoder reaches an average F1 of 82.90 $\pm$ 0.26 over public benchmarks. It matches a reasoning guard that is built on a much larger decoder, while it uses only a single forward pass over an input of at most 512 tokens. This is about a ~100x reduction in inference compute. We further show that this label-only encoder stays robust under training-label noise and retains far more recall at a strict false-positive rate than the reasoning guard, so a heavier reasoning guard is not the more robust choice either. Our finding suggests that the current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and that the necessity of CoT for moderation is still not proven. We release all source codes and models including LeanGuard at https://github.com/ndb796/LeanGuard.

AI.tldr: Lightweight label-only safety guardrail (LeanGuard) matches or outperforms reasoning-based approaches with ~100x less compute, challenging the necessity of chain-of-thought for content moderation.
AI.motivation: Current guardrail methods use chain-of-thought reasoning for content moderation decisions, making them heavy, slow, and unsuitable for resource-constrained environments like embodied robots. The paper questions whether safety guardrails actually need to reason.
AI.method: Train a lightweight bidirectional encoder (LeanGuard) and a reasoning guard on the same corpus, then remove only the reasoning component while keeping everything else fixed for controlled comparison. LeanGuard is a 395M parameter label-only encoder using a single forward pass on up to 512 tokens.
AI.result: LeanGuard achieves average F1 of 82.90 ± 0.26 on public benchmarks, matching reasoning guards built on larger decoders while using ~100x less inference compute. It also shows better robustness under training-label noise and higher recall at strict false-positive rates.
AI.conclusion: Chain-of-thought reasoning doesn't improve moderation accuracy in current benchmarks. Current guardrail benchmarks may not be hard enough to reward reasoning, and the necessity of CoT for moderation is not proven. LeanGuard provides an efficient alternative for on-device deployment.

title: Localizing RL-Induced Tool Use to a Single Crosscoder Feature
comment: Accepted as a spotlight at the ICML 2026 Mechanistic Interpretability Workshop
summary: Fine-tuning through RL reshapes the internal representations of language models to enable agentic behaviors such as tool use, yet the mechanistic basis of these changes remains poorly understood. While RL substantially improves structured tool-call generation, it is unclear which features emerge, which are preserved, and whether identified features can be leveraged for retraining-free behavioral control. In this work, we show that $\textit{Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC)}$ isolate a compact set of RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in $\texttt{Qwen2.5-3B}$. Across a $48$-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep, encode-decode reconstruction improves the RL model's tool correctness by $+31.1 \pm {9.7}$ pp and passively transfers tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by $+6.8 \pm 5.0$ pp which we call a $\textit{capability spillover}$. Our findings show that DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set that enables runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs.

AI.tldr: RL fine-tuning creates tool-use capabilities in language models, and Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) can isolate compact RL-specific features that enable both improved tool-calling and capability transfer to base models without retraining.
AI.motivation: While RL fine-tuning enables agentic behaviors like tool use in language models, the mechanistic basis of these changes is poorly understood. It's unclear which features emerge vs. are preserved, and whether these features can be used for retraining-free behavioral control.
AI.method: The paper introduces Dedicated Feature Crosscoders (DFC) to isolate RL-specific features that mediate tool-calling capability in Qwen2.5-3B. They conducted a 48-crosscoder hyperparameter sweep and used encode-decode reconstruction to analyze feature separation.
AI.result: DFC reconstruction improved RL model's tool correctness by +31.1 ± 9.7 percentage points and passively transferred tool-calling ability to the frozen base model by +6.8 ± 5.0 pp (capability spillover). DFC partitioning concentrates RL-introduced capability into a minimal, steerable feature set.
AI.conclusion: DFC isolates compact RL-specific features that enable runtime behavioral control of agentic LLMs without retraining, demonstrating that RL-introduced capabilities can be concentrated into a minimal, steerable feature set for retraining-free control.

title: Kalman Prototypical Networks for Few-shot Fault Detection in Combined Cycle Gas Turbines
summary: Combined-cycle gas turbines (CCGTs) play a key role in modern power generation, offering both high efficiency and reduced environmental impact. However, their complex thermo-fluid and mechanical interactions complicate fault detection, particularly when labeled fault data are scarce. In this paper, we introduce the Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning (FSL) framework specifically tailored for CCGT fault diagnosis. We model the evolution of class prototypes as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system to reduce episodic variance and improve robustness in embedding representation. Synthetic data sets generated with a high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulation of an offshore CCGT system were used, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions. Application of the proposed framework on simulated leak fault detection tasks demonstrate that KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods such as Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The proposed framework significantly improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations, making it well-suited for real-world CCGT fault detection where labeled data is limited.

AI.tldr: A Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN) is proposed for few-shot fault diagnosis of combined-cycle gas turbines, outperforming conventional methods in accuracy and stability when labeled fault data is scarce.
AI.motivation: CCGTs are complex systems with scarce labeled fault data, making traditional fault detection methods challenging to apply effectively. There's a need for robust few-shot learning approaches that can work with limited labeled examples for fault diagnosis.
AI.method: The authors introduce Kalman Prototypical Network (KPN), a metric-based few-shot learning framework that models class prototype evolution as latent stochastic states in a dynamic system. The approach uses synthetic data generated from high-fidelity Modelica-based dynamic simulations of an offshore CCGT system, simulating both normal operation and progressive leak faults under transient conditions.
AI.result: KPN outperforms conventional FSL methods (Matching Networks, Relation Networks, and MAML) in both accuracy and stability under varying support and query configurations. The framework improves training convergence and generalization by stabilizing class representations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed KPN framework significantly enhances fault detection for CCGT systems where labeled data is limited, making it well-suited for real-world applications through improved robustness in embedding representation and reduced episodic variance.

title: Retrieval-Warmed Energy-Based Reasoning: A Five-Arm Ablation Methodology for Diffusion-as-Inference on Structured Reasoning Tasks
summary: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it is rarely clear which part of the pipeline carries the gain. We study \textbf{retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR)} -- an IRED energy-based diffusion model \cite{du2024ired} augmented with a Modern Hopfield trajectory memory -- and contribute a \textbf{five-arm ablation methodology} (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) that separates three confounded effects: class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, and graph-aligned value reuse. The diagnostic decomposition is adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation \cite{ru2024ragchecker}. On \textbf{connectivity-2} (Erdős--Rényi all-pairs reachability), the aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches \textbf{$+35$\,pp} balanced accuracy on a fixed 1{,}000-graph validation-set diagnostic, with value distribution and retrieval mechanics fixed, only per-graph alignment destroyed, while per-query random initialisation falls below cold -- per-graph alignment, not bias shift or stochasticity, dominates. Yet the \emph{deployable} cold-prediction pipeline misses the acceptance gate at stored-value quality. The same diagnostic logic, stopped at the key-quality screen, applied to \textbf{Sudoku} with a task-specific key encoder produces a clean negative at a \emph{different} component -- key quality, under the current setup. The decomposition names the first blocking component on each task. The setting -- graph reachability refined by an iterative diffusion sampler, with explainability of failure modes as the lens -- places the work within structured and spatio-temporal reasoning.

AI.tldr: A framework for analyzing warm-started diffusion samplers using five-arm ablation to decompose three confounded effects (class-prior bias shift, stochastic warm-starting, graph-aligned value reuse) and identify blocking components in different reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Warm-started diffusion samplers accelerate iterative inference, but it's unclear which part of the pipeline contributes to performance gains. There's a need to disentangle confounding effects to understand the source of improvements and identify blocking components in different reasoning tasks.
AI.method: Proposes retrieval-warmed energy-based reasoning (RW-EBR) - an IRED energy-based diffusion model augmented with Modern Hopfield trajectory memory. Uses a five-arm ablation methodology (oracle, best-constant, per-query-random, shuffled, aligned) adapted from LLM-RAG evaluation to separate three confounded effects. Tested on connectivity-2 (Erdős–Rényi reachability) and Sudoku tasks with task-specific key encoders.
AI.result: On connectivity-2, aligned-vs-shuffled-oracle swing reaches +35pp balanced accuracy, showing per-graph alignment dominates improvements (not bias shift or stochasticity). However, deployable cold-prediction pipeline misses acceptance gate at stored-value quality. For Sudoku, the diagnostic reveals a different blocking component - key quality under current setup. The decomposition successfully identifies the first blocking component for each task.
AI.conclusion: The five-arm ablation methodology effectively decomposes confounding effects in warm-started diffusion samplers, revealing different blocking components across reasoning tasks (per-graph alignment for connectivity-2, key quality for Sudoku). This provides explainable failure analysis for structured and spatio-temporal reasoning systems using iterative diffusion samplers.

title: LithoDreamer: A Physics-Informed World Model for Multi-Stage Computational Lithography
comment: Correspondence to: Qi Sun <qisunchn \at zju \dot edu \dot cn>
summary: As semiconductor technology nodes scale, computational lithography is essential for ensuring yield and performance. However, lithography is a continuous physical process involving mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development, which existing models fail to capture. To overcome this limitation, we present LithoDreamer, the first physics-informed World Model (WM) framework for computational lithography, which formulates the ``Layout-Mask-Resist Image-After Development Image (ADI)'' pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. LithoDreamer captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces, in which it controls process intervention exploration and drives subsequent state transitions. To achieve interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision, we propose a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts the latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints, guiding the model to generate evolutions consistent with real lithography physics. Experiments show LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in forward evolution and inverse planning. Our lithography dataset is publicly available at GitHub (https://github.com/7jiangyq/lithodreamer.git).

AI.tldr: LithoDreamer is the first physics-informed World Model framework for computational lithography that models the full layout-to-ADI pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system using contrastive variational optimization.
AI.motivation: As semiconductor technology scales, computational lithography is crucial for yield and performance, but existing models fail to capture the continuous physical process of mask optimization, optical imaging, resist exposure, and development.
AI.method: Proposes LithoDreamer, a physics-informed World Model framework that formulates the layout-to-ADI pipeline as a decision-driven multi-step evolution system. Captures feature changes between adjacent states to model stage-specific physics-informed latent spaces. Uses a contrastive variational optimization paradigm that contrasts latent differences between intervention paths with variational evolution constraints for interpretable intervention optimization without continuous supervision.
AI.result: LithoDreamer achieves state-of-the-art performance in both forward evolution and inverse planning. The authors have also publicly released their lithography dataset on GitHub.
AI.conclusion: LithoDreamer successfully overcomes the limitations of existing computational lithography models by providing a physics-informed World Model framework that accurately captures the continuous lithography process and enables interpretable intervention optimization, representing a significant advancement for semiconductor manufacturing as technology nodes scale.

title: What Survives When You Compress a Recursive Reasoner for the Edge?
summary: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex structured tasks with only a few million parameters by repeatedly updating a latent state. Deploying these models on edge hardware requires significant compression, but unlike conventional sequence models, quantization errors compound across recursive reasoning cycles rather than across output tokens. As a result, standard intuitions about compression fail to apply. In this work, we ask what survives when recursive reasoners are compressed. Across a full precision sweep, three tasks, and two recursive architectures, we find that aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero under naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention alike. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair it. The collapse is architectural -- it strikes MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on the same task -- and we reverse it with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. We also introduce carry-trajectory fidelity, the cosine similarity to the full-precision reasoning path, as a label-free signal that predicts this damage and its recovery before a task evaluation. The combined result is a deployment recipe: flash-streamed embeddings remove a 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.

AI.tldr: Recursive reasoning models lose global reasoning capabilities when aggressively compressed, but this can be recovered with per-channel calibrated INT4 quantization without retraining.
AI.motivation: Recursive reasoning models can solve complex tasks with few parameters, making them suitable for edge deployment. However, compression for edge hardware presents unique challenges since quantization errors compound across reasoning cycles rather than output tokens, making standard compression intuitions inapplicable.
AI.method: The study conducts a full precision sweep across three tasks and two recursive architectures. It examines compression techniques including naive INT4 pruning, distillation, and linear attention. It introduces carry-trajectory fidelity (cosine similarity to full-precision reasoning paths) as a label-free damage prediction metric. A deployment recipe combines flash-streamed embeddings, INT8 optimization, and calibrated INT4 quantization.
AI.result: Aggressive compression preserves local prediction but destroys global reasoning: cell accuracy holds while puzzle-exact accuracy collapses to zero. Token-level objectives, including quantization-aware training, cannot repair this. The collapse is architectural - it affects MLP-mixing recursion but not attention on same tasks. Recovery is possible with per-channel calibrated INT4 without retraining. Deployment achieves: flash-streamed embeddings remove 99.4MB bottleneck, INT8 at one cycle matches full-depth accuracy at 6x fewer FLOPs (8MB SoC), and calibrated INT4 fits a 4MB microcontroller.
AI.conclusion: Recursive reasoning models exhibit unique compression failure modes where global reasoning collapses under aggressive quantization while local predictions survive. Architectural differences (MLP-mixing vs attention) affect sensitivity. Carry-trajectory fidelity effectively predicts this damage. A practical deployment recipe enables efficient edge deployment through calibrated quantization strategies.

title: A Latent ODE Approach to Spatiotemporal Modeling of Cine Cardiac MRI
summary: Cardiac magnetic resonance imaging (CMR) captures rich spatiotemporal information about ventricular structure and motion, but conventional risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases. We present a latent dynamical model that encodes bi-ventricular anatomy and full-cycle cine motion as a continuous latent trajectory, using heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equation (ODE) dynamics and a graph-based mesh autoencoder to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion. A covariate-conditioned prior defines the expected end-diastolic latent state, and a Cox proportional hazards model tests whether deviations from this prior predict incident heart failure. We studied 72,386 UK Biobank participants without baseline cardiovascular disease, including 367 incident heart failure events. In a held-out evaluation subset, adding the latent score to refitted pooled cohort equations improved the stratified C-index from 0.704 to 0.785, compared with 0.764 for seven established cardiac markers. Compared with non-graph and non-ODE approaches, the proposed model gave the best trade-off between reconstruction fidelity, generative realism, and downstream prognostic performance. These results suggest that continuous full-cycle modeling of ventricular motion provides informative cardiac phenotypes beyond conventional CMR summaries, while external validation in more representative patient cohorts is required before clinical risk-prediction use.

AI.tldr: A latent dynamical model uses heart-rate-aware neural ODEs and graph-based mesh autoencoders to encode full-cycle 3D cardiac motion from CMR images, enabling continuous trajectory modeling that predicts heart failure risk better than conventional cardiac markers.
AI.motivation: Conventional cardiac risk models use only a few image-derived indices from selected cardiac phases, wasting most of the rich spatiotemporal information in CMR data that captures full ventricular motion cycles.
AI.method: Combines heart-rate-aware neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs) with graph-based mesh autoencoders to reconstruct anatomically consistent 3D+t ventricular motion as continuous latent trajectories, then uses covariate-conditioned priors and Cox proportional hazards modeling for heart failure prediction.
