The dataset is provided at multiple levels of validation, enabling users to balance realism and scalability depending on their use case. This dataset supports the development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems, including summarisation tools, coding models, and decision support systems, without reliance on real patient data.

AI.tldr: A pipeline for generating synthetic clinical notes that combines structured patient generation, journey simulation, and LLM-based note creation to enable AI development without privacy risks of real patient data.
AI.motivation: Clinical documentation in healthcare is highly sensitive, restricting access to real-world data needed for developing and evaluating AI systems. There's a need for synthetic clinical data that avoids privacy risks while supporting AI tool development.
AI.method: Modular pipeline combining: 1) structured patient generation, 2) semi-structured patient journey simulation, and 3) unstructured clinical note generation using large language models. Includes LLM-based validation and augmentation steps to improve faithfulness, realism, and diversity.
AI.result: Released dataset of 70 synthetic patients, each with 20-50 clinical notes spanning a full hospital journey. Dataset provided at multiple validation levels to balance realism and scalability for different use cases.
AI.conclusion: The synthetic clinical notes pipeline and dataset enable development, testing, and evaluation of clinical AI systems (summarisation, coding, decision support) without relying on real patient data, addressing privacy concerns while maintaining clinical realism.

title: Zero-Shot Size Transfer for Neural ODEs on Sparse Random Graphs: Graphon Limits and Adjoint Convergence
summary: Graph Neural Differential Equations (GNDEs) model continuous-time graph dynamics by parameterizing Neural ODE velocity fields with Graph Neural Networks. Their local, size-independent filters suggest a zero-shot size-transfer principle: train on a small graph and deploy on larger, similar graphs without retraining. We develop a quantitative theory for this principle on sparse random graphs sampled from graphons. We consider Graphon Neural Differential Equations (Graphon-NDEs) and adjoint Graphon-NDEs as the infinite-node limits of the forward and adjoint GNDE systems, and establish well-posedness. For an $n$-node random graph with sparsity parameter $α_n$, we prove trajectory-wise convergence of GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions at rate $O((α_n n)^{-1/2})$, up to logarithmic factors, with high probability. We also establish uniform-in-time convergence bounds for adjoint systems governing hidden-state and parameter gradients. We further study discretize-then-optimize (DTO) and optimize-then-discretize (OTD) training. Under explicit Euler discretization with $M$ steps, we show that DTO and OTD are asymptotically consistent, with hidden-state and local parameter-gradient discrepancies of orders $O(1/M)$ and $O(1/M^2)$, respectively, up to sparsity and logarithmic factors. Experiments on HSBM and tent graphons support the theoretical rates, while zero-shot transfer experiments across four graphon classes demonstrate accurate deployment of learned GNDEs on larger independently sampled graphs.

AI.tldr: GNDEs enable zero-shot size transfer: train on small graphs and deploy on larger graphs without retraining, with theoretical guarantees for convergence on sparse random graphs.
AI.motivation: To provide a theoretical foundation for the zero-shot size transfer principle of Graph Neural Differential Equations, which allows training on small graphs and deploying on larger graphs without retraining, using graphon theory.
AI.method: Develop Graphon Neural Differential Equations as infinite-node limits of GNDEs, establish well-posedness, prove convergence rates for forward and adjoint systems, and analyze discretize-then-optimize vs. optimize-then-discretize training approaches with Euler discretization.
AI.result: Prove O((α_n n)^{-1/2}) convergence rate for GNDE solutions to Graphon-NDE solutions, establish uniform-in-time bounds for adjoint systems, show asymptotic consistency of DTO/OTD with gradient discrepancies of O(1/M) and O(1/M^2), and validate with experiments on HSBM and tent graphons.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical framework justifies the zero-shot size transfer capability of GNDEs, with rigorous convergence guarantees and practical validation across multiple graphon classes, enabling efficient scaling of graph neural networks to larger graphs.

title: Generative Retrieval via Diffusion Transformer with Metric-Ordered Sequence Training and Hybrid-Policy Preference Optimization
summary: Embedding-based retrieval ranks items by their similarity to a query in a shared vector space and usually aims to return the highest-scoring items. In many production settings this is not what is wanted: given a seed set that expresses a fine-grained pattern, one needs more items that both satisfy a target attribute and stay within that pattern. We formalize this as pattern-preserving attribute retrieval. The two goals pull against each other: averaging the seeds preserves the pattern but stays in a low-attribute region, while global attribute retrieval drifts to unrelated patterns. We approach the task with continuous generative retrieval, where a model reads a sequence of item embeddings and generates query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. We propose MO-DiT+HPPO, a staged framework with raw-sequence pretraining, multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining, tail-centroid fine-tuning, and HPPO. Metric-ordered training turns sparse online retrieval labels into in-pattern trajectories ordered from low to high predicted attribute density, teaching one model the metric-improvement direction across domains. HPPO aligns the generated query distribution with the true online objective by labeling a hybrid candidate pool with the online intersection metric and applying reference-anchored preference optimization. A Pareto pair filter keeps only winner pairs that do not lower same-pattern purity, raising the attribute metric without sacrificing the pattern. Across four attribute domains under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT improves the intersection metric over a pretrained generative retriever, and HPPO improves it further, with significant gains on seven of eight domain-split cells and a marginal tie on the hardest split. Metric-predictor validation, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons, and a candidate-policy ablation show where the gains come from.

AI.tldr: A framework for pattern-preserving attribute retrieval where MO-DiT+HPPO uses continuous generative retrieval with staged training to find items that both match a target attribute and preserve fine-grained patterns from seed sets.
AI.motivation: In production retrieval settings, standard embedding-based retrieval that just returns highest-scoring items is insufficient. There's a need to find items that both satisfy a target attribute AND stay within a fine-grained pattern defined by seed sets, avoiding either averaging seeds (low attribute) or global attribute retrieval (unrelated patterns).
AI.method: Continuous generative retrieval where a model reads item embeddings and generates query embeddings for nearest-neighbor search. MO-DiT+HPPO framework has four stages: 1) raw-sequence pretraining, 2) multi-domain metric-ordered continuation pretraining (creating trajectories from low to high predicted attribute density), 3) tail-centroid fine-tuning, and 4) HPPO (hybrid preference optimization) aligning generated queries with online objectives using Pareto pair filtering.
AI.result: Across four attribute domains under item- and pattern-holdout protocols, metric-ordered DiT improves the intersection metric over pretrained generative retriever, and HPPO provides further improvements. Significant gains on seven of eight domain-split cells, with marginal tie on the hardest split. Validation through metric-predictor, order ablations, CPT/SFT comparisons show sources of gains.
AI.conclusion: The proposed MO-DiT+HPPO framework effectively addresses the pattern-preserving attribute retrieval problem by combining staged training, metric-ordered learning of improvement directions, and preference optimization with Pareto filtering, successfully balancing attribute satisfaction with pattern preservation.

title: PersistentKV: Page-Aware Decode Scheduling for Long-Context LLM Serving on Commodity GPUs
summary: Autoregressive large language model (LLM) serving is increasingly limited by key-value (KV) cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Modern paged-attention systems reduce KV-cache fragmentation and mature kernels such as FlashInfer provide highly optimized native-paged decode attention. However, the best single-kernel implementation is not always the best serving schedule: low-active long-context decode can under-utilize commodity GPUs, while mixed sequence lengths introduce a tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches. We present PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine and page-aware scheduling study for grouped-query attention (GQA). PersistentKV maps work by KV-head group, is designed to reuse K,V tiles across grouped query heads, supports native page tables, and adds a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks. On an RTX 3060 with FP16, page size 16, Hq=32, Hkv=8, d=128, and identical correctness tolerance against FlashInfer, a calibrated adaptive policy selects FlashInfer for small active batches, PersistentKV sequence splitting for B1 long-context steps, and PersistentKV workqueue scheduling for B8 long-context steps. With thresholds and split counts fixed on calibration traces, one held-out trace seed improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal, uniform, and Zipf-like workloads and by 1.399x on a B1 bucketed trace. On the B4 bimodal boundary case, the policy avoids the PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. These results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling and show that work assignment, not only attention math, is a decisive serving-system variable.

AI.tldr: PersistentKV introduces a native block-table decode attention engine with page-aware scheduling for GQA that adaptively chooses between existing FlashInfer kernels and new scheduling approaches to improve KV cache movement efficiency in LLM serving.
AI.motivation: Autoregressive LLM serving is increasingly bottlenecked by KV cache movement rather than dense matrix multiplication. Existing paged-attention systems still face challenges: low-active long-context decode underutilizes GPUs, and mixed sequence lengths create tension between many exact-length launches and coarse padded batches, necessitating better scheduling approaches.
AI.method:
1. Developed PersistentKV, a native block-table decode attention engine for GQA that maps work by KV-head group and reuses K,V tiles across grouped query heads.
2. Designed native page table support and added a compact workqueue schedule that executes only non-empty row-KV-head-sequence-split tasks.
3. Created an adaptive policy that selects between FlashInfer kernels and PersistentKV scheduling based on workload characteristics (batch size, context length).
AI.result: On RTX 3060 with FP16: Adaptive policy improves synchronized wall throughput by 1.063-1.265x on B8 bimodal/uniform/Zipf-like workloads, and by 1.399x on B1 bucketed trace. On B4 bimodal boundary case, policy avoids PersistentKV regression by selecting FlashInfer. PersistentKV workqueue scheduling excels for B8 long-context steps while sequence splitting works best for B1 long-context steps.
AI.conclusion: Work assignment and scheduling, not just attention math optimization, are decisive factors in LLM serving systems. The results identify a concrete systems niche for adaptive page-aware decode scheduling, showing that intelligent scheduling can provide significant throughput improvements across varied workload patterns.

title: Learning to Recover Task Experts from a Multi-Task Merged Model
summary: Multi-task model merging aims to consolidate several task-specific experts into a unified model, yet static merging consistently suffers from parameter interference. While dynamic merging models aim to bridge this gap, many works rely on the costly storage and loading of redundant expert components at inference. In this work, from the perspective of task expert, we view parameter interference as parameter perturbation introduced to each expert during merging process. We show that such parameter perturbations can be modeled as affine transformation, which can be approximated as additive offsets. Motivated by these, we propose Recover Task eXpert (ReTeX), a framework that predicts those offsets, in order to undo parameter interference and recover task-expert performance from a single merged checkpoint. To recover the appropriate expert when task identity is unknown, we introduce a router-free task identifier based on SVD subspace signatures computed offline before inference. At inference, the identifier selects the task whose subspace yields the smallest projection residual for a given input. As a result, ReTeX recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains, while significantly improving generalization to unseen tasks. Crucially, we also show that the parameter offset prediction leads to emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for out-of-distribution (OOD) tasks. ReTeX adaptively interpolates seen expert knowledge to handle unseen tasks. Our code is available at https://github.com/BAIKLAB/ReTeX

AI.tldr: ReTeX is a framework that predicts parameter offsets to recover task-expert performance from merged models without storing multiple experts, using subspace signatures for task identification.
AI.motivation: Static multi-task model merging suffers from parameter interference, while dynamic merging methods require costly storage and loading of multiple expert components at inference time. There's a need for an efficient approach that can recover expert performance from a single merged checkpoint.
AI.method: View parameter interference as affine transformation approximated as additive offsets. ReTeX predicts these offsets to undo interference. Uses router-free task identifier based on SVD subspace signatures computed offline to select appropriate expert when task identity is unknown.
AI.result: Recovers over 95% of individual-expert performance in both vision and NLP domains, significantly improves generalization to unseen tasks, and shows emergent adaptive interpolation of expert knowledge for OOD tasks.
AI.conclusion: ReTeX provides an efficient framework for multi-task model merging that eliminates the need for storing multiple experts while recovering expert performance, enabling adaptive knowledge interpolation for unseen tasks through parameter offset prediction.

title: Algorithmic Foundations of Deep Learning: Complexity-Theoretic Rates and a Characterization of Universal Approximation
summary:
Feedforward neural network (NN) expressivity is typically studied by emulating optimal basis-expansion schemes. While powerful, this perspective is incomplete: it primarily captures complexity through regularity, and therefore does not distinguish intuitively simple and complicated objects with comparable regularity, such as the square-root function and a typical Brownian path.
  The guiding message is that neural networks should be viewed not only as flexible basis functions, but also as models of computation. If a function is computable by a real-valued circuit over a prescribed elementary gate language, then it can be computed to comparable accuracy by an NN with explicit depth, width, and non-zero-parameter bounds controlled by the depth, width, gate count, and gate structure. Thus, neural-network complexity is not governed by regularity alone, but also by algorithmic complexity. We then show that any definable NN model satisfying a natural parallelization condition, allowing possibly multivariate non-linearities such as attention or layer normalization, is a universal approximator if and only if it contains a non-affine nonlinearity.
  The scope of our theory is illustrated by deducing universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions, minimax-optimal approximation guarantees for Besov classes, logarithmic-error complexity for holomorphic functions, and by showing that NNs can emulate numerical algorithms such as Newton-Raphson root finding and power iteration without architecture-specific arguments. Its precision is illustrated by shortest-path computation on $k$-vertex graphs: compiling the tropical dynamic-programming circuit yields NNs with O(log(1/ε)) non-zero parameters, exponentially improving in 1/ε over the generic $O(ε^{-c k^2})$ Lipschitz-approximation scale, for a constant c>0.

AI.tldr: NN expressivity should be understood through both regularity and algorithmic complexity, showing that any definable NN model with parallelization is a universal approximator if it contains non-affine nonlinearities, with applications to various function classes and algorithms.
AI.motivation: Current understanding of neural network expressivity focuses primarily on regularity through basis expansion, which fails to distinguish between simple functions (like square-root) and complex ones (like Brownian paths) with similar regularity. There's a need to incorporate computational/algorithmic complexity into the analysis of NN expressivity.
AI.method: View NNs as models of computation, not just basis functions. Prove that functions computable by real-valued circuits can be computed by NNs with explicit depth/width bounds controlled by the circuit's structure. Show that any definable NN model with parallelization capability is a universal approximator if it contains non-affine nonlinearities.
AI.result: 1) NN complexity depends on both regularity and algorithmic complexity. 2) Universal approximation guarantees for continuous functions and various function classes. 3) Exponential improvement in parameter efficiency for specific tasks like shortest-path computation (O(log(1/ε)) vs O(ε^{-ck²})). 4) NNs can emulate numerical algorithms without architecture-specific arguments.
AI.conclusion: Neural networks should be analyzed through both regularity and computational perspectives. Their expressivity is fundamentally tied to algorithmic complexity, not just smoothness properties. This computational perspective enables better understanding of why NNs work well for structured problems and provides more precise approximation bounds.

title: Diagnosing Task Insensitivity in Language Agents
summary: Large language models can serve as capable long-horizon agents, but their out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization remains weak. We identify a key source of this failure as task insensitivity: when faced with similar but distinct tasks, models might apply patterns learned during training and fail to solve the task at hand. We show that models often continue with actions aligned with the original task even when the instruction is semantically corrupted and cannot be directly answered. We further find that, when we replace the task description in a trained prompt with another similar but distinct task, the model may still output the same action. This behavior is accompanied by a consistent training-time attention drift away from task tokens and toward local observations, suggesting an optimization bias toward shortcuts. To mitigate this problem, we propose Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that explicitly encourages action dependence on the task instruction. Extensive evaluations show that our intervention improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving more stable attention to task tokens.

AI.tldr: This paper identifies task insensitivity as a key failure mode in LLM agents' OOD generalization, proposes a contrastive regularization method called Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization to improve task sensitivity, and shows it enhances attention to task tokens and OOD performance.
AI.motivation: Large language models struggle with out-of-distribution (OOD) generalization when deployed as long-horizon agents. The authors identify task insensitivity as a core problem - models apply patterns learned during training even when faced with semantically corrupted instructions or different but similar tasks, failing to adapt properly.
AI.method: The authors propose Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization, a lightweight contrastive regularizer that explicitly encourages action dependence on task instructions. This method aims to mitigate optimization biases that cause models to drift attention away from task tokens toward local observations.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations show the proposed intervention improves task sensitivity and OOD generalization while preserving more stable attention to task tokens. Models become less likely to apply inappropriate patterns from training when faced with similar but distinct tasks.
AI.conclusion: Task insensitivity is a key limitation in LLM agents' OOD generalization, but can be mitigated through contrastive regularization techniques like Task-Perturbed NLL Optimization that explicitly strengthen the connection between task instructions and model actions.

title: HyperDFlash: MHC-Aligned Block Speculative Decoding with Gated Residual Reduction
summary: We present HyperDFlash, a block-parallel speculative decoding framework tailored to the novel multi-hyper-connection (MHC) architecture proposed by DeepSeek-V4. Despite the strong initial-token drafting performance of the native Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) module in DeepSeek-V4, its draft accuracy degrades sharply at later positions, as error accumulation from unverified intermediate tokens harms acceptance rates. Although the original DFlash method supports efficient one-pass block drafting, it cannot be seamlessly adapted to the MHC paradigm, since the multi-path residual stream of DeepSeek-V4 induces feature misalignment with conventional drafting designs. To resolve this mismatch, we propose two model-aligned optimizations for MHC residual streams. First, we adopt pre-collapse residual states as the exclusive conditioning signal, preserving multi-path structural information and aligning the drafter with the native prediction pathway of the target model. Second, we replace the heavy generic linear compressor with a lightweight gated residual reducer, whose parameters are inherited from the built-in hyper-connection head. This design yields input-aware path aggregation with three orders of magnitude fewer parameters while maintaining architectural alignment. We further enhance training via a targeted KL distillation loss applied to the LM-head, which regularizes predictions against the full target probability distribution and improves draft quality at early training stages. Experiments across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks show that HyperDFlash consistently outperforms both the native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation. It achieves substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup, validating the effectiveness of MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation for high-performance speculative decoding.

AI.tldr: HyperDFlash is a block-parallel speculative decoding framework optimized for DeepSeek-V4's multi-hyper-connection architecture, using MHC-aligned residual streams and gated reduction to outperform existing approaches.
AI.motivation: The native Multi-Token Prediction (MTP) in DeepSeek-V4 suffers from draft accuracy degradation at later positions due to error accumulation, and standard DFlash can't adapt to the MHC architecture's multi-path residual streams, causing feature misalignment.
AI.method: Two model-aligned optimizations: 1) Use pre-collapse residual states as exclusive conditioning signal to preserve multi-path information, 2) Replace heavy linear compressor with lightweight gated residual reducer inherited from built-in hyper-connection head. Also employs targeted KL distillation loss for training.
AI.result: HyperDFlash consistently outperforms both native MTP baseline and vanilla DFlash adaptation across math reasoning, code synthesis, and conversational benchmarks, achieving substantial gains in average accepted draft length and decoding speedup.
AI.conclusion: The MHC alignment, gated reduction, and targeted distillation components are effective for high-performance speculative decoding in DeepSeek-V4's multi-hyper-connection architecture, solving the feature misalignment problem while maintaining architectural consistency.

title: Where Do CoT Training Gains Land in LLM based Agents?
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning is widely used in language-model agents, but prior work has shown that verbalized CoT is not always faithful and may instead reflect post-hoc reasoning, which means the model already knows the answer before reasoning. We therefore ask what CoT training is actually improving: is the model getting better at changing its action through generated reasoning, or is it getting better at predicting the action directly from the prompt? We study this question by comparing \emph{prompt actions} (predicting action without CoT) with CoT actions (predicting action with CoT). Across checkpoints, prompt-action quality improves substantially. While interacting with the environment, the relative advantage of CoT actions over prompt actions remains similar, showing that CoT training does not widen the advantage of CoT reasoning, and it helps to improve the quality of prompt actions. We further find that later checkpoints are less likely to revise the action in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on the prompt. Motivated by these patterns, we selectively mask action-token supervision on a fraction of training examples. This intervention improves out-of-domain generalization.

AI.tldr: CoT training mainly improves direct action prediction from prompts rather than enhancing reasoning-driven action changes, and masking action-token supervision improves generalization.
AI.motivation: There's concern that Chain-of-Thought reasoning may not be faithful and could reflect post-hoc reasoning. The researchers want to understand what CoT training actually improves: whether models get better at changing actions through reasoning or better at predicting actions directly from prompts.
AI.method: The researchers compare prompt actions (predicting actions without CoT) with CoT actions (predicting actions with CoT) across different model checkpoints. They study how action quality improves and whether CoT training widens the advantage of CoT reasoning. They also examine revision likelihood and implement an intervention: selectively masking action-token supervision on some training examples.
AI.result: 1) Prompt-action quality improves substantially across checkpoints. 2) The relative advantage of CoT actions over prompt actions remains similar during environment interaction, showing CoT training doesn't widen the CoT reasoning advantage but helps improve prompt actions. 3) Later checkpoints are less likely to revise actions in response to CoT, suggesting greater reliance on prompts. 4) The intervention of masking action-token supervision improves out-of-domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: CoT training primarily enhances models' ability to predict actions directly from prompts rather than improving reasoning-driven action changes. The finding that selective masking of action-token supervision improves generalization suggests promising directions for more effective training methods that prevent over-reliance on superficial prompt patterns.

title: Structure Before Collapse: Transient semantic geometry in next-token prediction
summary: Neural Collapse predicts that balanced one-hot classification pushes model representations to be equally far from each other; a symmetric configuration that depends only on the output label and ignores any semantic similarity in the inputs. This creates a puzzle: next-token prediction language models are trained predominantly (as context length increases) with one-hot labels: the same context is very unlikely to appear twice in training with different labels. However, they clearly learn latent structural features. That is, despite the one-hot training regime, a language model's contextual embeddings represent the fact that the next word in ''Mary broke the ___'' is likely to be filled by tokens in the latent classes of a) medium-sized, b) rigid, c) inanimate nouns. How does gradient descent find such categorical semantic structure when co-occurrence statistics collapse to one-hot sparsity, eliminating any shared next-tokens among different contexts? To investigate this tension we identify three synthetic controlled settings where inputs have latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels. We find that semantic geometry emerges early in training, and that representations cluster by shared attributes despite receiving no explicit supervision to do so. This structure is transient: with sufficient capacity and time, the model eventually reaches the predicted symmetric state where all representations are equally separated. We study this phase transition through Gram matrix analysis and propose a preliminary modification to the commonly used unconstrained features model to capture the emergent semantic geometry.

AI.tldr: Language models develop latent semantic structure early in training despite one-hot labels, but this structure is transient and eventually collapses to symmetric neural collapse.
AI.motivation: To resolve the tension between neural collapse (which predicts symmetric representations for one-hot classification) and the observation that language models learn meaningful semantic structure despite one-hot training labels.
AI.method: Created three synthetic controlled settings where inputs have latent semantic factors but are mapped to distinct one-hot labels. Analyzed training dynamics, studied phase transitions through Gram matrix analysis, and proposed a modified unconstrained features model.
AI.result: Semantic geometry emerges early in training, with representations clustering by shared attributes without supervision. However, this structure is transient - with sufficient capacity and training time, models eventually reach the symmetric neural collapse state where all representations are equally separated.
AI.conclusion: Gradient descent can find categorical semantic structure even in one-hot settings, but this emerges transiently before collapsing to the theoretically predicted symmetric state. The work suggests modifications to existing models to capture emergent semantic geometry.

title: Look-Before-Move: Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention in Dynamic 3D Story Worlds
summary: As embodied AI and world models increasingly operate in dynamic 3D environments, visual perception must move beyond passively interpreting given observations toward actively deciding what to observe. We study this problem through camera planning in dynamic 3D story worlds, where the camera must not only generate smooth motion, but also decide what visual evidence should be acquired before it moves. We formulate this capability as Narrative-Grounded World Visual Attention, where the camera acts as an embodied observer that determines what to observe, how to compose the observation, and how to shift attention over time under narrative intent and physical 3D constraints. To realize this capability, we propose Look-Before-Move, a camera planning framework that separates observation specification from motion execution. It first builds a Semantic Observation Contract to convert directorial intent into executable visual constraints, then performs Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to find narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints, and finally applies Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect selected viewpoints into continuous, collision-aware, and temporally coherent camera motion. We further construct a dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender, covering 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots with animated characters, semantic scene configurations, and executable 3D environments. Experiments show that our framework improves subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines, demonstrating the importance of organizing visual attention before generating camera motion.

AI.tldr: A framework called Look-Before-Move separates camera planning into observation specification then motion execution for narrative-grounded visual attention in dynamic 3D story worlds.
AI.motivation: As embodied AI and world models operate in dynamic 3D environments, visual perception needs to evolve from passive interpretation to active decision-making about what to observe. Current approaches don't adequately address the problem of camera planning where the camera must decide what visual evidence to acquire before moving, especially under narrative intent and physical 3D constraints.
AI.method: Proposes Look-Before-Move framework with three components: 1) Semantic Observation Contract to convert directorial intent into executable visual constraints, 2) Monte Carlo Viewpoint Search to find narrative-compliant and geometrically feasible viewpoints, 3) Semantic Trajectory Grounding to connect selected viewpoints into continuous, collision-aware, and temporally coherent camera motion.
AI.result: The framework was evaluated on a new dynamic 3D Story World Benchmark based on StoryBlender with 50 stories, 457 scenes, and 1585 shots. Experiments show improvements in subject perception, intent consistency, and trajectory quality over representative baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates the importance of organizing visual attention before generating camera motion. Separating observation specification from motion execution enables better narrative-grounded world visual attention in dynamic 3D environments.

title: Batch-Invariant Spectral Intelligence for Robust and Explainable Insect Authentication
comment: 20 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables (excluding supplementary materials, submitted to journal
summary: Edible insects offer an efficient source of alternative protein, requiring less land, water and emitting less greenhouse gas than conventional livestock. However, their successful integration into the food supply chain demands reliable species authentication to control allergen exposure, prevent adulteration, and meet regulatory standards. Near-infrared spectroscopy provides a rapid analytical tool, but its performance drops when applied to production batches unseen during training due to batch-to-batch variation in spectral measurements. We introduce the Batch-Invariant Spectral Network (BISN), an end-to-end framework that combines a learnable preprocessing module, initialised with Savitzky-Golay filtering, with an entropy-regularised adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific spectral variation. In contrast to Domain-Adversarial Neural Networks, which enforce domain adaptation only after feature extraction, BISN suppress batch-effects before species-specific features are learned. Using 2,700 spectra from three species (Acheta domesticus, Hermetia illucens, and Tenebrio molitor) collected across three independent production batches, BISN achieves a mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy of 0.93 (standard deviation 0.04), outperforming the strongest baseline by four percent. Further insights gained by using explainable AI confirm that model decisions consistently rely on the lipid and protein absorption regions across all folds, connecting predictive performance to known insect biochemistry. BISN addresses both cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication under realistic industrial conditions. The source code and dataset are publicly available at https://github.com/majharB/bisn.

AI.tldr: BISN framework combines learnable preprocessing with adversarial training to achieve robust insect species authentication across different production batches using NIR spectroscopy.
AI.motivation: Edible insects are efficient protein sources but need reliable species authentication for allergen control, adulteration prevention, and regulatory compliance. Current NIR spectroscopy methods struggle with batch-to-batch variations that degrade performance on unseen production batches.
AI.method: Batch-Invariant Spectral Network (BISN): an end-to-end framework combining learnable preprocessing (initialized with Savitzky-Golay filtering) with entropy-regularized adversarial objective to suppress batch-specific spectral variation before species features are learned.
AI.result: BISN achieves mean leave-one-batch-out accuracy of 0.93 (SD 0.04) on 2,700 spectra from three insect species across three production batches, outperforming strongest baseline by 4%. Explainable AI shows model decisions rely consistently on lipid and protein absorption regions.
AI.conclusion: BISN addresses cross-batch robustness and biochemical interpretability for automated insect species authentication under realistic industrial conditions, providing both reliable performance and connections to known insect biochemistry.

title: Einstein World Models
summary: Does intelligence require the ability to reason about phenomena beyond direct experience? It is natural to suspect that some complex thought cannot be captured through language alone. However, of particular concern to this work, is whether visualising counterfactual events can complement language as a mechanism for complex thought. We ask whether LLMs can be trained to utilise such visualisation mechanisms, in a way that benefits their reasoning abilities. Motivated by this question, we propose Einstein World Models. EWMs are a blueprint for LLM-based reasoning systems that place visual-temporal rollouts inside the reasoning trace, allowing them to reason in ways that text alone may not support well. In an EWM, the LLM calls a world-module (not to be confused with a world model), to produce short rollouts of scenes under consideration. The returned rollout is treated not as the answer, but as an inspectable hypothesis that can support later reasoning. Einstein World Models extend the capability of LLMs for tool calling (such as web search or code execution), into the domain of visual thought experiments.

AI.tldr: Einstein World Models are LLM-based systems that incorporate visual-temporal rollouts to enhance reasoning beyond language capabilities, treating visualizations as inspectable hypotheses rather than final answers.
AI.motivation: The paper investigates whether intelligence requires reasoning beyond direct experience and explores whether visualizing counterfactual events can complement language for complex thought. The authors question whether LLMs can be trained to use such visualization mechanisms to benefit their reasoning abilities, addressing limitations of text-only reasoning.
AI.method: The authors propose Einstein World Models (EWMs) - a blueprint for LLM-based reasoning systems that incorporate visual-temporal rollouts within reasoning traces. In EWMs, LLMs call a "world-module" to produce short rollouts of scenes under consideration, treating these visualizations as inspectable hypotheses that support subsequent reasoning steps.
AI.result: The paper introduces a novel architecture that extends LLM tool-calling capabilities into the domain of visual thought experiments, enabling models to reason about phenomena beyond direct experience through visualization mechanisms integrated with language processing.
AI.conclusion: EWMs represent a significant advancement in AI reasoning systems by bridging language and visual-spatial reasoning, allowing LLMs to conduct thought experiments and reason about counterfactual scenarios in ways that pure text-based approaches cannot achieve effectively.

title: Escaping Iterative Parameter-Space Noise: Differentially Private Learning with a Hypernetwork
summary: Differentially private (DP) training of neural networks is often hindered by the large amount of noise required by gradient-based methods such as DP-SGD, which repeatedly inject high-dimensional noise in parameter space throughout training. In this paper, we propose a new framework for DP learning that avoids iterative optimization in parameter space. Instead of updating the target model using privatized gradients, we employ a hypernetwork trained on public datasets to map a private dataset to the parameters of the target model. Specifically, each example is embedded into a low-dimensional representation, the embeddings are aggregated and perturbed to obtain a DP dataset embedding, and the hypernetwork generates the target model parameters from this noisy embedding. Because privacy noise is injected only once into a low-dimensional dataset representation, our approach can significantly reduce the adverse effect of noise. We theoretically show in a synthetic setting that, under a fixed privacy budget, models produced by our approach achieve higher utility than those trained with DP-SGD. Moreover, we apply our approach to LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion models and show that it achieves lower FID than LoRA models trained with DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new differentially private learning framework that uses hypernetworks to map private datasets to model parameters via low-dimensional embeddings, avoiding iterative noisy gradient updates and achieving better utility than DP-SGD.
AI.motivation: DP-SGD suffers from excessive noise injection in high-dimensional parameter space during iterative training, which degrades model utility. The authors aim to develop a more efficient DP learning approach that reduces the adverse effects of privacy noise.
AI.method: Instead of iterative optimization with noisy gradients, the framework: 1) embeds each private example into low-dimensional representations, 2) aggregates and perturbs embeddings once to get DP dataset embedding, 3) uses a hypernetwork (trained on public data) to generate target model parameters from the noisy embedding.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis in synthetic settings shows models from this approach achieve higher utility than DP-SGD under fixed privacy budget. Empirical results on LoRA fine-tuning of diffusion models demonstrate lower FID scores compared to DP-SGD and other public-data-guided methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hypernetwork-based framework provides a more efficient alternative to DP-SGD by injecting privacy noise only once into low-dimensional dataset embeddings, significantly reducing noise impact while maintaining differential privacy guarantees and achieving superior model utility.

title: Adaptive Utility driven Resource Orchestration for Resilient AI (AURORA-AI)
comment: Accepted at IEEE Research and Technologies for Society and Industry 2026 conference
summary: Modern AI systems are increasingly deployed under non-stationary computational, demographic, and operational conditions in which static resource allocation strategies degrade both predictive performance and human-centric properties such as fairness and explainability. This paper presents AURORA-AI, an Adaptive Utility-driven Resource Orchestration framework for Resilient AI that unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and a fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy.The framework continuously redistributes computational budget across a population of heterogeneous AI models so that the global utility, defined jointly over predictive performance, demographic parity, cost, latency, robustness, and interpretability, remains maximised under disruption. The framework is evaluated in a stress-rich discrete-time simulation that concurrently injects demographic bias shocks, gradual concept drift, and abrupt black-swan disruptions, and is compared against five established controllers including Static, Round Robin, Greedy, LinUCB, and a deep reinforcement-learning agent based on Proximal Policy Optimisation. AURORA-AI achieves immediate recovery from the black-swan event compared to eighty-eight time steps for the Static baseline and twenty-two for Proximal Policy Optimisation, lifts the alpha-quantile and the super-quantile by twenty-nine and twenty-five percent respectively, simultaneously reduces the mean and maximum demographic parity gap, and increases the fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps. These results indicate that fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

AI.tldr: AURORA-AI is an adaptive resource orchestration framework for resilient AI that optimizes computational budget allocation across heterogeneous models to maintain high global utility under disruptions, outperforming five established controllers.
AI.motivation: Modern AI systems face non-stationary conditions (computational, demographic, operational) where static resource allocation degrades both predictive performance and human-centric properties like fairness and explainability.
AI.method: Unifies Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman feedback control, Lyapunov-based stability monitoring, and fairness-aware composite utility into a single closed-loop policy. Continuously redistributes computational budget across heterogeneous AI models.
AI.result: Achieved immediate recovery from black-swan event (vs. 88 steps for Static baseline, 22 for PPO), improved alpha-quantile and super-quantile by 29% and 25%, reduced mean and maximum demographic parity gap, increased fraction of Lyapunov-stable operating steps.
AI.conclusion: Fairness-aware adaptive orchestration grounded in stability theory is a practical and theoretically motivated path toward resilient human-centric AI deployment.

title: Reproducibility Study of "AlphaEdit: Null-Space Constrained Knowledge Editing for Language Models"
summary: Fang et al. (2025) introduced a null-space constrained projection, named AlphaEdit, for locate-then-edit knowledge editing methods, theoretically guaranteeing that edits do not disrupt previously preserved knowledge, and reports substantial gains over existing editing methods on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, and GPT-J. In this work, we present a reproducibility study of AlphaEdit, reproducing its reported results under the original experimental setup and extending the evaluation along three axes: new model architectures, additional downstream benchmarks, and substantially longer sequential editing horizons. We successfully reproduce AlphaEdit's reported metrics across the original models, though we identify a discrepancy in the reported fluency and consistency metric. Extending AlphaEdit to newer model families, we find that its advantage does not generalize uniformly, which we trace to architectural assumptions in the locate-then-edit paradigm that are violated by these newer models. We further stress-test AlphaEdit's central sequential-editing claim by extending the number of edits well beyond those evaluated in the original paper, and find that performance, which is stable at the originally reported scale, degrades as edits reach a much higher count, indicating that the null-space projection's protection against catastrophic forgetting is bounded rather than unconditional. Finally, we extend evaluation of edited models on three extra benchmarks, namely, BoolQ, HellaSwag, and XSTest, and we find that large-scale sequential editing degrades both general downstream task competence and safety-relevant refusal behavior. Our results confirm that AlphaEdit performs as reported within its original scope, while showing that its core theoretical guarantees are sensitive to model architecture and editing scale in ways that have practical implications for its deployment.

AI.tldr: A reproducibility study of AlphaEdit (a knowledge editing method) confirming its original results but revealing limitations in generalization to new architectures and scaling to many sequential edits.
AI.motivation: To verify and extend the evaluation of AlphaEdit, a recently proposed knowledge editing method that claims to preserve previously learned knowledge through null-space constrained projection.
AI.method: Reproduced AlphaEdit's original experiments on LLaMA3, GPT2-XL, and GPT-J, then extended evaluation along three axes: testing on new model architectures, additional downstream benchmarks (BoolQ, HellaSwag, XSTest), and substantially longer sequential editing horizons.
AI.result: Successfully reproduced AlphaEdit's reported metrics but found a discrepancy in fluency/consistency metrics. AlphaEdit's advantages don't generalize to newer model families due to violated architectural assumptions. Performance degrades at much higher edit counts (beyond original scale), and large-scale sequential editing harms general task competence and safety-relevant behavior.
AI.conclusion: AlphaEdit performs as reported within its original scope, but its theoretical guarantees are sensitive to model architecture and editing scale, with practical implications for deployment in real-world knowledge editing scenarios.

title: AIGP: An LLM-Based Framework for Long-Term Value Alignment in E-Commerce Pricing
comment: Accepted by KDD 2026 Applied Data Science Track (Oral presentation)
summary: Traditional dynamic pricing models in large-scale e-commerce suffer from limited interpretability, poor utilization of unstructured information, and misalignment with long-term business objectives such as cumulative Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), Return on Investment (ROI) and milestone achievement. We propose AIGP, a novel framework that leverages a Large Language Model (LLM) prompted with domain knowledge, structured data and textual context to make interpretable, knowledge-aware pricing decisions. For efficient deployment while maintaining high-quality outputs, we employ supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation. Central to AIGP is the Long-Term Value Estimator (LTVE), trained via offline reinforcement learning on historical data, which serves as a reward model to score candidate pricing actions and select preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), thereby aligning the pricing policy with long-term business objectives. Extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests on Tao Factory demonstrate that AIGP achieves significant improvements: +13.21% in GMV, +7.59% in ROI, and +8.20% in milestone achievement rate over 14 days compared to the production baseline, while simultaneously providing interpretable and transparent pricing rationales.

AI.tldr: AIGP is an LLM-based dynamic pricing framework that uses domain knowledge prompting, supervised fine-tuning, and offline reinforcement learning to create interpretable pricing decisions aligned with long-term business metrics like GMV, ROI, and milestones.
AI.motivation: Traditional dynamic pricing models have three main limitations: 1) limited interpretability, 2) poor utilization of unstructured information, and 3) misalignment with long-term business objectives like cumulative GMV, ROI, and milestone achievement.
AI.method: The AIGP framework leverages an LLM prompted with domain knowledge, structured data, and textual context. It uses supervised fine-tuning for knowledge distillation to enable efficient deployment. A key component is the Long-Term Value Estimator (LTVE) trained via offline reinforcement learning on historical data, which serves as a reward model to score candidate pricing actions and select preference pairs for Direct Preference Optimization (DPO).
AI.result: Extensive offline evaluations and large-scale online A/B tests on Tao Factory show significant improvements: +13.21% in GMV, +7.59% in ROI, and +8.20% in milestone achievement rate over 14 days compared to the production baseline. The framework also provides interpretable pricing rationales.
AI.conclusion: AIGP successfully addresses the limitations of traditional dynamic pricing by combining LLMs with reinforcement learning to create an interpretable, knowledge-aware pricing system that aligns with long-term business objectives and delivers substantial performance improvements in real-world e-commerce applications.

title: How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?
comment: Preprint of a book chapter to appear: P. Fränti, "How to evaluate clustering with ground truth?", In Center-based clustering, Springer Nature, 2026
summary: External indexes can be used for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available. We review the most common external validity indexes focusing on set-matching-based measures. We recommend centroid index (CI), because it is an intuitive cluster-level measure with an explainable result. If we need a more fine-tuned, point-level measure, there are more choices. Pair-set index (PSI) provides a normalized score which is not biased by cluster sizes. If all points should matter equally, then clustering accuracy (ACC) or any other set-matching measure is suitable.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews and recommends external validity indexes for cluster evaluation when ground truth is available, with specific recommendations based on evaluation needs.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to provide guidance on selecting appropriate external indexes for cluster evaluation when ground truth labels are available, as different indexes serve different evaluation needs.
AI.method: The paper reviews common external validity indexes, focusing on set-matching-based measures, and provides specific recommendations based on the desired evaluation granularity and characteristics.
AI.result: The paper recommends centroid index (CI) as the primary cluster-level measure due to its intuitiveness and explainability, and provides alternative recommendations for point-level measures: pair-set index (PSI) for size-unbiased normalized scores, and clustering accuracy (ACC) or other set-matching measures when all points should matter equally.
AI.conclusion: Different external validity indexes serve different evaluation purposes, and selection should be based on the desired granularity (cluster-level vs point-level) and specific requirements such as bias avoidance or equal point weighting.

title: Reasoning Quality Emerges Early: Data Curation for Reasoning Models
summary: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a small, high-quality set of long reasoning traces is an effective approach for eliciting strong reasoning capabilities in Large Language Models (LLMs). However, existing methods for curating high-quality SFT data rely heavily on strong reasoning models to filter examples based on diversity and difficulty, making the curation process costly while often yielding suboptimal data quality. In this work, we show that diverse and challenging reasoning examples can be identified using only the initial reasoning tokens. Specifically, we demonstrate that difficult problems can be reliably detected based on the loss of the first 100 reasoning tokens evaluated at a randomly perturbed checkpoint of the pretrained model. We further show that examples exhibiting similar loss patterns over their first 1k reasoning tokens across a small number of perturbed checkpoints extrapolating along the fine-tuning trajectory provably induce similar gradients. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B models on the M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets. Our method outperforms existing baselines by up to 1.7% while being 91% more token efficient.

AI.tldr: Using only the initial reasoning tokens, we can identify diverse and challenging examples for supervised fine-tuning by analyzing loss patterns across perturbed checkpoints, achieving better performance with higher token efficiency.
AI.motivation: Current methods for curating high-quality SFT data rely heavily on strong reasoning models to filter examples based on diversity and difficulty, which is costly and often yields suboptimal data quality. There's a need for more efficient and effective methods to identify high-quality reasoning examples for fine-tuning.
AI.method: The method uses only initial reasoning tokens (first 100 tokens) to identify difficult problems by evaluating loss at randomly perturbed checkpoints of the pretrained model. It further analyzes loss patterns over the first 1k reasoning tokens across multiple perturbed checkpoints to group examples that induce similar gradients during fine-tuning.
AI.result: The approach outperforms existing baselines by up to 1.7% on M23K medical reasoning and OpenThoughts-Math datasets when fine-tuning Qwen2.5-7B and Llama3.1-8B models, while being 91% more token efficient (requiring fewer tokens for example selection).
AI.conclusion: Initial reasoning tokens contain sufficient information to identify diverse and challenging examples for supervised fine-tuning, enabling more efficient and effective data curation for improving LLM reasoning capabilities without relying on expensive filtering with strong reasoning models.

title: Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for Large Language Model Agents
summary: For LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments, a key challenge is to make effective use of accumulated interaction experience. Existing work has typically separated two uses of such experience: keeping it outside the model as natural-language rules for later prompting, or using trajectories and feedback to update the model parameters. The former is easy to interpret but can fall out of sync with the evolving policy; the latter improves the policy more broadly but provides only limited correction for local mistakes in sparse-reward settings. We present Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies for LLM Agents (JERP), which updates a long-term experiential-rule pool and the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, JERP retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with the interaction history. After each episode, it uses the collected trajectories both to optimize the policy and to revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories. This coupling keeps the rule pool aligned with the evolving policy while allowing stable and effective behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself. Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks.

AI.tldr: JERP (Joint Learning of Experiential Rules and Policies) is a framework that jointly updates both an experiential-rule pool and LLM agent policies from interaction trajectories, keeping rules aligned with evolving policies while enabling effective behavior transfer.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for using accumulated interaction experience in LLM agents either keep experience as external natural-language rules (which can become outdated) or use it to update model parameters (which provides limited local correction in sparse-reward settings). There's a need to combine the interpretability of rules with the broad policy improvement of parameter updates while keeping both aligned.
AI.method: JERP maintains a long-term experiential-rule pool that gets updated alongside the policy from the same interaction trajectories. At decision time, it retrieves task-relevant rules and conditions the agent on them together with interaction history. After episodes, it uses collected trajectories to both optimize the policy and revise the rule pool by comparing current rollouts with reference successful trajectories.
AI.result: Experiments on AlfWorld and WebShop show that JERP yields consistent gains in decision performance for complex interactive tasks, demonstrating the effectiveness of jointly learning rules and policies.
AI.conclusion: JERP successfully couples rule learning with policy optimization, keeping rule pools aligned with evolving policies while allowing stable behaviors to be gradually absorbed into the model itself, offering a promising approach for LLM agents in multi-step interactive environments.

title: Quantization in Federated Learning: Methods, Challenges and Future Directions
summary: Federated Learning (FL) has become a foundational paradigm for privacy-preserving distributed intelligence, yet its scalability remains fundamentally constrained by communication bottlenecks, device heterogeneity, and the challenges of training under statistically non-IID data. Quantization is one of the most effective mechanisms for mitigating these limitations, reducing both uplink/downlink payloads and on-device computation. This paper provides the first FL-centric systematic review of quantization, introducing a novel taxonomy organized around FL-specific dimensions, including client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization. Beyond cataloging existing methods, we analyze how quantization interacts with core FL behaviors such as client drift, partial participation, convergence stability, secure aggregation, and differential privacy. We further identify cross-method insights, open research gaps, and design guidelines for practitioners deploying quantized FL on mobile, IoT, and edge platforms. This survey thus establishes quantization not merely as a compression technique, but as a fundamental systems component shaping the performance, robustness, and practicality of modern FL.

AI.tldr: This paper provides the first systematic review of quantization in Federated Learning, analyzing how quantization techniques address FL-specific challenges like communication bottlenecks, device heterogeneity, and non-IID data through a novel taxonomy.
AI.motivation: Federated Learning faces scalability constraints due to communication bottlenecks, device heterogeneity, and non-IID data challenges. Quantization offers potential solutions by reducing payloads and computation costs, but there's a need for FL-specific systematic analysis.
AI.method: The paper introduces a novel FL-centric taxonomy for quantization organized around six dimensions: client heterogeneity, aggregation consistency, communication-scheduling adaptation, non-IID robustness, privacy/security integration, and hardware/energy co-optimization.
AI.result: The survey analyzes how quantization interacts with core FL behaviors (client drift, partial participation, convergence stability, secure aggregation, differential privacy), identifies cross-method insights, and establishes quantization as a fundamental systems component in FL.
AI.conclusion: Quantization is not just a compression technique but a fundamental systems component that shapes the performance, robustness, and practicality of modern Federated Learning, with important implications for mobile, IoT, and edge platforms.

title: OpenRCA 2.0: From Outcome Labels to Causal Process Supervision
summary: Root cause analysis (RCA) poses a holistic test of LLM agentic capabilities, such as long-context understanding, multi-step reasoning, and tool use. However, existing datasets suffer from a fundamental gap: they label only the root cause, not the propagation path connecting it to the observed symptom, which largely simplifies the task to naive pattern matching. To support rigorous evaluation, we introduce PAVE, a step-wise labeling protocol that leverages known interventions from fault injection to reconstruct causal propagation paths. The mechanism is forward verification: reasoning from cause to effect rather than inferring backward from symptoms. Applying PAVE yields OpenRCA 2.0 (500 instances), the first cross-system RCA benchmark with step-wise causal annotations for LLM agents. Across 11 frontier LLMs, recovering the exact root-cause set succeeds in only 20.7% of cases on average. To locate where this difficulty lies, we relax the criterion and find what we call the ungrounded diagnosis: agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, but ground that service in a verified causal propagation path to the observed symptom in only 61.5%. Outcome-only evaluation hides this failure mode; step-wise causal ground truth is the missing piece for trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents.

AI.tldr: PAVE introduces a new labeling protocol for root cause analysis that reconstructs causal propagation paths rather than just labeling root causes, and creates OpenRCA 2.0 benchmark showing LLMs struggle with causal grounding despite identifying root causes.
AI.motivation: Existing RCA datasets only label root causes, not the propagation paths connecting them to observed symptoms, simplifying the task to naive pattern matching and failing to test true causal understanding.
AI.method: PAVE (step-wise labeling protocol) uses forward verification from fault injection interventions to reconstruct causal propagation paths, creating OpenRCA 2.0 benchmark with 500 instances and step-wise causal annotations.
AI.result: Across 11 frontier LLMs, exact root-cause recovery succeeds only 20.7% on average. While agents identify at least one correct root-cause service in 76.0% of cases, they ground it in verified causal propagation paths in only 61.5%, revealing "ungrounded diagnosis" failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Step-wise causal ground truth is essential for evaluating trustworthy LLM-based RCA agents; outcome-only evaluation hides the failure mode of ungrounded diagnosis where models identify causes but cannot connect them to symptoms via proper causal paths.

title: Asymptotically Optimal Learning for Parametric Prophet Inequalities
summary: We study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards drawn from an exponential-type parametric family with an unknown parameter $θ$, a class that includes exponential, Pareto, and bounded-support power-family distributions. We first characterize the optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio for this family. In the unbounded-support case, the limit is $ {\left(θ/({θ-c_+})\right)^{c_+/θ}}/ {Γ(1-c_+/θ)},$ while in the bounded-support case, the limit is $1$. We then propose a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning. By exploiting the explicit parametric structure, the policy achieves the same optimal asymptotic competitive ratio using only online observations, without external offline samples. We further derive distribution-specific convergence rates for canonical examples. Finally, numerical experiments on synthetic instances illustrate the performance of our algorithm.

AI.tldr: Proposes an online learning algorithm for prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards from exponential-type parametric distributions that achieves optimal asymptotic competitive ratio without offline samples.
AI.motivation: Study learning in prophet inequalities with i.i.d. rewards from exponential-type parametric families (including exponential, Pareto, and bounded-support power-family distributions) where the distribution parameter θ is unknown.
AI.method: First characterizes optimal full-information asymptotic competitive ratio, then proposes a confidence-based dynamic-programming policy for online learning that exploits parametric structure to achieve optimal competitive ratio using only online observations.
AI.result: Derives optimal asymptotic competitive ratio formulas: for unbounded-support case, limit is (θ/(θ-c₊))^(c₊/θ)/Γ(1-c₊/θ); for bounded-support case, limit is 1. The proposed algorithm achieves these optimal ratios without external offline samples. Also provides distribution-specific convergence rates and numerical validation.
AI.conclusion: The confidence-based dynamic programming policy effectively learns unknown parameters online while achieving optimal asymptotic performance for prophet inequality problems with exponential-type parametric reward distributions.

title: TOPS: First-Principles Visual Token Pruning via Constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets for Efficient MLLM Inference
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have achieved strong multimodal reasoning capabilities, but their efficiency is limited by the large number of visual tokens, which introduces substantial computational overhead. Visual token pruning offers a natural solution, yet existing methods are imperfect: attention-based criteria tend to retain redundant tokens, while diversity-based criteria are often agnostic to user instructions. Even methods that combine multiple criteria still lack a principled formulation of the intrinsic objective of token pruning. In this paper, we revisit visual token pruning from a first-principles perspective and formulate it as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Through a top-down information-theoretic analysis, we identify three fundamental principles for effective token selection: Task Relevance, Information Coverage, and Semantic Diversity. Based on these principles, we propose TOPS, a training-free and model-agnostic pruning module that can be applied to various MLLMs. Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks demonstrate that TOPS outperforms prior methods under diverse pruning settings. Notably, on LLaVA-NeXT, TOPS removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% and 100.6% performance on its 7B and 13B models, respectively, suggesting that pruning redundant visual tokens can sometimes mitigate hallucination and inspire future lightweight MLLM design.

AI.tldr: TOPS is a training-free visual token pruning method for MLLMs that removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving or improving performance by applying three information-theoretic principles.
AI.motivation: Current multimodal LLMs suffer from computational inefficiency due to large numbers of visual tokens. Existing pruning methods are suboptimal: attention-based methods keep redundant tokens, diversity-based methods ignore user instructions, and combined approaches lack principled formulation.
AI.method: Revisits visual token pruning from first principles as constructing Token Optimal Preservation Sets. Uses top-down information-theoretic analysis to derive three core principles (Task Relevance, Information Coverage, Semantic Diversity). Proposes TOPS - a training-free, model-agnostic pruning module applicable to various MLLMs.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on 7 MLLM backbones and 14 benchmarks show TOPS outperforms prior methods. On LLaVA-NeXT, removes 77.8% of visual tokens while preserving 100.0% (7B) and 100.6% (13B) performance. Surprisingly, pruning redundant tokens can mitigate hallucination.
AI.conclusion: TOPS provides a principled solution for visual token pruning in MLLMs, achieving state-of-the-art efficiency-performance trade-off. The approach suggests pruning redundant visual tokens may not only reduce computation but also improve model reliability by reducing hallucination.

title: GEOALIGN: Geometric Rollout Curation for Robust LLM Reinforcement Learning
comment: Accepted as a conference paper at ICML 2026
summary: Online reinforcement learning is widely used to align large language models (LLMs) with reward signals, yet training can be unstable under noisy or misspecified rewards. We identify a failure mode we call directional inconsistency: within a batch, a small set of high-reward rollouts induces representation-space preference directions that sharply disagree with the batch majority, resulting in high-variance and destabilizing updates. We propose geoalign, a lightweight plug-in for rollout curation in iterative policy optimization. Geoalign (i) forms within-prompt preference pairs, (ii) learns an online projector on per-rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and (iii) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via their angular deviation from a batch consensus prototype and rectifies them with within-prompt stable alternatives. Geoalign is forward-pass only and adds negligible overhead. Across dialogue alignment with a learned reward model and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, Geoalign improves final performance and reduces training oscillation, outperforming PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO. These results suggest latent directional consensus as an effective reliability signal for online LLM RL.

AI.tldr: GeoAlign: A lightweight method to stabilize online RL for LLMs by detecting and fixing directional inconsistencies in reward signals during training.
AI.motivation: Online RL for aligning LLMs suffers from instability under noisy or misspecified rewards, with a specific failure mode called "directional inconsistency" where high-reward rollouts create conflicting preference directions that destabilize training updates.
AI.method: GeoAlign is a plug-in rollout curation method that: 1) forms within-prompt preference pairs, 2) learns an online projector on rollout hidden states to concentrate reward-ordered displacement directions, and 3) detects directionally inconsistent rollouts via angular deviation from batch consensus and replaces them with stable alternatives. It's forward-pass only with negligible overhead.
AI.result: Across dialogue alignment with learned reward models and mathematical reasoning with binary verified rewards, GeoAlign improves final performance, reduces training oscillation, and outperforms baselines including PF-PPO, PAR, PODS, and Seed-GRPO.
AI.conclusion: Latent directional consensus serves as an effective reliability signal for stabilizing online RL training of LLMs, making GeoAlign a practical solution for improving training stability and performance.

title: A Process Harness for Uplifting Legacy Workflows to Agentic BPM: Design and Realization in CUGA FLO
summary: We introduce the process harness, a new mechanism for uplifting legacy workflows into Agentic Business Process Management (Agentic BPM) without replacing the underlying workflow engine. A process harness places a policy-governed agentic layer around a deterministic workflow engine, intercepting designated control points to contribute reasoning, adaptation, and oversight while the engine retains structural authority over the process. To define the process harness rigorously, we develop the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model, specifying both its data schema and its execution semantics. TDF decomposes LLM reasoning across three policy-governed agent types: a TaskAgent for knowledge-intensive task execution, a DecisionAgent for per-case gateway routing, and a FlowAgent that governs runtime flow adaptation through a principled hook mechanism. Each agent reasons within an explicit policy drawn from the process FRAME, the aggregate policy set governing all LLM calls in the system. We then present CUGA FLO as the design and implementation realization of the TDF model, and demonstrate it on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and hook-driven regulatory override. The process harness uniquely reconciles imperative requirements, realized through deterministic workflow execution that enforces structural compliance, with normative requirements, realized through policy-framed agentic autonomy invoked at designated control points wherever the process demands it.

AI.tldr: Process Harness: A framework that adds AI agent layer on top of existing workflow engines without replacing them, enabling agentic reasoning while preserving deterministic process structure.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between traditional deterministic workflow systems (which enforce structural compliance) and modern AI-driven agentic systems (which provide adaptive reasoning), allowing organizations to uplift legacy workflows into Agentic BPM without costly engine replacements.
AI.method: Introduces the Task-Decision-Flow (TDF) model with three policy-governed agent types: TaskAgent for knowledge tasks, DecisionAgent for case routing, and FlowAgent for runtime adaptation. Agents intercept workflow control points through a principled hook mechanism while the underlying engine retains structural authority.
AI.result: Presents CUGA FLO as the design/implementation of TDF, demonstrated on a loan approval workflow that exercises all three agent types and regulatory override mechanisms, showing reconciliation of imperative and normative requirements.
AI.conclusion: The process harness successfully reconciles deterministic workflow execution (for compliance) with policy-framed agentic autonomy (for adaptation), providing a practical path for organizations to adopt Agentic BPM while preserving existing workflow investments.

title: Decision-Aligned Evaluation of Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Uncertainty estimates in machine learning are typically evaluated using generic metrics such as the negative log-likelihood and expected calibration error, yet good performance on such metrics does not necessarily imply high utility in downstream decisions. We introduce decision-alignment, a criterion that reveals which evaluation metrics meaningfully align with downstream utilities. Applying this framework, we show that many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. We then propose prior-weighted utility metrics, a special class of proper scoring rules that provides decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation. Across benchmark experiments and real-world case studies, our metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics do not. Our results surface flaws in the current UQ evaluation protocol and offer a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation.

AI.tldr: Many standard uncertainty quantification metrics don't align with downstream decision utility; new prior-weighted utility metrics provide decision-aligned evaluation that better matches real-world task performance.
AI.motivation: Current uncertainty evaluation metrics (like negative log-likelihood and expected calibration error) may perform well on generic metrics but don't necessarily translate to good utility in downstream decisions. There's a need to connect uncertainty evaluation directly to decision-making outcomes.
AI.method: Introduce 'decision-alignment' criterion to reveal which evaluation metrics meaningfully align with downstream utilities. Propose prior-weighted utility metrics as a special class of proper scoring rules that provide decision-aligned uncertainty evaluation. Apply this framework to analyze widely used uncertainty metrics and validate with benchmark experiments and real-world case studies.
AI.result: Many widely used uncertainty metrics are either misaligned with common decision problems or encode pathological prior beliefs about the downstream task. The proposed prior-weighted utility metrics consistently align with realized decision utility, while conventional metrics do not.
AI.conclusion: The current UQ evaluation protocol has flaws, and prior-weighted utility metrics offer a principled extension of existing metrics toward decision-relevant UQ evaluation, bridging the gap between uncertainty quantification and real-world decision-making.

title: Vulnerability of Natural Language Classifiers to Evolutionary Generated Adversarial Text
