summary: Deep learning models have achieved impressive performance across various fields but remain vulnerable to adversarial inputs, particularly in NLP, where such attacks can have significant real-world consequences. Adversarial attacks often involve small, semantically similar token replacements to fool NLP models, and recent methods have become more precise by targeting specific vulnerable words, often by exploiting some level of access to the model's internal structure. This paper proposes GAversary, a hybrid Genetic Algorithm (GA) to generate adversarial attacks on natural language models. The GA is able to treat the target model as a black box, requiring only the logit value output by the model to guide the search. GAversary differs from GAs previously proposed for this problem by using GloVe embeddings to propose word replacements (the mutation operator) to improve the semantic similarity of the adversarial examples. GAversary is applied to several benchmark data sets and well-known target models. GAversary is able to substantially reduce the target model's accuracy on test data compared to the BAE and A2T attacks compared against (in the best case, reducing a 76.8% accuracy to 5.8%, compared to BAE's 27.6%). The trade-off is that GAversary perturbs just under twice as many words as the other two methods, with a slightly lower semantic similarity to the original text and around a 5% increase in run-time.

AI.tldr: GAversary is a black-box adversarial attack method using Genetic Algorithm with GloVe embeddings for word replacements, significantly reducing model accuracy while maintaining semantic similarity.
AI.motivation: Deep learning NLP models are vulnerable to adversarial attacks with real-world consequences, and existing methods often require access to model internals. There's a need for effective black-box attack methods that can fool models without requiring internal knowledge.
AI.method: GAversary uses a hybrid Genetic Algorithm that treats target models as black boxes, requiring only logit outputs. It uses GloVe embeddings for the mutation operator to propose semantically similar word replacements, and evolves adversarial examples through selection, crossover, and mutation operations.
AI.result: GAversary substantially reduces model accuracy (from 76.8% to 5.8% in best case, outperforming BAE's 27.6% and A2T). However, it perturbs nearly twice as many words, has slightly lower semantic similarity, and adds ~5% runtime compared to other methods.
AI.conclusion: GAversary demonstrates that genetic algorithms with semantic embedding guidance can create effective black-box adversarial attacks on NLP models, offering a trade-off between attack success and perturbation efficiency that may be suitable for different security testing scenarios.

title: Uncertainty quantification via conformal prediction in data assimilation
comment: Submitted to Quarterly Journal of the Royal Meteorological Society
summary: Quantifying the evolution of uncertainty is critical to both probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. In this study, we investigate the applicability of conformal prediction (CP), a recent machine learning (ML) method, to quantify uncertainty in a controlled, idealized setting. We use the one dimensional modified shallow water model, designed to mimic the convective process. CP provides a set of possible outcomes with a chosen confidence level. Here, we compare and evaluate the average empirical coverage, the average interval length, miss low, miss high and average interval score loss (AISL) for three variants of CP, namely a) Standard CP, b) Normalized CP and c) Conformalized Quantile Regression. We further compare these CP-based uncertainty estimates with traditional ensemble-based measures such as standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. In addition, we investigate the integration of CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle through CP perturbations. Our results highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing insight into the effectiveness of CP to complement common ensemble-based uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the application of conformal prediction methods for uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models, comparing them with traditional ensemble-based approaches and exploring their integration into data assimilation cycles.
AI.motivation: The evolution of uncertainty is critical for probabilistic forecasting and data assimilation in numerical weather prediction. The authors want to investigate the applicability of recent machine learning methods like conformal prediction for uncertainty quantification in atmospheric modeling.
AI.method: The study uses a one-dimensional modified shallow water model designed to mimic convective processes. It compares and evaluates three variants of conformal prediction: Standard CP, Normalized CP, and Conformalized Quantile Regression. These are compared with traditional ensemble-based measures like standard deviation intervals and ensemble spread. The research also investigates integrating CP-derived uncertainty within the data assimilation cycle through CP perturbations.
AI.result: The paper evaluates performance using metrics including average empirical coverage, average interval length, miss low, miss high, and average interval score loss (AISL). Results highlight the strengths and limitations of each approach, providing insights into CP's effectiveness to complement ensemble-based uncertainty quantification.
AI.conclusion: Conformal prediction methods show promise for uncertainty quantification in simplified atmospheric models, with the study providing valuable insights into their applicability and how they can complement traditional ensemble-based approaches in numerical weather prediction.

title: Ask, Don't Judge: Binary Questions for Interpretable LLM Evaluation and Self-Improvement
comment: Acceepted to the Second Workshop on Compositional Learning at ICML 2026, Seoul, South Korea
summary: Evaluating LLM outputs remains a major bottleneck in NLP: human evaluation is expensive and slow, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments on open-ended generation, and holistic LLM judges often produce opaque scores that are hard to debug. We propose BINEVAL, a framework that decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions and aggregates the resulting verdicts into interpretable, multi-dimensional scores. Given a task prompt, a meta-prompt generates fine-grained evaluation questions, and an LLM answers them independently for each output, yielding transparent question-level feedback together with calibrated overall scores. This decomposition makes evaluation easier to inspect, easier to diagnose, and directly usable for prompt improvement. Across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines including UniEval and G-Eval, with especially strong results on factual consistency benchmarks such as QAGS. Beyond competitive correlation with human judgments, BINEVAL better matches human score distributions and avoids the ceiling effects common in prior LLM judges, leading to better discrimination between borderline and clearly flawed outputs. We further show that the same question-level feedback supports iterative prompt optimization, improving evaluator prompts on summarization and generation prompts on IFBench under both self-update and cross-model update settings. Overall, BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, and interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic and optimization value.

AI.tldr: BINEVAL: An interpretable LLM evaluation framework that decomposes criteria into atomic binary questions, providing transparent feedback and calibrated scores while matching or outperforming existing evaluation methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation faces major bottlenecks: human evaluation is expensive, lexical metrics correlate poorly with human judgments, and LLM judges produce opaque scores that are hard to debug and interpret.
AI.method: BINEVAL decomposes evaluation criteria into atomic binary questions via meta-prompting, uses LLMs to answer these questions independently for each output, and aggregates verdicts into interpretable multi-dimensional scores with transparent question-level feedback.
AI.result: BINEVAL matches or outperforms strong baselines like UniEval and G-Eval across SummEval, Topical-Chat, and QAGS, with especially strong performance on factual consistency benchmarks. It better matches human score distributions, avoids ceiling effects, and improves discrimination between borderline and flawed outputs.
AI.conclusion: BINEVAL provides a task-agnostic, training-free, interpretable evaluation framework that combines strong empirical performance with practical diagnostic value, supporting both evaluation and iterative prompt optimization.

title: A Generalization Theory for JEPA-Based World Models
summary: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have recently emerged as a promising paradigm for world modeling by learning predictive dynamics in a latent space rather than generating future observations at the input level. Despite their empirical success, the theoretical understanding of JEPA-based world models remains limited. In this paper, we develop the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models. We formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem and show that the JEPA objective is equivalent to a low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. Building on this characterization, we establish a connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret, leading to a finite-sample generalization bound for JEPA-based world models. Our analysis reveals an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to the latent dimension, providing theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared with input-level predictive approaches.

AI.tldr: This paper develops the first generalization theory for Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) world models, formulating them as conditional spectral graph learning problems and connecting pretraining error to downstream planning performance.
AI.motivation: Joint Embedding Predictive Architectures (JEPAs) have shown empirical success for world modeling by learning predictive dynamics in latent space, but lack theoretical understanding. The paper aims to establish the first generalization theory for JEPA-based world models to provide theoretical foundations for this promising paradigm.
AI.method: The authors formulate JEPA pretraining as a conditional spectral graph learning problem and show that the JEPA objective is equivalent to low-rank factorization of an action-conditioned co-occurrence matrix. They then establish a connection between JEPA pretraining error and downstream planning regret.
AI.result: The paper provides finite-sample generalization bounds for JEPA-based world models and reveals an inherent trade-off between approximation and sample errors with respect to latent dimension. The analysis offers theoretical insights into the advantages and limitations of latent predictive models compared to input-level approaches.
AI.conclusion: This work provides the first theoretical framework for understanding JEPA-based world models, offering generalization bounds and theoretical insights that explain when and why latent predictive models outperform input-level prediction approaches, advancing the theoretical foundations of world modeling.

title: EO-WM: A Physically Informed World Model for Probabilistic Earth Observation Forecasting
summary: Earth Observation (EO) forecasting aims to predict future Earth surface dynamics from satellite observations under changing meteorological conditions. In this paper, we view this task as a partially observed, weather-driven world modeling problem, in which weather acts as a conditioning signal, while forecasting remains uncertain due to sparse observations and unobserved land-surface states. However, existing methods do not fully capture this setting: deterministic models collapse uncertainty into a single future prediction, while diffusion-based methods typically treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning signals, and existing benchmarks focus mainly on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts respond correctly to changed weather forcing.We introduce EO-WM, a video diffusion transformer for multispectral EO forecasting. EO-WM incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework that represents meteorological forcing through a climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. Specifically, it separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways, and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress. To evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, we introduce two diagnostic benchmarks: an Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware prediction of vegetation degradation under extreme weather, and a Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather forcing. Experiments show that EO-WM reduces the error in predicted Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI) decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The benchmarks and model will be made open-source at https://github.com/Luo-Z13/EO-WM.

AI.tldr: EO-WM: A video diffusion transformer for Earth Observation forecasting that uses physically-informed weather conditioning to improve prediction of vegetation response to extreme weather, with new diagnostic benchmarks for weather-response evaluation.
AI.motivation: Current EO forecasting methods don't adequately handle the partial observation nature of weather-driven world modeling. Deterministic models collapse uncertainty, diffusion methods treat weather variables as undifferentiated conditioning, and existing benchmarks focus on reconstruction accuracy rather than whether forecasts correctly respond to changed weather forcing.
AI.method: EO-WM is a video diffusion transformer that incorporates a physically informed conditioning framework representing meteorological forcing through climatological baseline, weather anomalies, and cumulative physical stress signals. It separates baseline and anomaly through distinct conditioning pathways and accumulates anomalous forcing over time to capture sustained heat and drought stress.
AI.result: EO-WM reduces error in predicted NDVI decline amplitude by a relative 5.63% and improves directional hit rate by a relative 7.80%, while remaining competitive on standard pixel-level metrics. The model also introduces two diagnostic benchmarks: Extreme Summer Benchmark for severity-aware vegetation degradation prediction and Seasonal Matched-Pair Benchmark for testing response fidelity under changed weather.
AI.conclusion: EO-WM advances EO forecasting by better modeling weather-driven world dynamics with a physically-informed conditioning approach and introduces new diagnostic benchmarks to evaluate weather-response behavior beyond standard metrics, with the model and benchmarks being open-sourced for community use.

title: Just how sure are you? Improving Verbalized Uncertainty Calibration in Medical VQA
summary:
Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) applied to Medical Visual Question Answering (VQA) tend to produce overconfident outputs regardless of actual correctness, and existing verbalized confidence calibration methods, developed primarily for text only LLMs, do not account for the multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
  This work proposes a training based framework that finetunes MLLMs to improve their calibration using a composite loss function combining a Brier style calibration term, an anchor regularizer that prevents confidence collapse toward extreme values, a contrastive image text alignment term, and a KL based model stabilization term. The alignment signal is derived from a $2 \times 2$ factorial perturbation design that crosses image presence with text integrity, probing the reliance of the model on visual modality input versus language priors. Finally, a top K KL divergence regularizer is used to protect the answering ability of the model during finetuning.
  Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), our method reduces calibration error by 60% or more, and improves discrimination by 26% or more, while preserving predictive accuracy. On average across benchmarks, the technique outperforms prompting based, sampling based, and training based approaches, and ablation experiments confirm that each component of the loss function is indeed necessary for improving the calibration. All code for the experiments is publicly available.

AI.tldr: A training framework that finetunes multimodal LLMs to improve calibration in medical VQA using a composite loss function with calibration terms, regularizers, and alignment signals derived from image-text perturbation experiments.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs in medical VQA produce overconfident outputs regardless of correctness, and existing calibration methods developed for text-only LLMs don't account for multimodal nature of medical image understanding.
AI.method: Proposes training-based framework with composite loss function: Brier style calibration term, anchor regularizer to prevent confidence collapse, contrastive image-text alignment term, and KL-based model stabilization term. Alignment signal comes from 2x2 factorial perturbation design crossing image presence with text integrity. Top K KL divergence regularizer protects answering ability during finetuning.
AI.result: Across three Medical VQA benchmarks and two architectures (MedGemma 4B IT and Qwen2 VL 7B Instruct), reduces calibration error by 60%+ and improves discrimination by 26%+, while preserving predictive accuracy. Outperforms prompting-based, sampling-based, and training-based approaches; ablation confirms each loss component is necessary.
AI.conclusion: The proposed training framework effectively addresses calibration issues in medical multimodal LLMs through a carefully designed composite loss function that balances calibration improvement with model preservation, with all code publicly available for reproducibility.

title: Simulation-based inference for rapid Bayesian parameter estimation in epidemiological models: a comparison with MCMC
summary: Mechanistic epidemiological models are widely used to support infectious disease forecasting and public-health decision making. Bayesian calibration of such models is commonly performed using Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC), which can become computationally expensive for high-dimensional nonlinear systems and repeated near-real-time analyses. Here, we investigate simulation-based inference (SBI) using neural posterior estimation as a scalable alternative for Bayesian calibration of a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 intensive care unit (ICU) occupancy data from Germany during 2020. We compared SBI and MCMC across multiple epidemic phases using both 31-day inference windows and a substantially more challenging 201-day reconstruction problem involving multiple transmission change points. Posterior agreement was evaluated quantitatively using Wasserstein distances and Kullback-Leibler divergences together with posterior predictive checks. Across the 31-day windows, SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC while accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. In the 201-day setting, SBI preserved the dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI, by combining CPU and GPU resources, substantially reduced computational runtime compared with MCMC, which was restricted to running on CPUs. Whereas MCMC required approximately 1000 seconds for the 31-day inference problems, SBI achieved comparable posterior and predictive performance in approximately 60-70 seconds on a single GPU. For the 201-day inference problem, SBI required an average of 157 seconds, while the MCMC runs took over 19,000 seconds. Our results demonstrate that SBI provides a rapid and computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis.

AI.tldr: SBI with neural posterior estimation provides fast, efficient Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, achieving comparable results to MCMC but with 16-120x speedup.
AI.motivation: Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models using MCMC is computationally expensive for high-dimensional systems and repeated near-real-time analyses, creating a need for scalable alternatives.
AI.method: Used simulation-based inference (SBI) with neural posterior estimation to calibrate a mechanistic SECIR epidemiological model using COVID-19 ICU occupancy data from Germany. Compared SBI against MCMC across multiple epidemic phases with 31-day windows and a challenging 201-day reconstruction problem.
AI.result: SBI recovered posterior distributions in strong agreement with MCMC for 31-day windows, accurately reproducing observed ICU trajectories. For 201-day setting, SBI preserved dominant posterior structure despite increased uncertainty. SBI achieved 16-120x speedup: 60-70 seconds vs 1000 seconds for 31-day problems, and 157 seconds vs 19,000+ seconds for 201-day problems.
AI.conclusion: SBI provides a rapid, computationally efficient framework for Bayesian calibration of mechanistic epidemiological models, supporting repeated near-real-time inference and rapid outbreak analysis as a practical alternative to MCMC.

title: Symplectic Neural Networks for learning Generalized Hamiltonians
summary: Hamiltonian Neural Networks (HNNs) integrate physical priors into neural models by learning a system's Hamiltonian, improving generalization and sample efficiency. Identifying the system Hamiltonian from noisy observations of state variables is a challenging task. For simulations to faithfully reflect the long-term behavior of Hamiltonian systems, especially energy conservation, it is essential to use symplectic integrators, which preserve the system's geometric structure. This fidelity comes at a cost: implicit symplectic integrators are more computationally intensive and make backpropagation through the ODE solver non-trivial. However, by leveraging the fact that symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system yield the same sensitivities associated by backpropagation, we obtain an efficient method of training the Neural Network parameters. In our work, we explore this alternate method of HNN training under noisy observation of trajectories with our HNN model based on an implicit symplectic integrator. Computationally, a predictor-corrector based ODE solver and fixed point iteration help to mitigate the computational cost of the implicit timestepping, resulting in more efficient generation of gradient updates. We showcase the numerical advantage, in experiments, in system identification and energy preservation on a range of non-separable, chaotic systems and the efficient computation and memory complexity of our method. We also observe that the post-processing of the learned Hamiltonian using backward error analysis yields a modified Hamiltonian that is a more accurate approximation of the true Hamiltonian without the need to use more accurate discretizations of the flow map.

AI.tldr: HNNs trained with implicit symplectic integrators via adjoint methods handle noisy trajectory observations efficiently while preserving energy conservation in chaotic Hamiltonian systems.
AI.motivation: Hamiltonian Neural Networks need to learn from noisy observations while maintaining energy conservation, but traditional methods using implicit symplectic integrators are computationally expensive and make backpropagation through ODE solvers difficult.
AI.method: Uses symplectic discretizations of the adjoint system to get efficient gradients, combines predictor-corrector ODE solver with fixed point iteration to mitigate implicit timestepping costs, and applies backward error analysis for Hamiltonian post-processing.
AI.result: Demonstrates numerical advantages in system identification and energy preservation on non-separable chaotic systems, with efficient computation and memory complexity, plus improved Hamiltonian accuracy via backward error analysis.
AI.conclusion: The method provides an efficient way to train HNNs on noisy observations while preserving symplectic structure and energy conservation, with improved accuracy through post-processing via backward error analysis.

title: Prompt Injection in Automated Résumé Screening with Large Language Models: Single and Multi-Injection Settings
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to screen and rank job applicants, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate algorithmic hiring systems. We study prompt injection in automated résumé screening, defined as subtle self-promotional text that introduces no new qualifications but is designed to influence LLM evaluations. Using controlled experiments, we show that prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when résumé quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject. However, its effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates inject, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. When candidate quality is heterogeneous, prompt injection is less effective on average, but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns. Overall, LLM-based screening is most vulnerable when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. Code and resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/preetb1199/Prompt_Injection_ACL26

AI.tldr: Prompt injection in LLM-based resume screening: subtle self-promotional text manipulation that improves rankings when few use it, but becomes ineffective when widespread, raising fairness concerns when lower-quality candidates outrank higher-quality ones.
AI.motivation: LLMs are increasingly used for automated resume screening, creating incentives for candidates to strategically manipulate these algorithmic hiring systems. There's a need to understand how prompt injection (subtle self-promotional text) affects LLM evaluations and the fairness of hiring decisions.
AI.method: The study uses controlled experiments to examine prompt injection in automated resume screening. They test how subtle self-promotional text that doesn't add new qualifications influences LLM evaluations under different conditions: homogeneous vs. heterogeneous candidate quality, and varying prevalence of injection use among candidates.
AI.result: Prompt injection reliably improves applicant rankings when resume quality is homogeneous and few candidates inject, but effectiveness rapidly diminishes as more candidates use it, collapsing when manipulation becomes widespread. In heterogeneous quality settings, prompt injection is less effective on average but can occasionally allow lower-quality candidates to outrank higher-quality ones, raising fairness concerns.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based screening is most vulnerable to prompt injection when manipulation is rare and candidate quality differences are small. The study reveals important limitations and fairness implications of using LLMs for automated hiring decisions, highlighting the need for safeguards against such manipulation strategies.

title: State Representation Matters in Deep Reinforcement Learning: Application to Energy Trading
summary: Energy trading decisions depend not only on current market prices, but also on expected future market conditions, and operational constraints. This makes the state representation given to a reinforcement learning agent an important design choice. We study this in HydroDam, a pumped-storage arbitrage environment, using a fixed Double DQN agent. The environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol are kept fixed; only the market features are changed. We compare absolute price/calendar features, relative features that compare current prices with recent market history, forecast features, and all combinations of these three feature families. Policies are trained and selected using 2007--2011 Belgian day-ahead prices and evaluated on two test settings: a later same-market test set from 2012--2025 and 39 other ENTSO-E market zones. Absolute features only reaches 28.8% on the test set and a median 5.7% across zones. Relative-only and forecast-only states also stay below a rolling price-score heuristic in the cross-zone median. Combining feature families is much stronger: absolute + relative reaches 49.9% on the test set and a 39.8% cross-zone median, while absolute + relative + forecast reaches 55.6% and 47.5%. These results suggest that state representation is not a minor preprocessing choice in storage-trading RL, but a central part of the policy design: robust transfer requires combining price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information, rather than relying on any single feature family.

AI.tldr: A reinforcement learning study on pumped-storage arbitrage showing that combining multiple feature families (absolute prices, relative price comparisons, and forecasts) significantly improves performance and robustness across markets compared to using single feature types alone.
AI.motivation: Energy trading decisions depend on both current market prices and expected future conditions, making state representation in reinforcement learning agents a critical design choice that needs systematic investigation for robust performance across different markets.
AI.method: Used HydroDam (pumped-storage arbitrage environment) with fixed Double DQN agent, keeping environment, action space, reward function, network, and training protocol constant while varying only market features. Compared absolute price/calendar features, relative features comparing current prices with recent history, forecast features, and all combinations.
AI.result: Absolute features alone reached only 28.8% on test set (5.7% median across zones). Relative-only and forecast-only stayed below rolling price-score heuristic. Combining features was much stronger: absolute+relative reached 49.9% (39.8% cross-zone median), while absolute+relative+forecast reached 55.6% (47.5% median).
AI.conclusion: State representation is not a minor preprocessing choice but a central part of policy design in storage-trading RL. Robust transfer across markets requires combining price scale, recent relative price context, and short-horizon forecast information rather than relying on any single feature family.

title: When Does Combining Language Models Help? A Co-Failure Ceiling on Routing, Voting, and Mixture-of-Agents Across 67 Frontier Models
summary:
Multi-model LLM systems such as routing, voting, cascades, fusion, and mixture-of-agents are used to beat single-model accuracy. We show that their gain is capped by a quantity the field rarely reports. For any policy whose output is one member model answer, accuracy cannot exceed one minus beta, where beta is the rate at which every model is wrong on the same query. In contrast, the usual diagnostic, average pairwise error correlation rho, cannot identify beta: error laws with identical marginals and pairwise correlations can have different all-wrong rates. A Clopper-Pearson bound on beta gives a finite-sample certificate on the largest gain any router, vote, or cascade could deliver before training a router.
  Across 67 models from 21 providers, a tetrachoric-calibrated single-factor model still underprices the all-wrong tail: on open-ended mathematics, observed beta is 0.052 versus 0.023 under the full 67-model Gaussian copula, about 2.5 times underpricing, with 90 percent CI 1.7 to 3.4 and k equals 17. The effect recurs on execution-graded code, where beta is 0.079. Re-asking the same GPQA-Diamond questions in free-response rather than multiple-choice form reopens the tail, with beta 0.127 and a five-judge panel with kappa 0.73 to 0.92, locating co-failure in answer format rather than subject. At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but on checkable tasks in our pool, combining models rarely beats the single best model without a strong query-level routing signal. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models.

AI.tldr: Multi-model LLM systems have accuracy gains capped by the "all-wrong rate" (beta), not just pairwise error correlations. Beta is substantially higher than predicted by Gaussian models, and gains come from models failing on different questions rather than simply adding more models.
AI.motivation: Current multi-model LLM systems (routing, voting, cascades, fusion, mixture-of-agents) aim to beat single-model accuracy, but their potential gain is poorly understood. The field rarely reports the fundamental limit on improvement.
AI.method: Theoretical analysis shows accuracy cannot exceed 1-beta where beta is the rate at which all models are wrong on the same query. Empirical analysis across 67 models from 21 providers, using Clopper-Pearson bounds and tetrachoric calibration. Experiments include open-ended mathematics, execution-graded code, and free-response vs multiple-choice formats.
AI.result: Beta is substantially higher than predicted by Gaussian copula models (0.052 vs 0.023 for math, 2.5x underpricing). On execution-graded code, beta=0.079. Free-response formats reopen the tail (beta=0.127). At matched quality, low-rho heterogeneous ensembles beat high-rho Self-MoA, but rarely beats the single best model without strong query-level routing.
AI.conclusion: The "all-wrong rate" beta, not pairwise error correlations, caps multi-model LLM system gains. The field must report beta to understand true potential. Gains come from models failing on different questions, not from adding more models. Task checkability and answer format significantly affect co-failure rates.

title: Finding Stationary Points by Comparisons
comment: 41 pages, 4 figures. To appear in the Forty-Third International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026)
summary: We study the problem of finding stationary points of non-convex functions when access to the objective is provided only through a comparison oracle that, given two points, outputs which has the larger function value. For a twice differentiable $f\colon\mathbb R^n\to\mathbb R$ with Lipschitz gradient and Hessian, we develop an algorithm that visits an $ε$-stationary point using $\widetilde O(n^2/ε^{1.5})$ queries. Our approach uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian to accuracy $δ$ using $\widetilde O(n^2\log(1/δ))$ queries. We further study this problem with a quantum comparison oracle model where queries can be made in superpositions, and develop the first quantum algorithm that finds an $ε$-stationary point, which takes $\widetilde O(n/ε^{1.5})$ queries.

AI.tldr: The paper develops both classical and quantum algorithms for finding stationary points of non-convex functions using only comparison oracle access, achieving query complexities of Õ(n²/ε¹·⁵) classically and Õ(n/ε¹·⁵) quantumly.
AI.motivation: Finding stationary points of non-convex functions is fundamental in optimization, but typical gradient-based methods require direct function or gradient access. The motivation is to solve this problem under the more restrictive setting where only comparison queries are available (given two points, output which has larger function value), which models practical scenarios where function values are only available through pairwise comparisons rather than exact evaluations.
AI.method: The classical approach uses a subroutine that estimates the normalized Hessian matrix to accuracy δ using Õ(n² log(1/δ)) comparison queries. The overall algorithm leverages this Hessian estimation to find ε-stationary points. For the quantum version, the authors utilize a quantum comparison oracle model where queries can be made in superpositions, enabling more efficient parallel comparisons through quantum superposition principles.
AI.result: The main results are: (1) A classical algorithm that finds an ε-stationary point using Õ(n²/ε¹·⁵) comparison queries, improving upon previous approaches. (2) The first quantum algorithm for this problem, achieving Õ(n/ε¹·⁵) query complexity, showing a quadratic speedup in dimension dependence compared to the classical algorithm (n vs n²).
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that efficient optimization is possible even with only comparison oracle access, and that quantum computing provides significant advantages for such optimization problems, achieving quadratic speedup in dimension dependence. The results bridge optimization theory with quantum algorithms and establish fundamental query complexity bounds for finding stationary points under limited oracle access.

title: Language-Based Digital Twins for Elderly Cognitive Assistance
comment: Accepted and published in the Proceedings of the ACM International Conference on PErvasive Technologies Related to Assistive Environments (PETRA 2026). The final published version is available through the ACM Digital Library
summary: Digital twins have emerged as a promising paradigm for personalized healthcare, enabling modeling of individual behavior and health trajectories. In cognitive health, early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging, where language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers. In this work, we propose a language-based digital twin framework that leverages large language models (LLMs) to mimic the conversational behavior of elderly individuals by incorporating stylometric cues and contextual metadata. To evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency, we introduce a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) that jointly measures reconstruction quality and predicts cognitive scores. Experiments on the I-CONECT dataset show that the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics and achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, while outperforming baseline GPT-generated responses. These results highlight the potential of language-based digital twins as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring.

AI.tldr: A language-based digital twin framework using LLMs to mimic elderly conversational behavior, incorporating stylometric cues for MCI detection, evaluated with multi-head cVAE on I-CONECT dataset, showing promising results for cognitive health monitoring.
AI.motivation: Early detection of Mild Cognitive Impairment (MCI) remains challenging in cognitive health. Language and conversational patterns serve as non-invasive biomarkers that could be leveraged for personalized healthcare through digital twins.
AI.method: Proposes a language-based digital twin framework using large language models (LLMs) to mimic elderly conversational behavior with stylometric cues and contextual metadata. Introduces a multi-head conditional variational autoencoder (cVAE) to evaluate fidelity and cognitive consistency by jointly measuring reconstruction quality and predicting cognitive scores.
AI.result: Experiments on I-CONECT dataset show the digital twin preserves identity-specific characteristics, achieves reconstruction and MoCA prediction errors comparable to real data, and outperforms baseline GPT-generated responses.
AI.conclusion: Language-based digital twins show potential as a scalable and non-invasive approach for personalized and continuous cognitive health monitoring, particularly for early MCI detection.

title: Data-Free Reservoir Features for Efficient Long-Horizon Cold-Start Continual Learning
summary: Cold-start exemplar-free class-incremental learning requires learning a growing set of classes without replay, external pretraining, or a large initial task. Existing cold-start methods typically either train the backbone throughout the stream and compensate for semantic drift, or freeze a backbone after the first task, producing features biased toward the initial classes. These choices also create a computational tension: drift-compensation methods require repeated backbone training and increasingly expensive updates as the task horizon grows, while frozen-backbone methods are cheap but weak under cold start. We study a third option: a feature extractor that is never fit to image data at all. We propose CIRCLE, a class-incremental classifier built from fixed bidirectional two-dimensional reservoir features, adapted from BiRC2D for image classification, and streaming linear discriminant analysis heads. CIRCLE groups multiple random reservoir instantiations into feature ensembles and averages the softmax outputs of independent SLDA heads, yielding a tunable bias-variance tradeoff between richer random features and prediction-level ensembling. Because the feature extractor is fixed and the head admits streaming closed-form updates, CIRCLE performs sample-wise training without replay, task-boundary information, or backbone backpropagation. On CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, ImageNet-Subset, and ImageNet-1k, CIRCLE is competitive at 10-20 task splits and substantially outperforms strong CS-EFCIL baselines at 50, 100, and 500 task splits, while training much faster than trained-backbone drift-compensation methods. Ablations show that the BiRC2D-style extractor, SLDA head, and balanced feature/prediction ensembling each contribute to the final performance.

AI.tldr: CIRCLE is a novel cold-start class-incremental learning method that uses fixed bidirectional 2D reservoir features and streaming linear discriminant analysis, eliminating the need for backbone training, replay, or task boundaries while achieving competitive performance especially at high task splits.
AI.motivation: Existing cold-start class-incremental learning methods face fundamental trade-offs: either train backbones throughout (requiring expensive drift compensation and repeated training) or freeze backbones after first task (creating initial-class bias). Both approaches create computational tensions and limitations in the cold-start setting.
AI.method: CIRCLE uses a fixed bidirectional two-dimensional reservoir feature extractor (BiRC2D adapted for images) that never trains on image data. It combines this with streaming linear discriminant analysis heads. The method creates feature ensembles from multiple random reservoir instantiations and averages softmax outputs from independent SLDA heads, enabling tunable bias-variance tradeoff.
AI.result: CIRCLE performs competitively at 10-20 task splits and substantially outperforms strong baselines at 50, 100, and 500 task splits on CIFAR-100, TinyImageNet, ImageNet-Subset, and ImageNet-1k datasets, while training much faster than trained-backbone methods. Ablations confirm contributions from all components.
AI.conclusion: CIRCLE demonstrates that effective class-incremental learning is possible without backbone training, replay buffers, or task boundaries by leveraging fixed random features and streaming closed-form updates, offering an efficient and scalable alternative to existing cold-start approaches.

title: Transformer-Based Classification of Bacterial Raman Spectra with LOOCV
summary: Transformer-based models have recently attracted increasing attention for Raman spectral classification. In this study, a transformer-based approach was systematically evaluated using a nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation framework and compared with conventional machine-learning pipelines combining PCA or ICA with LDA, SVM, and Random Forest classifiers. A bacterial Raman dataset comprising 5,417 single-cell spectra from six bacterial species and nine independent measurement replicates was used. The transformer consistently achieved the highest classification performance across independent test replicates and significantly outperformed all conventional approaches. Analysis of the learned latent feature space revealed improved class separation compared with PCA- and ICA-based representations. Furthermore, the transformer maintained superior performance when applied directly to raw Raman spectra without preprocessing, demonstrating robust behavior across measurement replicates. These findings highlight the potential of transformer-based models for robust Raman spectral classification and emphasize the importance of replicate-aware validation for realistic model evaluation.

AI.tldr: Transformer models outperform traditional machine learning methods for bacterial Raman spectral classification, showing superior robustness and performance without preprocessing requirements.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for robust classification of Raman spectra from bacterial samples, recognizing that while transformer-based models have shown promise for Raman spectral analysis, there hasn't been systematic evaluation comparing them with conventional machine learning pipelines using replicate-aware validation frameworks.
AI.method: The study uses a nested leave-one-replicate-out cross-validation framework to evaluate a transformer-based approach against conventional machine learning pipelines (PCA/ICA with LDA, SVM, and Random Forest). The dataset consists of 5,417 single-cell Raman spectra from six bacterial species across nine independent measurement replicates.
AI.result: The transformer consistently achieved the highest classification performance across independent test replicates, significantly outperforming all conventional approaches. It maintained superior performance even when applied directly to raw Raman spectra without preprocessing, and analysis of learned latent features showed improved class separation compared to PCA/ICA-based representations.
AI.conclusion: Transformer-based models offer superior performance for Raman spectral classification, demonstrating robustness across measurement replicates and tolerance to preprocessing requirements. The findings emphasize the importance of replicate-aware validation for realistic model evaluation in spectroscopic applications.

title: Heavy-Ball Q-Learning with Residual Weighting Correction
summary: This paper proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method for reinforcement learning (RL) and establishes its convergence. It also identifies conditions under which the method is theoretically guaranteed to converge faster than standard Q-learning. The same construction is then extended to Q-learning with linear function approximation, where analogous convergence and acceleration statements are derived. The analysis is based on a switched linear system (SLS) representation of Q-learning algorithms and on the joint spectral radius (JSR) of the associated switching families. This SLS viewpoint is not commonly used in standard analyses of Q-learning, and it provides a complementary framework and new insight into how heavy-ball momentum can accelerate Q-learning.

AI.tldr: A corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method is proposed with guaranteed convergence and conditions for accelerated convergence compared to standard Q-learning, extended to linear function approximation, analyzed via switched linear systems and joint spectral radius theory.
AI.motivation: To develop a heavy-ball Q-learning method that not only converges but also provides theoretical guarantees for faster convergence than standard Q-learning, while extending the approach to linear function approximation settings.
AI.method: Proposes a corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method, then extends it to linear function approximation. Analysis uses a switched linear system (SLS) representation of Q-learning algorithms and the joint spectral radius (JSR) of associated switching families.
AI.result: Establishes convergence of the corrected heavy-ball Q-learning method, identifies conditions for faster convergence than standard Q-learning, and derives analogous convergence and acceleration results for Q-learning with linear function approximation.
AI.conclusion: The SLS viewpoint provides a complementary framework and new insights into how heavy-ball momentum can accelerate Q-learning, offering both theoretical guarantees and practical acceleration benefits.

title: Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network with Inverse Propensity Score under Unobserved Confounding
summary: Uplift modeling, crucial for estimating individual treatment effects (ITE), faces dual challenges: flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to enhance discriminative power and debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios. In this paper, we propose the Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network (CHAUN) and Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score (RA-IPS) method to address these limitations. CHAUN employs shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention mechanisms to dynamically integrate treatment-specific and control-specific representations, enhancing inter-group correlation modeling. Theoretically, we prove that access to the true propensity scores ensures ITE identifiability even with unobserved confounders. For practical scenarios lacking true propensity scores, RA-IPS adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to mitigate bias from unobserved variables. Experiments on public datasets (CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA) and a production e-commerce dataset demonstrate CHAUN's superiority over state-of-the-art uplift models, achieving relative improvements of up to 25.6% in QINI scores. RA-IPS further enhances robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding. The results validate the effectiveness of our proposed methods in real-world causal inference tasks.

AI.tldr: CHAUN combines cross-head attention networks with robust adversarial propensity score weighting to improve uplift modeling under unobserved confounding, achieving up to 25.6% QINI score improvements.
AI.motivation: Uplift modeling faces two main challenges: 1) flexibly leveraging inter-group similarity to enhance discriminative power, and 2) debiasing under unobserved confounding scenarios where true propensity scores are unknown.
AI.method: Proposes CHAUN (Cross-Head Attention Uplift Network) with shared feature embeddings and cross-head attention to dynamically integrate treatment-specific and control-specific representations. Also introduces RA-IPS (Robust Adversarial Inverse Propensity Score) that adversarially optimizes propensity weights within constrained uncertainty sets to handle unobserved confounders.
AI.result: Experiments on CRITEO-UPLIFT, LAZADA, and production e-commerce datasets show CHAUN outperforms state-of-the-art uplift models with up to 25.6% relative improvement in QINI scores. RA-IPS further enhances robustness, outperforming standard IPS by 5.4% under unobserved confounding.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CHAUN and RA-IPS methods effectively address both discriminative power and unobserved confounding challenges in uplift modeling, validating their effectiveness in real-world causal inference tasks.

title: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) for aerodynamic prediction: a comparison with MLPs and GNNs
summary: Kolmogorov Arnold networks (KAN) have recently been introduced as a (deep) neural network architecture whose trainable parameters adapt the activation functions, instead of the coefficients of the affine transformations at the core of traditional architectures such as deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs). This architecture builds on the Kolmogorov-Arnold theorem, which endows it with universal approximation properties. While the advent of KANs has been received with excitement, there is a current debate about the possible KAN supremacy over deep multilayer perceptrons (MLPs) for classic fields such as symbolic regression, generic-purpose machine learning, natural language processing or computer vision. Here we assess the performance of KANs --and its nuanced comparison against MLPs and graph neural networks (GNNs)-- in the realm of fluid dynamics surrogate modelling. To that aim, we consider the task of predicting the surface pressure distribution over subsonic and transonic airfoils, a canonical task in aerodynamics. Our results show that KAN models show good performance in predicting the whole pressure coefficients and is able to interpolate across Mach numbers and angles of attack, however its performance is comparable --marginally inferior-- to a suitably trained MLP, where best performance is achieved by a GNN at the expense or requiring lengthier training. While the optimal KAN model have typically much lower complexity than MLP and GNN --hence resulting in faster training--, we find that KANs suffer from training instabilities, and their performance is highly dependent on a proper hyperparameter optimisation.

AI.tldr: Kolmogorov Arnold Networks (KANs) show good but not superior performance compared to MLPs and GNNs for fluid dynamics surrogate modeling of airfoil pressure distributions, with comparable accuracy to MLPs but inferior to GNNs, though KANs have lower complexity and faster training.
AI.motivation: To assess the performance of recently introduced Kolmogorov Arnold Networks (KANs) in comparison to traditional MLPs and GNNs for fluid dynamics surrogate modeling, specifically for predicting surface pressure distribution over airfoils - addressing the current debate about KAN supremacy over MLPs.
AI.method: Evaluated KANs against MLPs and GNNs on the canonical aerodynamics task of predicting pressure coefficients on subsonic and transonic airfoils, testing interpolation across Mach numbers and angles of attack, with proper hyperparameter optimization.
AI.result: KANs show good performance in predicting pressure coefficients and can interpolate across conditions, but performance is marginally inferior to suitably trained MLPs, while GNNs achieve best performance at the cost of longer training; KANs have much lower complexity and faster training but suffer from training instabilities and high sensitivity to hyperparameter optimization.
AI.conclusion: KANs demonstrate competitive but not superior performance to MLPs for fluid dynamics surrogate modeling, with GNNs being the best performers when computational cost is not a concern; KANs offer advantages in model complexity and training speed but require careful hyperparameter tuning and stability management.

title: fTNN: a tensor neural network for fractional PDEs
summary: We develop the fTNN, a deterministic tensor neural network subspace method for problems involving the fractional Laplacian on bounded domains, taking the fractional Poisson equation and time-dependent fractional advection-diffusion equation as typical representatives. The work employs a geometry-adapted integration split featuring a spatially dependent near-field radius, which decomposes the fractional Laplacian into three contributions: a singular near field, a regular interior far field, and an analytical exterior far field. Then the singular radial integrals are treated by Gauss-Jacobi quadrature, the regular radial integrals by Gauss quadrature, and the angular variables by deterministic angular quadrature, yielding a fully deterministic integration framework of the fractional Laplacian operator. To accurately resolve low-regularity solutions and the associated loss functional, we construct boundary-singularity-aware trial functions enriched with explicit boundary features, and propose two strategies for automatically selecting the leading exponent and evaluating the loss function from the singularity structure induced by the fractional operator, or jointly by the fractional operator and the source term. For time-dependent fractional PDEs, we design a spatiotemporally separable neural network that factorizes the time-space residual into a sum of low-dimensional temporal and spatial integrals, and we integrate this representation with an alternating neural network subspace optimization strategy for efficient training. Numerical experiments show that the proposed framework attains high accuracy on the tested benchmarks and improves substantially over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.

AI.tldr: A deterministic tensor neural network method (fTNN) for solving fractional Laplacian problems with enhanced accuracy for boundary singularities and long-time simulations.
AI.motivation: Existing methods like fPINN and Monte Carlo approaches have limitations in handling problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations for fractional PDEs on bounded domains.
AI.method: Uses a geometry-adapted integration split with spatially dependent near-field radius to decompose fractional Laplacian into three contributions, employs Gauss-Jacobi quadrature for singular integrals, Gauss quadrature for regular integrals, constructs boundary-singularity-aware trial functions, and designs spatiotemporally separable neural networks with alternating optimization.
AI.result: The fTNN framework achieves high accuracy on tested benchmarks and substantially improves over existing fPINN and Monte Carlo baselines, particularly for problems with strong boundary singularities and long-time simulations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deterministic tensor neural network subspace method provides an effective framework for solving fractional Laplacian problems with superior performance in handling boundary singularities and long-time dynamics compared to existing approaches.

title: Stochastic Gradient Optimization with Model-Assisted Sampling
summary: This work addresses the problem of variance in stochastic gradient estimation for machine learning optimization. Deep learning relies on mini-batch methods such as stochastic gradient descent, which approximate full gradients but introduce noise, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing methods, including variance reduction techniques (e.g., SVRG and SAG) and adaptive optimizers, aim to mitigate gradient noise but may introduce additional computational overhead. We propose a model-assisted sampling framework that interprets mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Our aim is to bridge machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. By incorporating auxiliary gradient-prediction models, we construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling arising as a special case when no auxiliary information is used. Our approach integrates easily with existing optimizers, improving efficiency without altering their dynamics. Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of the experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces in our benchmarks. Notably, with momentum-based optimizers such as AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves clearly better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baseline estimator.

AI.tldr: A model-assisted sampling framework that applies survey sampling theory to reduce variance in stochastic gradient estimation, improving optimization efficiency and generalization without changing optimizer dynamics.
AI.motivation: Mini-batch gradient methods in deep learning introduce noise due to stochastic gradient estimation, creating trade-offs between convergence stability, speed, and generalization. Existing variance reduction techniques like SVRG and SAG, as well as adaptive optimizers, have limitations including computational overhead.
AI.method: Proposes a model-assisted sampling framework interpreting mini-batch gradients through survey sampling theory, treating the dataset as a fixed finite population and gradients as sample-based estimates. Incorporates auxiliary gradient-prediction models to construct more efficient gradient estimators, with uniform sampling as a special case when no auxiliary information is used.
AI.result: Empirical results on synthetic and six benchmark datasets show performance gains in 71-86% of experiments, particularly for medium-sized input spaces. With momentum-based optimizers like AdamW, the proposed estimator achieves better generalization in roughly half the training epochs compared to baselines. The approach integrates easily with existing optimizers without altering their dynamics.
AI.conclusion: The paper successfully bridges machine learning optimization and survey sampling theory by combining their perspectives on sample-based estimation and variance reduction. The model-assisted sampling framework provides an effective approach to reduce gradient noise while maintaining computational efficiency and compatibility with existing optimization methods.

title: RecallRisk-BERT: A Multi-Task Framework for Post-Report Medical Device Recall Triage
summary: Medical device recalls are a critical regulatory mechanism for protecting patient safety. The growing volume of FDA recall records presents challenges in post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies mostly address recall occurrence prediction or root-cause analysis separately, while joint modeling of recall severity and root-cause categories has received limited attention. We develop an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA, covering the period from 2002 to October 2025. We first evaluate classical machine learning and boosting-based models for recall severity and root-cause category prediction. We then develop RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model that combines PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured categorical features, including product code, regulation number, and medical specialty. The model simultaneously predicts recall severity (Class I/II/III) and a consolidated root-cause category (9 classes). Performance was evaluated using accuracy, macro-averaged precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC. In single-task severity prediction, our LightGBM-based text--tabular configuration achieved the strongest performance, with an accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. In the multi-task setting, RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed the single-task PubMedBERT baseline. Model-derived risk rankings were strongly consistent with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6). These findings indicate that text--tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis.

AI.tldr: Automated FDA medical device recall triage framework using 54,165 recall records with a multi-task BERT model that simultaneously predicts recall severity and root-cause categories, achieving high accuracy and demonstrating strong consistency with observed risk patterns.
AI.motivation: The growing volume of FDA medical device recall records creates challenges for post-report recall triage, severity assessment, and root-cause interpretation. Existing studies typically address recall prediction or root-cause analysis separately, but joint modeling of both aspects has received limited attention despite its importance for efficient regulatory decision-making.
AI.method: Developed an automated recall triage framework using 54,165 FDA medical device recall records from openFDA (2002-2025). First evaluated classical ML and boosting models (LightGBM) for single-task prediction of recall severity (Class I/II/III) and root-cause categories. Then developed RecallRisk-BERT, a multi-task model combining PubMedBERT-based textual representations of recall narratives with embedding-based representations of structured features (product code, regulation number, medical specialty) to simultaneously predict severity and 9 consolidated root-cause categories.
AI.result: In single-task severity prediction, LightGBM-based text-tabular configuration achieved accuracy of 0.963, macro-F1 of 0.856, and ROC-AUC of 0.974. RecallRisk-BERT substantially outperformed single-task PubMedBERT baseline in multi-task setting. Model-derived risk rankings showed strong consistency with observed root-cause severity patterns (rho = 0.983, p = 1.936e-6).
AI.conclusion: Text-tabular learning can support scalable post-report recall triage, regulatory decision support, and model-based root-cause risk analysis for FDA medical device recalls. The multi-task approach demonstrates practical utility for joint severity and root-cause prediction.

title: Automating Potential-based Reward Shaping with Vision Language Model Guidance
summary: Sparse rewards are inherently challenging for reinforcement learning agents as they lack intermediate feedback to guide exploration and to correctly attribute the sparse success rewards to relevant parts of the trajectory. Naive reward shaping can induce reward hacking, yielding policies that exploit auxiliary signals instead of solving the intended task. Potential-based reward shaping (PBRS) guarantees preservation of the optimal policy set, but requires the definition of a heuristic potential function over the state space. In this work, we introduce the VLM-guided PBRS framework VLM-PBRS that learns the potential function directly from vision language model (VLM) feedback. We query a lightweight VLM to obtain preferences over image pairs and train a model of the potential function using these preferences. As this approach is based on potential-based reward shaping, it preserves the original optimal policies, and removes the need for expert-designed reward shaping terms. Because large VLMs are prohibitively expensive to invoke repeatedly during policy learning, we employ smaller, more computationally efficient VLMs. Although the resulting preference labels are less accurate, empirical evidence shows that the preference labels can still be used to accelerate learning. We validate our method empirically in the Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments and highlight the connection between VLM preference label accuracy and sample efficiency improvements. Our contributions are threefold: (1) the first application of VLM preference-based learning to synthesize a potential function for PBRS, (2) a principled, low-cost solution that leverages small VLMs, and (3) extensive empirical demonstration of improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking.

AI.tldr: VLM-PBRS uses vision-language model preferences to learn potential functions for reward shaping, improving RL sample efficiency without reward hacking while using efficient small VLMs instead of expensive large ones.
AI.motivation: Sparse rewards make RL challenging due to lack of intermediate feedback. While potential-based reward shaping (PBRS) preserves optimal policies, it requires manually designed potential functions. The paper aims to automate this using VLM feedback while avoiding the computational cost of large VLMs.
AI.method: Proposes VLM-PBRS framework that queries lightweight VLMs for preferences over image pairs, then trains a potential function model using these preferences. Uses computationally efficient small VLMs despite less accurate preference labels, and applies the learned potential function for PBRS to accelerate RL learning.
AI.result: Validated in Meta-World and Franka Kitchen environments. Shows connection between VLM preference label accuracy and sample efficiency improvements. Achieves improved sample efficiency and robustness to reward hacking compared to naive approaches.
AI.conclusion: VLM-PBRS successfully leverages small VLMs to learn potential functions for PBRS, providing a principled, low-cost solution that accelerates RL learning without inducing reward hacking. The approach balances computational efficiency with sufficient preference accuracy for practical improvements.

title: Explaining Temporal Graph Neural Networks via Feature-induced Information Flow
summary: Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) have demonstrated strong performance across a wide range of applications, including social network analysis, epidemic tracing, recommender systems, and political event forecasting. However, their increasing complexity poses significant challenges for explainability. Existing explanation methods focus only on a subset of the information flow within ETGNNs, typically tracing contributions from the event-related embeddings to the output. Consequently, they overlook the important pathways through event-induced variables, which mediate interactions between nodes and thereby play a central role in capturing long-range temporal dependencies. To overcome this limitation, we propose a novel attribution method that analyzes the \emph{entire} information flow through all event-associated variables. Our method is built upon the recent Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, which enables explicit quantification of information flow originating from event embeddings as well as information flow passing through event-induced variables. It also ensures comparability of latent variables across layers, and supports higher-order analysis of interactions between events. To handle the architectural complexity of ETGNNs, we extend the NRM framework with a modular decomposition procedure that facilitates the systematic construction of relevance structure for complex neural architectures. We evaluate our approach on two synthetic datasets for epidemic tracing and social dynamics, as well as a real-world dataset of political event networks. Our qualitative and quantitative experiments show that our method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.

AI.tldr: ETGNNs lack comprehensive explainability methods that trace all event-associated information flows. This paper proposes a novel attribution method using Normalized Relevance Measure with modular decomposition to analyze complete information flow through both event embeddings and event-induced variables.
AI.motivation: While Event-based Temporal Graph Neural Networks (ETGNNs) perform well across applications like social networks, epidemic tracing, and political forecasting, their complexity challenges explainability. Existing methods only trace event-related embeddings to outputs, overlooking crucial pathways through event-induced variables that mediate node interactions and capture long-range temporal dependencies.
AI.method: The paper proposes a novel attribution method that analyzes the entire information flow through all event-associated variables. Built on the Normalized Relevance Measure (NRM) framework, it quantifies both information flow from event embeddings and through event-induced variables. To handle ETGNN complexity, the NRM framework is extended with modular decomposition that facilitates systematic construction of relevance structures for complex neural architectures.
AI.result: The method was evaluated on two synthetic datasets (epidemic tracing and social dynamics) and a real-world political event network dataset. Both qualitative and quantitative experiments show the method consistently outperforms existing explanation approaches while producing more human-interpretable explanations.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the limitation of existing ETGNN explainability approaches by capturing the complete information flow through all event-associated variables, including event-induced variables that mediate temporal dependencies. This leads to more accurate and interpretable explanations.

title: Graph Neural Networks Applications Across Domains: All Insights You Need
summary: Graph neural networks have moved from a niche representation-learning technique to the default model class wherever data carry relational structure. The interesting question is no longer whether message passing helps on a given dataset, but where graph structure earns its computational cost and where it does not. This survey organises the field around a single design space, derives the spectral and spatial formulations from shared first principles, and connects expressive power to the Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy with explicit statements of what current architectures can and cannot separate. Against that methodological backbone we examine twelve application domains, among them recommendation and social networks, knowledge graphs and language-model integration, drug discovery and molecular property learning, healthcare and neuroscience, computer vision, traffic and urban computing, power and renewable-energy systems, wireless and sixth-generation networks, fraud and cybersecurity, industrial prognostics, materials science, and climate modelling. For each domain we specify the graph-construction choices and their costs, identify which architecture families dominate and why, and separate reported gains from artefacts of weak baselines or favourable splits. A cross-domain comparison exposes recurring patterns: heterophily and scale undercut the same models almost everywhere, temporal graphs remain harder than their static counterparts, and the architectures that top public leaderboards are seldom the ones that reach deployment. We treat over-smoothing, over-squashing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability not as a closing checklist but as the constraints that decide adoption.

AI.tldr: A comprehensive survey of graph neural networks that organizes the field around a single design space, connects expressive power to theoretical foundations, and examines twelve diverse application domains with cross-domain insights.
AI.motivation: Graph neural networks have become the default model class for relational data, but there's a need to understand when graph structure justifies its computational cost and which architectures work best across different domains. The paper aims to organize the field systematically and separate real gains from methodological artefacts.
AI.method: Organizes GNN field around a unified design space, derives spectral and spatial formulations from first principles, connects expressive power to Weisfeiler-Leman hierarchy, and examines twelve application domains with detailed analysis of graph construction, dominant architectures, and methodological rigor.
AI.result: Identifies recurring patterns across domains: heterophily and scale consistently challenge certain models, temporal graphs remain difficult, and public leaderboard models rarely reach deployment. Provides systematic analysis of each domain's specific graph-construction choices, cost considerations, and why particular architectures dominate.
AI.conclusion: The survey provides a unified framework for understanding GNNs, emphasizing that practical adoption is driven by constraints like oversmoothing, robustness, distribution shift, fairness, and explainability rather than just theoretical expressive power, with deployment considerations often differing from academic leaderboard performance.

title: The Geometry of Updates: Fisher Alignment at Vocabulary Scale
comment: Accepted at the 43rd International Conference on Machine Learning (ICML 2026), PMLR 306. 64 pages total (main paper plus appendix), 4 figures, 29 tables
summary: Training-free source selection for LLM families with shared vocabularies arises in scientific string domains such as SMILES, protein, and genomic sequences, where candidate corpora share a tokenizer but differ in prediction targets. This creates an activation-dark regime: representation-similarity metrics can be uninformative without assumptions about label-conditioned error geometry, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. We show that, in a shared-output head setting, representation metrics (e.g., CKA) are non-identifiable for transfer; models can share identical representations yet have orthogonal head updates. The key identity is that head Fisher alignment is exactly a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, exposing activation, error, and coupling factors rather than requiring a materialized Fisher matrix. FisherSketch estimates this cosine directly in a single streaming pass, making K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical with a 16 KB task signature (m=4096) and a 192 KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store next to a model hash, but encoding transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the same signatures and marginals provide a diagnostic instrument for studying whether LLM task similarity is driven by activations, errors, or their coupling; shared-parameter and internal-layer validations, together with Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift experiments, show that FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.

AI.tldr: FisherSketch: A lightweight method for training-free source selection in LLM families with shared vocabularies by estimating head Fisher alignment through kernel mean embeddings without materializing large Fisher matrices.
AI.motivation: In scientific domains like SMILES, proteins, and genomic sequences, LLM families share tokenizers but have different prediction targets. Current methods face problems: representation-similarity metrics (like CKA) become uninformative without label-conditioned error assumptions, while classical update-geometry metrics are computationally prohibitive at vocabulary scale. There's an "activation-dark regime" where models can share identical representations but have orthogonal head updates, making representation metrics non-identifiable for transfer.
AI.method: FisherSketch estimates head Fisher alignment as a cosine between kernel mean embeddings in the joint activation-error space, capturing activation, error, and coupling factors. The key insight is that head Fisher alignment equals this cosine without needing a full Fisher matrix. The method works in a single streaming pass with compact signatures: 16KB task signature (m=4096) and 192KB per-task streaming state, small enough to store with model hashes.
AI.result: The method makes K=128,256 head Fisher alignment practical and provides transfer-relevant update structure. Beyond source selection, the signatures serve as diagnostic tools for analyzing whether LLM task similarity stems from activations, errors, or their coupling. Validation experiments with shared-parameter models, internal layers, and Llama-3.1-8B verbalizer-shift show FisherSketch remains informative when activation similarity cannot distinguish tasks.
AI.conclusion: FisherSketch addresses the activation-dark regime problem by providing efficient, informative metrics for transfer learning in LLM families with shared vocabularies. It enables practical source selection while also serving as a diagnostic instrument for understanding task similarity drivers in large language models.

title: Effective Covariance Dynamics in Solvable High-Dimensional GANs
